- Testing Seakeeper: Gyroscopic Stabilization for Boats: Kind: captions Language: en You boers already know it. There's nothing worse than rocking and rolling in a beam sea. Not only is it uncomfortable, heck, it'll make all your guests seasick. Seaeper says they have the solution with a gyroscopic stabilization unit called the 3DC. This unit's small. It can be put in new boats. It can be put in pre-existing boats. It can be retrofitted. And it can be put in boats all the way down to just 30 ft in length. But here at boats.com, we're not taking anybody's word for it. That's why we flew down to Florida to jump on this 35 ft contender. It's rigged up with the Seaeper 3DC. We're going to test it for ourselves. We got some hard data. Okay, folks. We're out here in the Atlantic Ocean to take a look at how this Seaeper works. It's quite interesting. It spins a flywheel inside at high RPM in a vacuum. This creates torque, and as the boat rolls, the torque counteracts the rolling motion. Now, you can hear the flywheel spinning away in there. And you may be wondering just how loud this unit is. But check this out. If I completely expose the unit, you can tell that it's really no louder than the four-stroke outboards idling on the transom of this boat. Now, let's translate the roll reduction into numbers. I've got this handy dandy inclinometer app on my phone here, and we can see that with the unit running, the boat is barely rocking at all. You see 0 to 1°. Now, let's turn it off. You can see a steady increase. Whoa. And it gets all the way up to 10°. And that folks is in really calm seas. Now, let's turn the phone into a calculator. Seaeper claims to get a 70 to 90% roll reduction with these units. And in this case, well, wait. I don't even need a calculator. 1°ree 10°. That's a 90% roll reduction. Go right into another boat's wake. Right on the beam. Let's see what happens with the seaeper. Not much. Heck, maybe I'll just stop and see if it'll roll the boat right here. Nope. Well, that was really an interesting experiment, wasn't it, folks? At seaeper clearly does have a significant effect. If you'd like to learn more about it, be sure to visit boats.com where we have all kinds of articles that tell you all about boats.

- What is Seakeeper Ride? | What It Does, Advantages and Basic Operation: Kind: captions Language: en Welcome to the introductory video of the&nbsp; Seakeeper ride system. For those of you&nbsp;&nbsp; not familiar with this new offering from&nbsp; sea keeper, in this video we're going to be&nbsp;&nbsp; talking about what it does, the advantages, &nbsp; as well as basic operating procedures.&nbsp;&nbsp; When you're thinking about the new Seakeeper&nbsp; ride product, other products on the market&nbsp;&nbsp; may come to mind. First off, the ride&nbsp; product line is not a gyroscopic&nbsp;&nbsp; system like you may have seen in one of our&nbsp; larger models with the optional Seakeepers. It is designed to be installed on the same spot&nbsp; as traditional trim tabs. However, the features of&nbsp;&nbsp; this product far outperform trim tabs and other&nbsp; similar self-leveling systems on the market.&nbsp;&nbsp; Other self-leveling systems out there simply&nbsp; don't have the speed or sensors required&nbsp;&nbsp; to stabilize. Compared to ride, those systems&nbsp; are slow and designed to just level the boat.&nbsp;&nbsp; Having zero capability when it comes&nbsp; to smoothing out the ride of the boat&nbsp;&nbsp; underway. Before we go any further&nbsp; let's take a look at some footage&nbsp;&nbsp; of the system in action. As you're watching&nbsp; this footage, take a look at the difference&nbsp;&nbsp; of the running angle of the boat when the&nbsp; system is on versus when the system is off.&nbsp;&nbsp; Starting with the ride system off, you'll&nbsp; notice there's a nice bit of chop on the water&nbsp;&nbsp; making the ride a little bouncy. Notice&nbsp; the bow compared to the horizon here&nbsp;&nbsp; and how much movement there is. Once the system&nbsp; is turned on you notice an immediate change in&nbsp;&nbsp; keeping the boat's true running attitude. Once the&nbsp; system is kicked off again, you can see the same&nbsp;&nbsp; effect yet again. The bow begins to bounce higher, which makes the ride much more uncomfortable. The&nbsp;&nbsp; boat remains at the same speed nothing changes&nbsp; other than we are turning the system on and off. Moving to a new date, you'll notice the conditions&nbsp; are much rougher there are bigger swells stacked&nbsp;&nbsp; together with more wind chop on top creating&nbsp; very rough conditions for smaller vessels.&nbsp;&nbsp; However, once we turn the system&nbsp; on the boat's attitude changes&nbsp;&nbsp; drastically. It's still rough, but there is a&nbsp; huge difference in how the boat is handling.&nbsp;&nbsp; Moving to an even rougher area of&nbsp; water, you notice with the system&nbsp;&nbsp; off that the boat is jumping out of&nbsp; the water. When the boat comes down,&nbsp;&nbsp; it's coming down on its port and starboard&nbsp; size making it difficult to control the boat. Once the ride system is turned&nbsp; on, the boat no longer jumps&nbsp;&nbsp; and stays level even in extremely rough conditions. Each one of our hulls is designed&nbsp; to run at a certain angle.&nbsp;&nbsp; Keeping this running attitude consistent,&nbsp; allows the hull design to perform&nbsp;&nbsp; at an optimal level and achieves the best&nbsp; ride performance and fuel consumption. The Seakeeper&nbsp;&nbsp; ride can go from zero to one hundred&nbsp; percent deployment in one tenth of a second.&nbsp;&nbsp; Meaning that it can make fine adjustments very&nbsp; quickly and this is what makes the ride smoother.&nbsp;&nbsp; For us, one of the most exciting features about&nbsp; this product is how extremely intuitive it is.&nbsp;&nbsp; The product is completely autonomous and just&nbsp; works without having to do anything at all.&nbsp;&nbsp; Everyone will be able to enjoy the&nbsp; smoother ride from the minute you&nbsp;&nbsp; launch it, to the second that you&nbsp; put the boat back on the trailer.&nbsp;&nbsp; Whether you're a weekend warrior going to&nbsp; the sandbar or hardcore offshore fishermen,&nbsp;&nbsp; this product is 100% effective in all of those&nbsp; conditions. For model year 2023, the ride system&nbsp;&nbsp; will be standard in our Open 232 and Heritage&nbsp; 231 as well as our Open 212 and Heritage 211. Let's break down the actual hardware of the&nbsp; system. There are two actuators mounted on&nbsp;&nbsp; the transom. One for the port side and one for&nbsp; the starboard side. The rotary design requires&nbsp;&nbsp; minimal amp draw making them very efficient. The&nbsp; scoops deploy a total of one inch to create the&nbsp;&nbsp; counter effect needed to stabilize the boat. On the&nbsp; dash, there are no visible control panels at all. &nbsp; For those new to boating, the feedback has&nbsp; always been that trim tabs are one of the&nbsp;&nbsp; more difficult skills to master while learning&nbsp; how to boat. Then, there's the ongoing adjustments&nbsp;&nbsp; that need to be made as weight shifts around&nbsp; the boat. For example, if a person goes from&nbsp;&nbsp; one side of the boat to the other, then this will&nbsp; necessitate a separate adjustment of the trim tabs. Seakeeper ride system will take care of&nbsp; this problem by auto leveling on the fly.&nbsp;&nbsp; With this technology, it doesn't matter how good&nbsp; an operator is. The system can still outperform&nbsp;&nbsp; just because of how quick it is. It can react&nbsp; and stabilize throughout the entire process&nbsp;&nbsp; so you're not only leveling, but you're&nbsp; also stabilizing for a much smoother ride. There are some conditions that may require&nbsp; to make some slight adjustments and for that&nbsp;&nbsp; you can use the built-in one hell map on your&nbsp; Garmin system. The app allows you to add a bias&nbsp;&nbsp; to adjust the running attitude or deliberately&nbsp; create a list to account for sea conditions.&nbsp;&nbsp; These adjustments are measured in terms&nbsp; of degrees. Let's say that you needed a&nbsp;&nbsp; little bit of an angle or list in&nbsp; the boat's current sea conditions.&nbsp;&nbsp; The operator can go into the one helm app and&nbsp; click on the port or starboard buttons to add&nbsp;&nbsp; that in in half degree increments. The Seakeeper&nbsp; ride system will continue to stabilize the boat&nbsp;&nbsp; but retain this bias to one side. You can always&nbsp; reset it back to zero when it's no longer needed. One main key advantage of this system is&nbsp; that it helps the boat get up on plane.&nbsp;&nbsp; Let's do a quick side by side comparison of time&nbsp; to plane with the engine at the same level of trim.&nbsp;&nbsp; For the first test, I will keep&nbsp; the Seakeeper ride system off. For this next test, we're going to turn on the Seakeeper&nbsp;&nbsp; system, but keep the&nbsp; engine trim exactly the same. This is a great feature of this product and it&nbsp; will help newcomers feel very confident behind&nbsp;&nbsp; the wheel. The way that if system does this&nbsp; is by reading the ride profile that we've&nbsp;&nbsp; installed into each of the boats. We've created a&nbsp; ride profile that fits many different conditions&nbsp;&nbsp; and it's a great all-around performance. But if&nbsp; you're an advanced user and you want to make&nbsp;&nbsp; certain adjustments to fit your boating style&nbsp; better, you can go into the advanced section&nbsp;&nbsp; on the screen and make the necessary adjustments. A&nbsp; good use case for a custom adjustment might be if&nbsp;&nbsp; you typically run the boat with people sitting at&nbsp; the bow. You can dial down some of the deployment&nbsp;&nbsp; at the lower speeds as the additional weight up&nbsp; front will assist the boat getting up on plane.&nbsp;&nbsp; As far as maintenance goes, there is&nbsp; virtually no maintenance with the system.&nbsp;&nbsp; The same as it would be with any traditional trim&nbsp; system. The Seakeeper ride system comes back with&nbsp;&nbsp; an exclusive three-year warranty period and joins&nbsp; the Sportlink Electronics Integration system.&nbsp;&nbsp; The Seakeeper ride system revolutionizes&nbsp; the way we think a boat should perform&nbsp;&nbsp; and handle. It can be enjoyed by everyone on&nbsp; board and the difference in rod quality simply is&nbsp;&nbsp; mind blowing. From Stuart Florida, my name&nbsp; is Victor and thank you for watching.

- MJM Yachts Seakeeper Gyro Stabilizer Review and Installation: Kind: captions Language: en MGM Yachts has become world-renowned for the luxury of effortless driving and there's many components that make that effect one of which is a gyro stabilizer So today we're going to take a look at the components and installation of a seeper 5 the seeper 5 is a compact system comprised of the display panel located at the helm and the gyro main unit the seat keeper is self-contained with no external hardware other than the display and a seawater pum pump located within the unit is the computer that controls the seae Keeper's operation shown here is the drive box located behind this panel is the IMU sensor or inertial measurement unit the brain that makes continuous calculations so seeper active control can adjust gyro torque to eliminate up to 95% of roll the computer controls hydraulic dampeners allowing the gyro to apply differing levels of torque relative to the boat speed and SE date the core component of the gyro the item that makes all the magic happen is a 400 lb steel flywheel inside this outer sphere the flywheel spins in a vacuum at 10,700 RPM to produce the inertia necessary to eliminate virtually all of a boat's roll although power usage is minimal when the gyro is in operation like anything electrical it produces some heat and needs to be cooled seae keeper has remedied this via a closed loop cooling system and heat exchanger similar to your engine or generator in addition to the patented cooling system seke keeper installation requires a through hole fitting so the water pump can circulate sea water through the heat exchanger to cool water in the closed loop system the temperature is monitored by sensors within the sphere enclosure seen here is one of the temperature gauges easy installation and maintenance as well as reliability are assured through the use of aircraft quality ditch connectors sealed against any moisture intrusion MJM yachs was the first brand in the world to make a cber gyro standard equipment doing so in the MJM 50z the unit fits perfectly onto structural reinforcing stringers low in the hull customized to deal with the high loads imparted by waves trying to rip the hull off the seae keeper which is acting like an immovable Rock it's secured in position with 16 high strength Bol s to mounts cast from an aluminum alloy the installation is straightforward and can be retrofitted into boats having their requisite structure testing is shown seeke keeper eliminates 92% of roll on the MJM 40z at rest and underway combine a seae keeper gyro stabilizer with the low vertical center of gravity Advanced epoxy composite construction and the iso category a ocean certification of the MJM 40z or 50z and you get a very special yacht the ultimate in cruising comfort

- Yacht Stabilizers: Gyro vs. Fins Pros & Cons: & Important Safety Information About Fin Stabilizers!!: Kind: captions Language: en engine room of this Absolute 75 Nevada on top of a Sea Keeper gyroscope that comes equipped on this boat. And want to talk briefly about some of the pros and cons of gyroscopic stabilization versus traditional fin stabilization. Um, we have another video that explains how gyroscopic stabilization works. So, gyroscopic stabilizers are internal. So, there's nothing outside the boat like fins sticking out. Um, and gyroscopic stabilization is really a passive system. It's using physics to allow the gyroscope to move forward and aft and apply perpendicular torque when the boat moves side to side. That works incredibly well. Now, a fin stabilizer system is different. Of course, we have fins outside the boat. um that means that a computer system is moving those fins to counteract the roll side to side of your boat. And that means too, as you can imagine, a fin system is more effective at speed because you have more uh surface area on those fins or more speed, more water resistance on those fins, allowing them to control the roll of the boat. Although modern fin stabilizer systems also still do work very well at rest because they have enough surface area and the computer technology to move those fins quickly ensures that your boat does not go side to side either at rest, slow speed or high speed. Now fins obviously we're adding drag. We have things that are coming down from the hull. We are adding potentially collision issues. Oh, obviously we could hit something with a fin. If we if there was debris or we went into shallow water, a fin could hit something. There are some safety issues with fins as well. When the boat is at rest and you have a tender right next to the boat or people are swimming, those fins are underwater working and they can sometimes cause problems uh and be dangerous. So, it's really best to disable your fin stabilizer systems when you're at anchor and you have people in the water or tenders pulling up alongside your boat. So, just be aware of that. Now, a gyroscopic stabilizer, of course, has nothing outside the boat. And it's passive because once that gyroscope is spinning at 10,000 RPM, and we engage it, we allow it to freely move. It freely moves for and aft in response to that side to side motion of your boat. Works incredibly well. It's very efficient. Um, you're not having any drag. You're having easier mechanical maintenance. Um, and it just works incredibly well. So uh the other thing too again about a gyroscope is remember it works very well and really equally well regardless of the speed of your vessel. So whether you are at rest moving slowly or moving quickly a gyroscope is having an equal ability to reduce the side to side roll of your boat. Fins improve their ability to reduce side to side roll the faster you're going. Um so that is the difference. A gyroscope always has that ability. So, especially at slow speeds, a gyroscope is going to have just as much capability to reduce side to side roll as at high speeds or even at rest. Um, so just some of the differences between gyroscopic stabilization and a traditional fin stabilizer system. I'll tell you, I assumed when I was learning about gyroscopes years ago that the gyroscope moved this way to counteract the boat going that way. But that's not the way the physics work. This boat, this gyroscope never goes side to side. It only goes for and aft. Gyroscopic precession and perpendicular torque to very effectively and dramatically reduce and virtually eliminate side to side roll in your boat. Um, again, it varies according to boat size of seaeper installed and so on, but it's a very effective solution that we spec in all of our boats, traditionally up to 75 ft at least. Um, gyroscopes just work very, very well for our customers. So, my name's Christopher with One Water Yacht Group. If I can assist you enjoying South Florida yaching or explain the ST stabilizer systems that we offer on our yachts, please give me a call. 561-2851212. Thank

But for charter, ultra luxury etc. gyro is really nice just due to how good it is at keeping boat stable when still.

  20. @rykehuss3435: Gyro does not work at speed. Maybe at displacement speeds it can still do something, but its effect vanishes at around 20 knots because the forces on the boat's roll increase with speed. So if you want roll stabilization at planing speeds, fins are your only option because fins get stronger at speed because of the increased lift generated due to more water flow over the surfaces.

- How this boat yard had a dangerous energy problem - until now: Kind: captions Language: en On an island off the coast of West Cork, there's a boatyard that's been welding, servicing and building vessels since 2004, and a power problem that was slowly eating the business alive. This is how one family on the edge of the Atlantic took control of their energy and futureproofed their business for the next generation. This film is available in several alternative audio languages and subtitles. Just click the cog to change the settings. Their island sits just south of Ireland. Getting here means a ferry. Getting anything done here means dealing with the realities of island life, and that includes power. We had three-phase on site because my father had a fish farm. We were doing abalone before, so that was very unique to have a three-phase supply on an island. It sounds like an advantage and it was up to a point, but as the boatyard grew, the supply simply couldn't keep up. When we started, we had 40 kilowatts ...was our income. And the crane alone with a power crane here, the crane was taking a hundred kilowatts, with a compressor, taking 25 kilowatts, Running a crane and multiple compressors on a supply that was already at its limit meant the voltage was constantly dropping. Sensitive equipment could not handle that, and on a working boat yard, the consequences were costly. We were at a hundred percent overload. For half the time when we were at the edge, we were blowing motors and it was costing us a fortune. And when the grid went down, everything stopped. Except here, stopping is not an option. There's a dry dock, seven metres below sea level, and it has to stay dry. We always had a backup so we didn't stop. We kept going because we couldn't stop because if you have a boat inside the dock and you were disturbing two boats or holding her, if the water comes up, she's gone. We can't afford it. For years, the answer was diesel. A 250 kVA generator, running as a backup, covering the heavy loads that the grid couldn't handle. It kept things moving, but the bill kept growing. When we started back in 2004/5 it was costing us about 80 euros to pump out the dock. Diesel was 25 cents a litre, so it's exponentially gone cuckoo. So we looked around to see how can we sort out or reduce running costs. We have to make our business future proof. Being on an island adds another layer. Everything costs more to bring over. And when energy prices spiked after the war in Ukraine, the pressure became unsustainable. When the war started, oil prices went up to eighteen and a half thousand Euros. That's unsustainable. Just in one bang from eight thousand to eighteen and a half. It's just ridiculous. We could not do it. There were no surviving in the business. We were down off a cliff. Gerard had looked at solar before. An early attempt with panels on the wrong side of a shed had cost him 40,000 euros in lost generation. That experience has taught him something important. The technology is only as good as the people installing it. We started with the with the product first. We started with Victron Energy actually, because we were used to playing with Victron on the fishing boats. They had chargers and so on and so forth - so we knew that there and there was a backup attached, so that was very important for us. They attended a Victron training course on the mainland where they were introduced to what a modern hybrid Victron Energy system could actually do. That's also where they met Maciek Banski from Energy Freedom Systems. Their vision was very, very interesting. You know, they had done it in smaller companies with other ones. So that, that to me was very, very I was delighted with it. It was way more than what I expected Before a single panel went up. Maciek's process starts with data - not a rough estimate of what the site was using, but four months of real measurements. What I'm interested in the most is the peak power, peak load because this is the key number to size the system. For Bear Island Boatyard, that data reveals a particularly unusual load pattern. Most businesses quiet down overnight as people go home. This one, however, can't afford to. Most important thing for this place is to keep the dry dock dry as it's seven metres below sea level. That place cannot afford losing power because it would've got flooded and would destroy a lot of equipment. Overnight depending on the weather conditions... if there is a rain and wind blowing in more water into the dock, we could even consume 30 to 50 kilowatt hours overnight, keeping the dock dry. The solution is built around Victron Energy equipment and it is substantial on the main dock building. An east west solar PV setup feeds into a 40 kilowatt grid tie inverter. Behind the boat yard, there's a ground mounted array. It's the ground mount array of 280 solar PV panels bi-facial driven by three Fronius Eco 27 kilowatt inverters. Bi-facial panels are worth explaining: They generate power from both sides. Picking up reflective light from the ground below. On a ground mounted setup that extra efficiency genuinely adds up. All that solar generation feeds into a purpose-built container that is the heart of the power system. In the container, there is a Victron Energy system, one hundred and eighty kVA, built of Quattro-II 15 kVA - 12 units - and the battery bank that contains 18 units, 16 kilowatt hour each of storage lithium batteries, 288 kilowatt hours of lithium ion phosphate storage. And to handle the current moving through the system at peak loads, Maciek's team had to fabricate something custom. We are dealing here with up to 2000 Amps. So we had to build bespoke busbar space on thousand square millimetres, pure copper busbar so they can easily deal with our amps without any voltage loss at peak power. The existing 250 kVA diesel generator remains in the system, but its role has completely changed. It no longer runs to cover the grid's shortfalls. Now it's a safety net controlled entirely by the Victron Energy Cerbo GX. It's programmed to stand by kicking in, charging batteries like a cruise control in the car. It's charging up the batteries and shutting down. There is no idling, there is no unefficient fuel burning. What ties everything together is Victron's 'Dynamic ESS'. It's an algorithm that manages when the system draws from solar, when it uses stored battery power and when it buys from the grid. Always choosing the cheapest available source System decides when to purchase energy, when to sell energy, how much to buy; the system is self-learning. We can see the improvement over weeks and weeks. The goal is not to trade energy for profit, but to use as much of what the site generates as possible. At the beginning, I was a bit reluctant to use it on such a big operation as is Bear Island boatyard because they can't afford loss of energy or shortage of power. So I was just playing with the settings remotely. Day by day I saw progress and I saw Dynamic ESS running. Basically it plays by itself. Dynamic ESS basically tries to use cheapest available energy, which is either solar pv or the night-rate of the grid, especially in the winter. The results have been immediate and tangible. A boat valued at four and a half million euros was brought into the dock after being built in the Netherlands. The engineer on board commented that it was the most stable shore power the vessel had experienced since leaving the shipyard. We had the exact opposite before because we plugged boats in and the voltage was dipping. And the boat that we were dealing with are fishing boats and they're very high spec. You know they have radars and sonars and all sorts of stuff in them, and they cannot have voltage fluctuation because it blows them. It blows the machines and it has happened to us. So it was a huge cost when it goes wrong. The wider financial picture is also becoming clearer. The full system cost around 230,000 euros. Gerard expects the payback period to be five to six years and possibly sooner if energy prices continue to rise. And it was more important to get the right people that we could, so that if something went wrong they'd fix it. And that there were products we could add - and we'd have support in that. That for me was critical. And on remote access - which matters when your installer is not on the island with you every day - Victron's VRM (Victron Remote Management) is central to how Maciek manages the system. I can do 90% of my work remotely. Other manufacturers are way behind Victron that way. Gerard's ambitions do not stop at the fence of the boatyard. He sees what he has built here as the first step in something much larger for his own business and for the island as a whole. I intend to keep moving along because it's been a resounding success. One of our biggest problems coming in here is transport. Because we're living on an island, everything is maybe 30% more expensive. We have two vessels costing about 30,000 euros a year to run - which is an economic thing. We'll put more solar up, more batteries and we want to get that technology into the vessels. We have a perfect position here because the boats are coming to us. Okay? So if we have something that we can show them, the skippers will say, oh yeah, Jesus, that's working! Community of Bear Island is slightly different to the communities on the mainland and they would love to get completely independent. They are looking into getting renewable energy as their main source of energy rather than using the grid. It's not lost on Gerard that what has been built here is something rare. A working demonstration on a working island. That energy independence is achievable and worth it. We're the first generation that can produce electricity or so my thing is cut off the power, have control, invest in, it's just upfront investment. It's one of the best things I've ever done. Bear Island boatyard is a business built on the water shaped by the demands of the sea, and now powered by the sky. What started as a problem with a leaking dock and an overloaded grid has become something far more significant - proof that even on an island energy independence is not just possible, it's the smart play.

  1. @BigAssBoatOnATrailer [8 likes]: Be careful watching this video, this was a very specific installation for a specific need. 
It ONLY powers a cockpit fridge and an outlet for the Cockpit TV. They did not connect it to the boats AC for inverter OUTput. They re-used the Cockpit refrigerator breaker to supply power IN to the inverter battery charger. This is not a typical inverter marine installation. The earth ground should not be tied into the battery negative terminal. Yes the DC negative should also be tied to the earth ground but doing it here is improper and an  assumption. The inverter earth ground should go directly to the boat main bonding cables.

on the interstate or the motorway when you're at cruising speeds the loads are relatively light unless you're going uphill or there strong headwinds uh and so then we can get extended range out of if you've got a generator powering an electric motor in a boat is less efficient than if you took that generator engine and put it on the propeller in the first place and it's very expensive to do that because you've got to have the generator and the batteries and the electric motor and there's lots of single point failures in the system so generally speaking it doesn't pay but for short applications where you basically just want to get off the dock and put the sails up and go sailing and then you come back and and you can plug back in and recharge the batteries electric propul is terrific and then if occasionally you need more range you can put a generator in there but the recog izing that whenever you're using the generator you're probably less efficient than you would have   1. @intheshell35ify [9 likes]: Generally speaking, an old diesel engine can't kill you when you work on it.
  2. @udothiel8009 [8 likes]: As Nigel says "IT depends on ..."

I changed last year from a 15hp petrol outboard to a 6kW electric outboard (comparable thrust). Even without capability to charge my battery with shore power I never ran out of power after more than 2000nm sailed in coastel waters.
I have 30' trimaran and the hydrogeneration of the outboard while sailing delivered all the power for needed motoring and with 400Wp of solar power it not just gave a backup but also plenty of power for cooking when at ancor.
So for me it has been the right decision but of course it is not for all.
Fair winds and good season 2025!
  3. @CaptnButch [6 likes]: Nigel is right.  While it looks fun and cutting edge, adding complex electric propulsion systems to a boat does not make sense.  The problem is that batteries still don’t have the energy capacity required to reliably power a boat for prolonged use.  And the last thing a private boat owner needs is more complexity with a hybrid system for so little payback.  I respect Delos and the Wynns for trying something new, but for most this added complexity takes away from the whole point which is to practically live and explore on a reasonably priced and maintained boat.

Not just recreational but even commercial. IE there are commercial electric ferries and tug boats operating in some countries.   13. @tonyreynolds2328: With all due respect to Nigel, who is known by many people to be very knowledgeable, I have to say that his answer here in this short video is too simple to be accurate. If you want to drive your boat like a car under engine/motor power the whole time, then yes, electric power is probably not there yet as the amount of battery and electric generation is too great. However, batteries are getting better all the time (see Donut Labs announcement at CES this year) and we are one breakthrough away from batteries that are much better suited to the task. However, even without a new battery breakthrough, if you sail from a marina, and you really want to sail, then electric conversions are a great idea. I bought my sailboat to sail under wind power, and I absolutely hate the noise/vibration/smells of diesel engines. I currently have a catamaran and am designing my system with two electric motors, two banks of batteries, and two chargers/inverters. Either battery bank or charger/inverter can be used to power both motors or charge both battery banks if a failure occurs in one of them. And if you're generally just using your sailboat, as I currently am, sailing from a Marina only when the wind blows, then you'll hardly use any of your battery capacity, like, ever! Only if you're living on your boat, cruising out in the world, will battery capacity ever become an issue if you are, for example, under way in rough weather and you MUST have propulsion for many, many hours at a time. In that scenario, running out of battery could be life threatening. However, in almost every other scenario, its just an inconvenience and if you have redundant systems, like I'm designing, then that situation is mitigated quite a bit. I almost forgot to mention that I do have a powerful generator onboard and can fall back to that in an emergency if I deplete all battery capacity.

- The $790M Lawsuit Against a Widow & Bayesian Yacht Owner- Deep Analysis 😲: Kind: captions Language: en Imagine suing a grieving widow for nearly $1 billion because your unsinkable yacht sank in 16 minutes. That is exactly what is happening right now. The Italian Sea Group has filed a $790 million lawsuit against the family of Mike Lynch, claiming the Basian crew made unspeakable errors that ruined the manufacturer's reputation. They say the data proves the crew was negligent. But we have analyzed the tracking data they are using and it contains a fatal technical flaw. A missing data point that could prove their entire open door theory is a lie. While lawyers fight over millions in court, we are also tracking a completely different scandal in the Bahamas where a million-doll yacht has been left to rot on a reef abandoned by everyone. Today we are exposing the legal bullying, the data errors, and the ugly truth about accountability in the super yacht world. Chapter 1, the $790 million claim. $790 million. That is the staggering price tag the Italian Sea Group has placed on its own reputation. And they are coming to collect it from a widow and a crew that barely survived a nightmare. In a move that legal experts and maritime insiders are calling cynical, desperate, and potentially calculated. The manufacturer of the Basian superyacht has filed a massive lawsuit claiming that the vessel was unsinkable and that its loss was entirely due to crew negligence. They are alleging unspeakable errors, demanding compensation for a slump in their stock price, and effectively blaming the victims for ruining the brand's image. But when you strip away the corporate press releases and look at the raw data, the timeline, and the technical realities of the ship itself, their story begins to fall apart. The narrative that the crew left a door open is crumbling under scrutiny, and the left turn that supposedly doomed the ship might be nothing more than a data ghost. We are going deep into the filings, the flawed AIS data, and the update on that abandoned yacht in the Exumas that everyone seems to have forgotten. The Italian Sea Group, specifically their subsidiary, Pirini Na'vi, has launched a legal offensive that is almost unprecedented in the luxury yaching world. They are suing the company that owned the Basian, Revto Limited, which is controlled by Angela Bakarz, the widow of Mike Lynch. Lynch, his daughter, and several others perished in this tragedy. Yet, the lawsuit contends that the manufacturer has suffered ruinous loss of profit and reputational damage to the tune of nearly $1 billion. This aggressive stance isn't entirely new. Remember, Giovani Constantino, the CEO of the Italian Sea Group, was on television blaming the crew and the captain almost immediately after the sinking. While the search and rescue teams were still diving to recover bodies, the narrative was already being spun that this was human error, not a failure of engineering. Now that PR spin has turned into a formal legal filing. The lawsuit alleges that the crew was incompetent. They claimed the crew failed to close hatches, ignored weather warnings, and didn't lower the keel, which they argue would have saved the ship. They are pointing to a specific sequence of events, claiming the ship took on water because a shell door on the port side was left open, causing a catastrophic flood as the vessel healed over. It sounds plausible on paper until you look at the evidence that contradicts it. Chapter 2. The AI's data flaw. The cornerstone of the manufacturer's argument is the automated identification system or AIS data. They claim this tracking data proves the basian made a sharp turn to port to the left during the storm. Why does this matter? Because if the ship turned left into the wind, it would heal to the right. If it healed to the right, the port side, the left side, would be lifted out of the water. If the port side is out of the water, then an open door on that side wouldn't let water in. To make their open door theory work, they need the ship to heal to the left, submerging that port side door. So, they are arguing the ship actually turned right, exposing the port side to the waves. But the AIS data, according to them, shows a left turn. It gets confusing, but here is the critical flaw in their entire technical argument. The AIS data they are relying on is likely invalid for determining the ship's heading. We have analyzed the raw tracking information, and what is missing is the gyro data. Without a gyroscope feeding heading information to the transponder, AIS only records course over ground. This is a vector, not a heading. It tells you which direction the object is moving across the face of the earth, not which way the bow is pointing. In a violent storm with hurricane force winds, a super yacht with a massive mass acts like a sail. Even with the canvas furled, the wind pushes the boat sideways. If the basian was being pushed sideways by 60 knot winds, the AIS would show movement in that direction. If the boat was actually facing into the wind, as the crew testified they were trying to do, but was being blown backward and sideways, the track on a screen might look like a turn, but the boat wasn't turning. The boat was sliding. This distinction is everything. If the boat was sliding and healing to starboard, as the physics of a downburst would suggest, then the port side was high and dry. If the port side was up in the air, then it doesn't matter if that shell door was open or closed. It wouldn't have been the primary source of water ingress. And let's not forget, when the divers finally reached the hull, reports indicated that the shell door in question was found closed. Chapter 3, the centerboard myth. This brings us to the second major point of contention, the retractable keel. The lawsuit and armchair experts alike have hammered the crew for not having the keel fully lowered. The argument is simple. A lower keel means a lower center of gravity, which means more stability. Therefore, if the keel was up, the crew was negligent. But this ignores the operating manual of the basian itself. The documentation for this specific vessel states that the keel should be raised when the ship is under engine power or when it is navigating in shallow waters. It is designed to be a performance sailing feature specifically to improve upwind performance under sail. It is not a storm survival switch that you just flip in an emergency. Lowering a keel of that size is not instant. It is a massive hydraulic operation that takes time. Time the crew did not have. When a downburst hits, the priority is not tweaking the underwater appendages. It is gaining steerage. The crew testified that their primary focus was getting the engines running and the generators online so they could turn the bow into the wind. This is standard seammanship. If you can point the bow into the waves, you survive. If you are caught broadside, you capsize. They were fighting for control of the ship's orientation, which relies on propulsion. The keel position was likely a secondary concern and according to the ship's own protocols for being under engine, it was in the correct position. The idea that a crew fighting for their lives in a sudden violent squall forgot to press a button is a gross oversimplification of maritime crisis management. Furthermore, we have to look at the timing of this lawsuit. It was filed just as the Italian prosecutors were beginning their own intricate investigation. By filing a civil suit now, the Italian sea group is effectively trying to preempt the criminal investigation. They are putting their version of facts on the record before the official accident report is even released. It forces the defense, the widow, and the surviving crew to fight a massive expensive legal battle on two fronts. They have to defend themselves against potential manslaughter charges from the state and simultaneously defend against a nearly billion dollar bankruptcy inducing claim from the manufacturer. Sources close to the Lynch family have described this as an attempt to bully the victims into silence or settlement. If the manufacturer can successfully paint the crew as villains in the court of public opinion, it shields their brand. Super yachts are sold on the promise of invincibility and luxury. Admitting that a vessel could sink due to a design flaw, a stability issue, or a failure of the down flooding angles would be catastrophic for future sales. It is far cheaper to spend a few million on lawyers blaming the captain than to admit your unsinkable ship had a vulnerability. Chapter 4. Abandoned yacht update. While the lawyers battle over hundreds of millions in Italy, there is another maritime disaster unfolding in the Caribbean that highlights a different side of the industry. Total abandonment. We have been tracking the situation with the yacht known as motor yacht Doubleshot. This vessel ran ground in the Exumas, a pristine chain of islands in the Bahamas. For weeks, it has been sitting on the rocks, battering against the reef, and slowly disintegrating. Recent drone footage shows the extent of the damage. The hull is breached. Diesel fuel is leaking into the water. Pieces of the interior are washing up on the nearby beaches. And where is the owner? Gone. Silence. This is the dark side of the yaching world that rarely makes the glossy brochures. When a boat becomes a total loss, especially in international or foreign waters, the logistics of salvage are a nightmare. It is often cheaper for an owner to walk away, claim the insurance, and let the local government deal with the mess. But the government of the Bahamas doesn't have the unlimited resources to salvage every pleasure craft that tourists crash onto their reefs. So, the boat sits there. It becomes an environmental hazard, killing coral and polluting the water. Under maritime law, this vessel is effectively abandoned. Technically, if you have the means and the barge to drag it off the rocks, it is yours. But the cost of the salvage would likely exceed the value of the scrap. It is a stalemate where the environment is the loser. Chapter 5. Accountability crisis. The juxtaposition between these two stories is jarring. In Italy, you have a manufacturer fighting tooth and nail over a ship that is already at the bottom of the ocean, suing a widow for millions to protect their stock price. In the Bahamas, you have a million-dollar asset rotting on a reef because nobody wants to spend a dime to clean it up. Both situations reveal a lack of accountability. In one case, it is corporate accountability being deflected onto the crew. In the other, it is personal accountability being abandoned along with the ship. We are going to keep tracking the Basian filings, but this isn't the only legal battle tearing the industry apart right now. If you think this $790 million claim is messy, you need to see the truth about the Nautiles versus Bearing Yachts lawsuit. We have a full breakdown of that case and how it exposed the dark side of yacht marketing. It is all in our Super Yacht Files playlist. Click the box on your screen right now to watch the Nautiles investigation next. I'll see you in

- How Does a Ship’s Propeller Shaft Seal Work Underwater? | Science Behind Marine Engineering: Kind: captions Language: en I have always been curious about how the propeller of a ship which is underwater and the propeller shaft which goes through the hull for power transmission actually achieve sealing to prevent seawater from entering the engine room. Early tail shaft ceiling designs were relatively simple using packing seals which involve pressing rings of oil soaked asbestos or hemp rope around the shaft by adjusting the tightness of the gland to form a seal. But this has obvious drawbacks. high friction, easy wear and frequent leakage. Especially during high-speed navigation, it is harder to ensure ceiling performance, so it was gradually phased out. The most commonly used stern shaft sealing structure on ships today is the lip seal. Let's take a look at how it works. A typical stern shaft sealing system usually includes the stern shaft, stern tube, sealing device, and lubrication system. The sun tube is generally welded directly to the hole and sealing devices like lip seals are located at both ends of the stand tube consisting of the forward seal and the off seal. Let's focus on the forward seal. The lip seal consists of multiple rubber ceiling rings arranged sequentially on the ceiling housing with springs embedded inside the rings to provide continuous pressure against the shaft. Each ceiling ring looks like a lip which is where its name comes from. The space between the ceiling rings is called the intermediate chamber. As shown in the diagram, there are a total of five chambers. When operating underwater, the first lip seal on the outside closely adheres to the rotating shaft, preventing seawater from entering. But facing the strong water pressure is not enough to completely stop seawater infiltration. So air pressure slightly higher than water pressure is introduced into chamber number three. While the lubrication oil pressure levels in chambers four and five are slightly higher than the air pressure. This way the air pressure is conducted through the second chamber to the first chamber. This pressure difference can help prevent seawater from backflowing into the ceiling chamber and also helps elastically maintain the ceiling ring's adhesive force preventing loosening due to external pressure changes ensuring long-term ceiling effectiveness. The lubricating oil has three functions. First, lubrication to reduce friction between the ceiling ring and the shaft. Then cooling to dissipate the heat generated by the shaft's rotation. Finally, isolation using pressure differences to separate sea water from the interior environment. The second lip seal mainly prevents oil from leaking out, forming an oil seal chamber with the first one. And if a small amount of sea water and lubricating oil seeps into the third chamber, the flowing air will carry the mixed liquid away, then enter the collection cylinder for separation. You might notice a P-shaped seal in front of the first lip seal. Its main function is to prevent damage to the ceiling system from fishing nets and fishing lines.

The vent dampers were not water tight when closed.  The engine room only had flooding 29 feet deep..  lets fix the bulkhead shaft seals.   Think of a V seal that has a wide flange.  A seal on both sides of the bulkhead that rub the bulkhead surface. This wide seal like a mexican  sombrereo runs with a little lube applied.  Any water pushes it against the bulkhead even tighter. - Is Your Equipment Covered OFF-SITE? (The Truth About Inland Marine Insurance): Kind: captions Language: en Hello. So glad you're here. If you're running a business, you know that your most important assets aren't always just sitting in your office, right? They're out there on the road at a client's site heading to a customer. So, today we're going to talk about something crucial. How to make sure your business property is protected no matter where it is. So, let me just ask you this straight up. Think about your tools, your laptops, that expensive equipment, or even the products you're shipping out. The moment that stuff leaves your building, are you 100% certain it's still covered if something happens? A lot of people just assume it is, but well, the real answer can be a bit of a wakeup call. Okay, let's get into the nitty-gritty. Your standard commercial property policy is fantastic for things that don't move. It's built to cover your building and everything inside it, right at that specific address on the policy. But you see the problem, don't you? This creates a massive gap. What about your gear at a job site or inventory on a truck or even equipment you've left with someone else to get it repaired? That's exactly where most standard policies just stop. But don't worry, because there's absolutely a solution built for this exact problem. It's a special kind of coverage that basically follows your property around, making sure it's protected wherever it goes. And it's called inland marine insurance. So, what is it really? Think of commercial inland marine insurance as the missing piece of the puzzle. It's specifically designed to cover your business property once it leaves your main location. It protects your stuff while it's on the move, stored somewhere else, or even just in someone else's hands for a little while. And I know what you're probably thinking. Inland marine? Is this for boats? The name is super confusing. I get it. But let's clear that up right now. It has absolutely nothing to do with water. It's just a very old-fashioned name from a time when things were insured for transport either over the ocean or inland. So, yeah, no life jackets needed for this one. All right, so what kind of stuff are we actually talking about here? Let's look at the specific types of property that this coverage is really designed to protect. This really breaks it down into three key areas. First up, property and transit. Pretty simple. This is anything you're shipping, whether it's in your own truck or with a carrier like FedEx. Then there's property in a Bailey's care. That's just a fancy insurance term for when you leave your property with someone else, like a repair shop or a storage facility. And finally, you've got specialized movable property. This is a huge one. It covers everything from a contractor's tools to a photographers's cameras to the displays you take to a trade show. Now, you might be thinking, well, can't I just get more standard property coverage? And the thing is, that policy just isn't built for these kinds of risks. Let's dig into why a dedicated inland marine policy is really the smarter and frankly safer choice for your business. So, it really comes down to this. Inland marine insurance is specifically engineered for these three situations. Number one, property and transit. It protects your goods from the second they leave your door until they arrive safely. Number two, Bailey coverage. This makes sure that if say that repair shop has a fire, your property is still covered. And number three, specialized property. This gives you the flexible, tailored protection that unique and high value mobile equipment really truly needs. So, let's end with a question we started with. Your business is not static. It's out there. It's moving. The real question is, does your insurance policy move with it? If you have even a tiny bit of doubt about that, it's probably a good time to take a closer look. You need to be sure that your most valuable assets are protected no matter where your work takes you.

Swallowed by the dark water by the time they reached the dock. Jake's arms burned from rowing. his shirt clinging to him, breath ragged in his chest. He lifted the girl out first, handing her to her mother, who hugged her as if she'd never let go again. The father stumbled onto the dock, collapsing to his knees. Staring back at the empty stretch of water where their boat had vanished. Jake climbed out last dripping and exhausted, his hair plastered to his forehead. "You folks all right?" he asked, voice low. The mother looked up, tears streaming down her face. "We'd be dead if you hadn't been here," she whispered. "Don't know how." "Too, thank you." Jake shook his head, rubbing the back of his neck awkwardly. "Just glad I was close enough to hear you," he said. The little girl peeked out from her mother's arms, her big eyes full of wonder as she whispered, "You're a real hero." Jake knelled to her level, his rough hands resting on his knees. "Nothing heroic about not letting good people drown," he said softly. Just doing what needed doing. He drove them to the nearest inn, making sure they had dry clothes and a warm meal. When he finally returned home hours later, salt still on his skin, he thought little of it. He'd saved a family, but that was all. It wasn't heroism. It was just what a decent man was supposed to do. What Jake didn't know was that the next day, when the morning sun climbed high over his little shop, the quiet hum of his tools would be interrupted by the low purr of a luxury car rolling up his gravel drive. The same family would step out not just to thank him, but to offer a chance that would change his life forever. Turning one night of instinctive courage into a future he never imagined. The next morning, Jake had barely gotten his first sip of coffee before the memory of last night came rushing back. The girl's terrified eyes, the weight of her tiny body clinging to him as the boat went under. He shook it off, grabbed his wrench, and got back to work on an old Chevy he'd been fixing for a neighbor. To him, it had just been another night. Another moment where you didn't stop to think. He just acted dot. Around midm morning, the distant sound of an engine made him glance up. A sleek black luxury car glided down his gravel driveway, kicking up dust that looked out of place against its spotless paint. Jake frowned, wiping his hands on a rag as the car came to a stop. The same family from last night stepped out. The parents looking rested but emotional. The little girl holding a small stuffed toy in one hand and waving shily with the other. The father walked up first, gripping Jake's hand tight. "You saved our lives," he said firmly, voice thick with sincerity. We're not leaving it at just a thank you. Jake shifted awkwardly, not knowing what to say. Something bigger was about to land on his doorstep. Something tied to last night's rescue that would change his small town world forever. Jake leaned against the doorframe of his shop, watching as the father waved to the man in the luxury car. You may not know the name, but we own the boat manufacturing company the Parker family here runs. He glanced warmly at the mother and child standing nearby, then looked Jake dead in the eye. They told me what you did last night. You didn't just save their lives. You gave this family another chance. And we think that deserves more than just a handshake. Jake shifted his weight, unsure. Folks, I didn't do it for a reward. I just couldn't stand by. Richard gave a knowing smile. That's exactly why we're here. Our business has been trying to launch a community rescue initiative for years. We wanted someone with real grit, real heart, not just a name on a corporate roster. Last night proved you're exactly the kind of man we need. The father stepped closer, his voice low but full of emotion. Jake, we've seen what you can do under pressure. My family's alive because of you. We want you to partner with us. We'll sponsor your garage, upgrade your equipment, and fund. A training program right here where you can teach life-saving mechanical skills, boat safety, and rescue readiness for our local community. He paused, glancing toward his wife, who nodded with tears in her eyes. "We want to turn your shop into something that keeps saving lives, just like you did for us." Jake blinked, overwhelmed. His shop had always been a place to scrape by, fixing what was broken for a few bucks at a time. He'd never imagined it could be anything more than that. He looked down at his calloused hands, remembering how they'd gripped the orars last night. How the little girl had clung to his shirt, like he was the only thing keeping her in the world. Finally, he looked up, his voice gruff but steady. If we're doing this, it ain't about money or fancy titles. It's about Mackin. Sure, no one else has to scream for help the way you did. If we can save one more family, I'm in. The little girl stepped forward, clutching her stuffed toy, and hugged Jake's leg tightly. "I told mom you were a hero," she whispered. Jake knelt, resting a big, rough hand on her shoulder, eyes glistening. "I'm no hero, kiddo," he murmured. "Just a man who couldn't let you go under." Richard extended the contract, but Jake didn't need to read the fine print to know his life was about to change. For the first time in years, his small shop wasn't just a way to survive was about to become a lifeline for others. A place where knowledge, kindness, and courage turned into something bigger than himself. That evening after the luxury car disappeared down the road, Jake stood outside the garage. The salty breeze carrying the scent of the bay where it had all begun. The old sign above his shop creaked in the wind. Matt and Kristen hold their breath. The dead dolphin might not hold the operation. The boat survives the lift, but the hull looks like a nightmare. Marine growth covers large sections of the bottom and rust is visible everywhere. Matt's confidence begins to crack. The most terrifying discovery of all is the keel. A new one alone will cost $15,000, three times what they paid for the entire boat. >> The mixer elbow has completely broken off of the exhaust manifold. They will rebuild this boat from the floor up or go broke trying to save the dead dolphin. Matt and Kristen's plan is to tackle the exterior first, restoring the entire surface. Once that's done, he'll replace the engine and rebuild the rotten interior. But they can't afford to stay on land for long. Yard fees run $80 a day, so they're giving themselves just 10 days to get back in the water, where the renovation becomes far more affordable. After a deep pressure wash of the hull, Matt starts filling the holes and imperfections with an epoxy repair filler designed for boats. Now he can begin sanding, creating a smooth and level surface. Matt borrows the heavyduty sander and proper protective gear from local peer workers. Equipment that would have stretched their budget even further. Every inch of the hull and deck needs to be prepared for a fresh paint. The head sails are pulled out and taken off the boat to protect them from sanding dust and ongoing repairs. Replacing a damaged sail can cost thousands. And right now they can't afford that mistake. As the work intensifies, the boat turns into a hazardous construction zone. They refuse to have bear living there. Today is the day that we got some exciting news. We're now going to get off the boat. We get an Airbnb. So, this is where it really turns into a construction zone and I'll really really be able to rip it and dip it. They pack their entire lives into boxes and prepare to move. Every night in a real bed will add to the burn rate, but for now, living on board isn't safe. The next morning, Matt faces a challenge most sailors dread. Cutting into the deck. Soft spots underfoot have been warning him something's wrong. As he opens it up, the rot is clear. Years of trapped water have weakened the structure. If left unfixed, the floor could collapse under a hard blow. But the mast is the real threat. A rotten core could destabilize it, letting it fall and crash through the Matt has never performed this kind of surgery before, but in the boatyard, his philosophy is simple. There is nothing he can take off that they can't find a way to put back on. Under the relentless sun, Kristen joins Matt, helping shoulder the grueling work of sanding. The surface is prepared. Now it's time to give the dead dolphin a new look. They decide to handle every step of the preparation and application of the paint themselves. Matt mixes the base and a hardener, but because of the Puerto Rican heat, they have just an hour before the mixture hardens and becomes useless. Two layers are needed so they both arm themselves with rollers and work in sync. It's a demanding task. The second layer needs to be applied before the first one dries. This ensures the layers achieve a chemical bond, fusing into a single shield and preventing peeling. Suddenly the tropical weather turns. >> When the rain comes by and soaks everything for 45 minutes and then goes away. And these 30-minute rain showers will cost you half a day. By the time you clean up your entire work site, get everything changed and then when it comes out, you pull everything back out, work for 5 minutes, then it rains for another 30 minutes. Hours of meticulous work are threatened in minutes. For Matt and Kristen, these delays are frustrating and expensive. Luckily, the rain didn't ruin the hull paint, so they moved their focus to the In a marine environment, a smooth painted deck becomes as slippery as ice when wet. To keep themselves and bear steady in heavy seas, they apply a specialized non-skid additive on top. As the hours pass, the scale of what's left begins to sink in. The hull still waits, the most visible part of the boat. And for that, good won't be enough. They're aiming for a flawless mirror-like finish, and they won't settle for less. >> Since we're putting in all this effort, Matt put in all that work sanding it. We want the best. So, we're getting someone to hairspray it at a good price. >> Yeah. The master of all Caribbean painting, Salich, is going to put the final gloss on for just the hole. We still painted everything else. >> Salicha joins to inspect the boat. With a pencil in hand, he begins circling Matt's low spots and uneven sanding marks. He's going around with his pencil now and circling all my whoopsies that I either missed or made. >> You got an F. >> It's a great quiz. Okay. You probably got to see. They're professionals. >> Yeah, they they're going to do a good job. >> Matt sanded the deck too aggressively and removed the gel coat, the outer skin that protects the fiberglass beneath. They are forced to apply two to three times more paint layers than they initially planned just to build the surface thickness back up. They use Allgrip, a premium marine paint. It's expensive, but it delivers a glossy mirror-like finish that stands up to salt and sun. The air sprayer atomized the gray base into a fine mist, allowing for a level of uniformity that rollers and brushes simply couldn't have achieved. To complete the transformation, a worker prepares the surface to give the boat its modern yacht identity. Using blue masking tape as a guide, they plan to create decorative stripes along the hull that will be painted in a different color. For the first time since Puerto Rico, the dead dolphin is beginning to show real signs of progress. The focus shifts to the deck and hardware. Every fitting and repair has been sealed. They're aiming to lower the boat back into the water this week. If it holds, they can finally move to the inside renovation from the water. After all the delays, Matt and Kristen have missed their deadline and already spent more than $2,000 in yard fees. They can't afford more mistakes. Every penny spent is one less they can put toward renovating the interior. Back to the exterior paintwork. Only the section below the waterline remains. Its job is simple. Keep barnacles and algae from sticking. It doesn't have to look perfect. So, they decide to handle it themselves and save some money. The final coats go on and the dead dolphin is almost ready. One night to cure and tomorrow the real water test begins. It's launch day. The tension is palpable. They have worked nonstop to reach this moment. And they will finally find out if the boat is ready for the sea. As the hull touches the water, Matt climbs aboard, carefully checking that no water is getting in. Matt went in to check the through holes before they let go of the straps. and he's been in there for a long time and I'm starting to get nervous. I don't know what's going on. Because the part is obsolete, it can't be ordered. It has to be handcrafted from scratch, and they have no idea how to do it. From here, Matt takes over, carefully reassembling the system. On the first trial, the dead dolphin answers the helm. Control is back in their hands. Starting with the engine. >> We're going to attempt to take out our old, rusty, dusty engine. >> The 300lb engine hasn't left this room in four decades. Getting it out will not be an easy task. When Matt disconnects the hoses, all that's left of the old coolant is orange, rusty water. A sign of just how deep the corrosion goes. The old engine weighs too much. It's not coming out. They improvise an ingenious solution using the boat as a third helper. The howiard normally used for raising sails is attached to the engine. And using the mast's winch, they crank it up from above, turning the mast into a pulley. The boat does the heavy lifting while Matt and Kristen guide and control it. I'm going to try to get it out, but if it's not going perfect, I will back down. I'm not going to drop a 300lb motor on my head. I don't want to do that. They're tackling it themselves to avoid the cost of a professional, but if it gets too risky, they'll step back. The goal is to lift the engine out and slowly rest it on protective foam, keeping the deck safe from any damage. It's happening. The engine's going up. This is going to be nerve-wracking. Okay, I'm coming. I'm coming out. >> The operation begins. A 300lb drop would be disastrous. They move with extreme care. The engine is too heavy and the rope gives way. Oh my god. Why did that happen? Holy. >> Luckily, the protective foam absorbs the impact. They now have to take the engine to the shipyard, but it's miles away. With no car, they take a high stakes shortcut, moving it by water. Mission accomplished. The old engine is finally out. A brand new one is on the way, worth more than twice the price of the boat itself. It's an investment that could redefine the dead dolphin's future. But while they wait, there's no time to lose. An entire internal renovation awaits. Demolition begins inside the salon. The old cabinetry has to come out to reveal the damage underneath. As Kristen peels back the panels, the truth is clear. The wood is completely rotted. It has to be cut out entirely and rebuilt from scratch. The old side bulkheads hold the plates that keep the mast standing. If they failed while sailing, the mast could collapse. Matt removes them and will rebuild the structure, giving it a stronger base to support the mast. For the replacement, Matt was tempted to save money on standard plywood, but he was advised that marine grade was essential to make it last. At $150 a sheet, it's a serious investment, especially since he's not a professional woodworker. Meanwhile, Kristen chimes in with the next point. >> These pocket doors, he hates them and he wants to get rid of them. We don't use them. I don't mind them. And you know, if you want to shut a room, you have that option. He doesn't want them. He wants to move them so you can sweep the floor. They have the thing there. The dead dolphin is the couple's home, and every decision feels personal. With the doors out of the way, Matt removes the big bulkheads. The plan is to use them as templates for the new sheets, so the removal needs to be careful. He's pushing just hard enough to separate the panels, but not so much that they snap. Space is a major challenge during the interior renovation. The new sheet is too large to pass through the boat's narrow doorway, so the work has to be done on the pier. The plan is to cut the sheet in two horizontally and rejoin it later. adding extra work, but it's the only way to make it fit. Working on the water brings its own difficulty. While staying in a slip saves $80 a day, the waves make every task harder, especially for precise woodworking. Matt turns his attention to one of the salon's weakest points, the old windows. They bought a new one as a test and couldn't wait to try it, planning to replace the rest later in the renovation if it works well. After all, it wouldn't make sense to turn the dead dolphin into a brand new yacht without first sealing any potential water entry points that could flood it. The glass is so clear that it almost looks like there's no glass at all. They had forgotten what it was to have clear windows. As work continues, the cabinetry can finally be installed. Once again, Matt relies on templates taken from the original pieces. The new cabinetry is engineered as one solid unified piece. It is designed to be removable, allowing Matt and Kristen to easily detach it in the future to inspect the critical chain plates hidden behind the salon walls. He's thought of a solution designed for life at sea while giving the interior a clean, modern look. Mechanical push catch latches will keep the cabinets firmly closed. With a simple press, a spring releases the door, popping it open just enough to grab. For the sofa, the original fold down was unstable, uncomfortable, and cramped. So, he rebuilds the base from scratch. Now the top platform slides out from the wall to form a bed larger than a twin with fold down legs strong enough for two adults. As Matt works, the tropical weather provides a grim reminder that the boat's exterior defenses are still failing. >> The other day when it was raining, I just kind of looked in here, but it was so much water coming out, I couldn't exactly tell where it was coming from. I believe it was this thing here, but I'm not 100% sure. So, I'm going to go outside and start throwing some buckets of water around. >> He's coming. >> I don't see anything yet. >> Okay, I'm going to pour some more. If you see it, just knock on this. Okay. >> Okay. Okay, I see it. >> It's coming out of the chain plate type thing. >> It wasn't coming out of any of the spots up here.
