I think I'm going to just go up there with some more silicone, tape it off, get some goo in there, and then that should keep the grain out of the boat. Water is the ultimate enemy of any boat project. Matt is sealing every opening on deck to protect their hard work from leaks while giving the ceiling a cleaner look. Only once the boat is watertight can the kitchen transformation begin. The galley gets a total purge, a messy demolition down to a blank canvas, making sure nothing rotten or patched with band-aids is reused. The first replacements are the ruined, unreliable drawers that have jammed for decades. They used to swing open while sailing, so Matt builds custom units on stainless steel slides. He also adds mechanical push button locks to keep pots and cutlery from becoming dangerous projectiles. Matt replaces the awkward floor level bin with a custom slide out trash can at counter height, making it easy to open and close. The entire rail system is designed to be removable, giving them full access to the sink if they need to make future repairs or updates. On a boat, easy access is key to avoiding problems down the line. Next, they tackle a heavy, useless cabinet that was so impractical it ended up just collecting junk. Matt destroys it, replacing the bulk with sleek bypass doors that dramatically increase storage depth for pots and pans. To keep the peace while sailing, he lines the interior with foambacked vinyl, ensuring their dishes travel in silence even in heavy seas. For the countertop, they need a material that is lightweight and 100% waterproof, and PVC turns out to be the best option. The couple wrestles the oversized sheet through a doorway barely wide enough to fit it, eventually cutting it down to size. Cheaper than most alternatives, PVC won't rot, swell, or attract termites in the tropical humidity. Sharp corners and moving waves are a bad combination. For safety, Matt rounds off every edge to prevent injuries when the ocean gets rough. They also create a 2-in overhang for a custom high-end look. But when they try bending it with a heat gun, the material bubbles like a burnt marshmallow, ruining the finish. Just then, they get the news they've been waiting weeks for. The $15,000 engine is finally ready. They drop everything and head out to collect it. Thanks to Salicha's generosity, Matt and Kristen borrow his truck to handle the load, picking up the most expensive and heaviest piece of the project, the boat's new heart. After clearing the paperwork and handling the local taxes, the engine officially has new owners. Back at the harbor, the unboxing begins. Matt reveals a gleaming red 30 horsepower Beta Marine engine. Running at just 50 to 60 dB, it's way quieter than a standard diesel. a total game changer compared to their old rusty nightmare. >> We are finally going to put our engine in. The installation should go pretty easy. But it's not the engine I'm worried about. It's our boat. It's This thing's 100,000 years old. So, is it going to fit? What am I going to have to change? Hopefully, it just goes right in there and it's a plug and play. There's some prep work that has to go with that. So, I have to clean out my engine room and it's time to get busy. Unboxing the components felt like Christmas morning, but the excitement is quickly met with a reality check. The arrival of the engine has only highlighted how much structural work remains before they can finally install it and turn the key. With the workspace cleared, Matt faces a dangerous task. Replacing the shaft seal. This component wraps around the spinning propeller shaft, allowing it to turn while preventing the ocean from rushing through the hole in the hull. After 40 years of salt and friction, the old seal is unreliable and must come off, but it refuses to move. I've been beating on that thing for about an hour now, which I haven't got a even a hair out of it. Desperate, Matt calls on Salicha for help. It takes the intense heat of a blowtorrch and a lot of patience to break the rust's grip and free the shaft. >> Matt then brings in the brand new replacement. >> This is the part that goes on that keeps the water out of the boat. So, this thing is a important. Once the internal shaft seal is in place, Matt must perform one final underwater task. Reinstalling the propeller. Finally, the engine can come in. >> I'm not going to say I'm nervous, but I'm not not nervous. Selchicha and the professional yard crew step in for the heavy lift, swinging the 400-lb motor onto the boat. Unlike the old engine, there's a lot more at risk. Matt is terrified. Fortunately, the maneuver the couple feared for days takes less than a minute. With four pairs of hands, the engine is safely in place. >> So teacher is the man. He got it in here. I was nervous. Definitely not the way I would have done it. I was scared. I was like a little baby boy about to start crying. But he got it in. You know, that's why you hire the pros. >> They ensure the connection is perfectly aligned since even a small imbalance could cause severe vibrations at high revolutions per minute. With the mechanical connection solid, Matt installs the life support systems, a seawater strainer to filter the ocean water that cools the engine, and a Raker fuel filter to protect their investment from the dirty diesel often found in remote Caribbean ports. >> So, now it's time to put the vent loop in. This is basically because our engine's at the water line. We have to get a siphon anti-ciphon valve up above the water line so that if something crazy were to happen, water doesn't come back through and end up going toward the engine. It all just goes away from the end. With the starter battery installed, they're ready to crank the engine. Fresh oil goes in and the moment of truth arrives. This plugged in and I have my battery hooked up. I'm just going to flick the key a little bit to make sure my power is good. Make sure that's running before I have to troubleshoot something. put the filter back on. They can only hope nothing serious happened to the engine. Aren't you excited? >> I'm going to crank crank that soldier boy. >> The engine works perfectly. Matt forgot to reinstall the oil filter at first, leaving the system open and sending oil spraying everywhere. With that solved, the next phase is installing the two new control cables, one for speed and one for shifting. Both will run from the engine straight up to the steering pedestal. And finally, they bring in a brand new $2,000 wheel autopilot. This single upgrade is a total lifestyle shift for a two-person crew. No longer tethered to the helm for 24-hour crossings, they can now step away to fish or actually sleep while the boat holds its course. He mounts a pulley ring to the wheel, creating a direct mechanical link to the new autopilot. The drive motor at the base provides the brawn to pull the belt, while the control box on the side acts as the brain. He must space them precisely. If the motor's magnets get too close to the control box's internal compass, the interference would send the boat in circles. Back inside, the bathroom renovation begins with the toilet as Matt sets a base to achieve the optimal sitting height and ensure complete stability. Months have passed since they began this massive renovation, but now their worst nightmare is approaching. A hurricane storm is forecast for next week, threatening to cause serious damage to the boat. But until the storm arrives, Matt and Kristen keep pushing forward. The lower part of the shower's structural frame is rotted from decades of leaks. Matt rebuilds it from scratch and fiberglasses the area to prevent future damage. At the top of the shower, he fabricates a removable PVC niche to cover an old access hole, angling it backward to keep shampoo bottles in place. For polishing and painting, they clear the space to reach every corner. The final touch is a major quality upgrade. They plan to replace the stained plastic sink with an elegant porcelain basin. To finish the vanity, Matt applies a modern wood grain laminate to the upper cabinet. His skills are clearly improving with each step of the renovation. He builds custom push lock doors with built-in mirrors, giving the vanity a sleek, modern touch. For the final countertop finish, they move to their Airbnb where a stable floor makes the job easier. Matt applies heat to the epoxy mixture to release trapped air bubbles and ensure a clean finish. Know a lot of y'all were worried about Bear cuz you haven't seen her since we moved on the boat, but she's just living her best Airbnb life. Limitless couches, limitless bunk beds, limitless air conditioning. So, you know, she's being a sweet baby. She's happy, but she's kind of turned into a little stinker. In the afternoon, the weather begins to turn. Matt and Kristen rush down to the pier. >> The storms are coming in. It's getting windy, and it's not even the day the storm's going to be here yet, so I'm not feeling very good about this. >> With a hurricane approaching, every item exposed to the wind must be removed before it's too late. The head sail fights back as the wind picks up in violent bursts. Finally, they managed to bring it under control and drag the massive fabric onto the grass to gain enough leverage to fold it. To prepare for the storm surge, Matt reinforces the dead dolphin with extra 1 in thick lines. With the new engine in place and parts of the boat already renovated, there's too much at stake now. From above, they watch helplessly as the harbor disappears into gray mist and driving rain. Matt fears the boat may not withstand the fury and hurries back down. >> Well, you better be careful because I'm not advising you to go out in this weather any appears in order until he spots a friend's vessel untied and drifting. Against the fierce storm, Matt battles to secure the runaway boat, fighting to prevent disaster. He manages to tie it down just in time. Nightfall brings even worse conditions. Will the dead dolphin survive the night? The next morning, the sky breaks clear and the week's forecast looks much better. Just coming to check my ropes from last night. Still coming out to see if anything anything moved. With the dead dolphin holding strong, they dive back into the renovation. After finishing the bathroom, they move on to the V-birth, their personal bedroom. Being at the point of the boat, the room is narrower and more compact, so every inch must be carefully planned to ensure comfortable sleeping. For the new walls, they install custom wood shiplap, but it's hard to follow the boat's natural curves with the straight planks. Next, Matt tackles the front cabinetry. He completely demolishes the original, poorly built unit to create space for a modern, functional replacement. This area is critical because it hides the boat's chain plates and windless wiring, requiring a design that prioritizes emergency access. Kristen specifically planned this area to store and use her laptop and other electronics. So, the new design has an extra lower cabinet to maximize storage. As Matt finishes the new front cabinet, she begins painting the entire room white. This layer helps eliminate the cave feeling of the bow. Soon, they notice cool air entering the room and discover the next problem. a broken window, likely shattered by the hurricane's powerful gusts. Luckily, replacement windows were already on their list, and the new ones recently arrived. They quickly set about fixing it. To finish the space, the walls and ceiling remain. Matt and Kristen choose custom maple wood trim for laminating, carefully crafting each piece to fit perfectly and add warmth to the room. Trying to bend the new panel, it snaps at the corner. Rather than waste more material, Matt comes up with a solution. A custom maple trim piece that hides the seams and gives the room a polished, professional finish. Although the laminating looks perfect, they're not convinced by the white wood walls. >> We're going to be tearing it out and replacing it with 1/4 in PVC. >> Trying to force the straight boards onto the curved wall caused sections to stick out. And the intense white paint looks terrible. They tear it all out and start over. This time wrapping 1/4 in PVC boards in high quality moldresistant vinyl. For the ceiling, they install modern LED overhead lights. They're discreet and match the room's style. Now the most transformative stage has arrived. Laminating all the rooms starting with the bathroom. This is the final step before the finishers. Soon the yacht will be complete. After nearly a year in Puerto Rico, working week after week on the dead dolphin and learning through trial and error, the amateur labels are finally peeling away. Matt and Kristen have moved beyond simply making it work to truly making it perfect. The new bathroom door opens a full 180° instead of sliding into the wall, instantly making the bathroom feel much more spacious. They continue the laminating work for the salon. Matt's technique is beyond impressive. By this point in the renovation, he's looking like a real pro. >> You know what they call me here in Puerto Rico? They call me Larry Lemonado. I don't >> because I do the laminating perfectly. I can do it. >> In the galley, the most challenging sections are the curves. Learning from past mistakes, Matt uses a heat gun to soften the rigid sheets, allowing them to bend smoothly around the rounded corners of the countertop. Because their new adhesive bonds instantly on contact, they use wooden dowels as a floating barrier. This allows them to line up the panel perfectly without the glue touching the wall prematurely. Once aligned, they pull the spacers out and let the laminate drop into place. The goal for the trash bin is to make the galley's least glamorous feature blend seamlessly into the countertop as if it weren't there at all. Precision is the priority for the new sliding cabinets, which replace the clunky door they previously hated. Matt laminates the face frame as one continuous piece because he believes seams are amateur. The old navigation station, a space they no longer needed thanks to digital navigation, is now being converted into a booze locker. The new cabinet houses an elevated fridge and a deep compartment that can store up to 12 bottles of liquor. With the kitchen complete, it's time to fix the boat's secret shame. the electrical system. Years ago, Matt had wired the panel with cheap lamp wires that once sparked and nearly caused a fire. To properly secure their home, he reroutes every line up the bulkheads and into the ceiling so nothing chafes while the boat is in motion. He installs 10 dimmable lights throughout the living area, giving the space the feel of a modern private airplane rather than a 40-year-old sailboat. Next, the control center gets a redesign. Matt removes the outdated AC fuse box and keeps the 115V and 12vt system separate to reduce fire risk. The new station features essential switches, a 12vt outlet, and a battery monitor, giving them full control over their energy use. On the dock, a package has arrived. After years of washing clothes in a bucket, they finally splurge $1,200 on a 30 lb mini washing machine that can run their laundry entirely off solar power. To make it fit their lifestyle, they repurposed the aft cabin, once a pantry and makeup room, into a combination laundry room and garage. Before the renovation, the water maker was basically a piece of luggage under the couch. Every time they needed fresh water, Matt had to haul it out and run a hose to the sink. Now it has a permanent spot connected directly to the sink, so fresh, portable water is always on tap. They finish the shower by installing the water valves and head. The hot water dial is purely for aesthetics since they don't have a heater, but it fits the high-end look they want. The next morning, they focus on the hatches, the primary line of defense against the ocean. They considered buying new ones, but at $1,000 each, and with exact fits nearly impossible to find for such an old boat, it would have meant cutting into the Restoring the originals was the only way to ensure an affordable, watertight fit without compromising the home. The final stretch begins at the entrance with just the companion way. the salon floor and the sofa left to complete. For the companion way, Maple sets the tone for a modern home, saying goodbye to weathered teak and leaks. The old floor was a minefield of gaps and splinters. To replace it, they chose vinyl planks in a beach sand tone. The color hides Caribbean sand. The surface is waterproof and it's tough enough to handle their cat's claws. Lessons from the past have sunk in. Years ago, they used cheap fabric for the couch cover that shrank and metal zippers that rusted in the salt air. This time they chose Sunbrella fabric, UVresistant and nearly indestructible in the tropics. Kristen handles the upholstery herself. After more than a year of relentless work, the tools are finally down. The renovation of the dead dolphin has come to an end. What started as a $5,000 warning is now a modern yacht that defies its 40-year age. It's easy to forget how far they've come. This project began as a boat filled with holes and rot, barely standing. Since then, they've survived a long trip through the Caribbean, an engine failure in the unforgiving Mona Passage, and faced the heartbreak of a splash day disaster that sent them right back to the yard. But they didn't give up. Far from family and friends and with no professional experience, they faced the full renovation of the boat head on. They even stood their ground against the 70 mph fury of a hurricane and worked non-stop to transform and care for every inch. The result is far greater than they could have ever imagined a year ago when all they could think about was tearing it apart and abandoning their dreams of a life at sea. Of course, a project like this doesn't come cheap. The total price tag for this transformation came to $25,479. And by the end of the project, Matt has invested over $100,000 worth of man-hour into rebuilding the dead dolphin. But for Matt and Kristen, this boat is more than fiberglass and wood. It's their home, the result of years of persistence, long nights in the yard, and the belief that something broken can be brought back to life. Originally, the entrance had uncomfortable washboards. They replaced them with custom swinging saloon doors with mosquito screens, letting in the breeze while keeping the bugs out. Matt built a custom drop-down door for the electrical system, neatly concealing all cables and equipment. Kristen installed a Wi-Fi switch to control four lighting zones through a dongle. Fully dimmable and app connected, they can adjust brightness or turn the lights off straight from their phones. After over a year stuck in port, the dead dolphin is finally ready to sail. And the family is eager to leave the dock behind Bear is back on board, happy to be home again and reunited with his family. What Kristen doesn't know yet is that a big surprise awaits. >> We're engaged. We just got engaged at the cave. I cried a little bit. I just got to tell the world. I'm so happy. I just got to tell the world. >> I'm just happy that such a beautiful, perfect woman would be that happy to marry some old cuss like me. I love this woman more than anything on earth, and I couldn't go another day without being engaged with her. I love you and I'm happy we're engaged. >> It's official. We're fiances >> and you're going to be my wife. >> The project that tested their home, their relationship, and their resilience in ways they never imagined ends with a forever love. More adventures will come for Matt and Kristen. And if you want to join the crew, follow their YouTube channel, Sailing Good, Bad, and Ugly.

- Poor Couple Gets A Baby From The Sea, But The Twist is💥🤯⁉️⚠️ | Movie Explained in Hindi: Kind: captions Language: en-US Welcome, dear Rockers. How are you all? Today, we bring you a movie that will make it difficult for you to decide whether it's just a story or a heart-warming poem. After the war, a World War I soldier finds himself alone on a deserted island, working as a lightkeeper. That is, maintaining a lighthouse. What happened on that island? How did his life progress? And did this man, suffering from war trauma, ultimately live a happy life? Look, friends, the intro seems pretty normal. But when you watch this story, you'll be deeply emotional. So, without further ado, let's get started. This excellent romantic drama, The Light Between Oceans, was released in 2016. At the beginning of the story, a lightkeeper is being interviewed. The interview is being conducted by a World War I soldier named Tom. This scene takes place a few months after the end of World War I. The interviewer explains that you 'll have to follow certain rules to live on the island where you're going to be a lightkeeper. You'll live alone on the entire island and keep us informed of even the smallest incident. No one else can live there, and this isn't a permanent job. It 's only a six-month contract. You'll join duty in two days, and until then, you're allowed to stay on the mainland. Tom agrees, and in the next scene, he's on a train to the mainland. From the mainland, he 'll be taken by boat to the island with the lighthouse, Janus Rock. The mainland is actually a small, picturesque village. After getting off the bus, Tom starts walking towards the village when he sees a girl on the shore. For some reason, Tom feels a little strange seeing her. The girl also looks at him. There are officials who manage Janus Rock . Tom first meets them. Then the girl he saw on the shore arrives. She tells him her name is Isabelle, and Tom tells her his name as well . Other than that, nothing else happens between them. They all sit down and have dinner. Then they tell Tom that the lightkeeper who was on Genesis Rock before him often experiences hallucinations. The problem is that it's a deserted island, where the lightkeeper has to live alone. A person who spends his days alone is bound to have a mental breakdown, and there's a chance of hallucinations . They further explain that the lightkeeper is currently undergoing treatment. Until he returns, they'll have to take care of the lighthouse and report everything. In the next scene, they take Tom on a boat and leave him at Genesis Rock. Tom can't make contact with the outside world . In an emergency, he can only contact his superiors via radio . Such are the restrictions of this job. Anyway, work must be done . They leave Tom on the island and depart. With only a lighthouse and a small house to live in, Tom begins his life at Genesee Rock . He often stares out the window, sometimes walks the shore, sometimes works as needed. Sometimes he cuts trees, and sometimes repairs any problems in the lighthouse. Three months pass quickly. Then, one day, some people from the mainland come and take Tom with them. They tell him that the previous lightkeeper at Genesee Rock, for whom they had been waiting to return, is no longer alive. Therefore, Tom will have to make this job permanent, meaning he will be able to stay at this job for the next three years. First, he is asked, "Are you ready?" Tom signs the contract, saying, "Okay, I'm ready." After two days, Tom will have to return to Genesee Rock. Then, Tom remembers Isabelle and goes straight to her house. Her mother opens the door. Tom tells her, "My job has been made permanent." She's overjoyed to hear this. But Isabelle knows perfectly well that Tom has come to inquire about her. So Isabelle's mother invites Tom inside, and they all have dinner together. Her mother asks, "Son, you're going to James Rock tomorrow, right? So what do you plan to do today?" Isabelle quickly replies, "Hey, Tom promised to come on a picnic with me." Everyone agrees, "Well, that 's great." But Tom suddenly looks at Isabelle in shock, as he hadn't even spoken to her before. Tom doesn't say anything, but he understands that Isabelle wants to spend some time with him. In the next scene, the two of them sit together on the beach, talking about many things. Isabelle asks, "How big is that?" How do you manage to live on the island? Don't you get lonely? Tom answers in just two or three words, "Yes, but what can I do? I have work to do." But Isabelle keeps talking . Tom enjoys listening to her, friends. Then the sunset comes. Isabelle explains, "Both my brothers were soldiers, but they died in World War I. You're a soldier too, right?" He says, "Yes, so where are your parents? My mother passed away, and I don't know about my father. I'm alone right now." She continues, " Will you take me to that island too?" "No, no, I can't take you . According to the rules, I can only take my wife." Isabelle quickly says, "Okay, then marry me." Tom laughs. "Oh, I know you're joking. Because if you're serious, there's no one more stupid than you. Who would want to marry a man like me?" Then they both leave . Isabelle says, "You'll definitely be lonely there. So do one thing, keep writing to me. I'll write to you too." The next day, Tom leaves for Janus Rock, and the first thing he does is start writing to Isabelle. "Dear Isabelle, every day, every night, whenever I look out at this desolate sea, I look for you. I think you're waiting for me on that shore. I chose the job of lightkeeper on Janus Rock to stay away from humans. In this war, my family and my friends all perished before my eyes. Only I survived. I was dying day and night because of this Guild. That's why I longed for a world without humans, and when I found such a world in this job, I readily accepted. But now I don't want loneliness, nor am I alone. Those conversations with you echo in my mind. And I think of you all the time. Thank you for coming into my life, becoming the cure for my loneliness." After writing all this, he sends a letter to Isabelle, and she is delighted to read it and responds. She also sends a photo of herself. She writes, "Even living alone there has alleviated your loneliness. But I live with so many people, yet I feel lonely here without you. I constantly wonder when you'll come back? When will we talk again." Through such letters, their love deepens. One day, Tom proposes to Isabelle. She and her family agree . They get married on the mainland. The villagers live like family, friends, and celebrate their wedding with great pomp. Then, Tom and Isabelle move to Genesee Rock. It was Isabelle's first time setting foot on the shore, and the place seemed beautiful to her, as if it were made just for Tom and Isabelle. Their life begins full of happiness. Isabelle loves playing the piano. But even though they have a piano here, she can't play it because it's broken. These friends also raise chickens. At night , they spend the night stargazing and talking. Isabelle becomes pregnant some time later, but they don't tell her family or anyone else on the mainland. They keep it to themselves. Isabelle says they'll tell her when the baby is born. No one can come here anyway. Four months pass. One night, a massive storm hits, and the lighthouse must always be on. So Tom sets off for the lighthouse, which is near their home. After his departure, Isabelle suddenly experiences stomach pains . She tries to inform Tom via radio, but the signal is blocked by the storm. Finding no other way, Isabelle somehow manages to make it to the lighthouse entrance. She screams, but Tom can't hear her over the noise of the storm. The next day, when Tom emerges from the lighthouse , poor Isabel is lying there unconscious. She's lost a lot of blood. Yes, friends, the poor woman had an abortion. They bury the baby at Janus Rock . Isabel had dreamed of having a child, of having a small, happy family. But that dream was shattered. Tom says we should go to the mainland and see a doctor and tell your family . But Isabel refuses, saying people will then say all sorts of things. We won't tell anyone. I don't even want to go to a doctor. Then, friends, one day Tom brings some people to Janus Rock. Upon seeing them, Isabel knows Tom has definitely brought a doctor, and she becomes angry with Tom, saying, "How many times did I tell you that I need you?" She refuses to see a doctor. Angered by this, Isabelle goes and sits on a mountain . After a while, she returns home to vent her anger on the doctor. It turns out the doctor Tom brought wasn't a doctor, but a piano repairman. She apologizes, "Sorry for making false accusations against him." So their lives go on. Just fine. Isabelle becomes pregnant again, but now she's afraid she might have a miscarriage. So she 's very careful with everything. But this time, she starts having stomach pains. She's terrified. She says, "I don't want to lose this baby. Tom, please do something." So he goes about his daily routine . But Isabelle is lying at the spot where the two aborted babies were buried. Suddenly, a baby cries out. Isabelle thinks it's just her imagination. He immediately comes out and calls out to Isabelle, who also runs to see. They see that the boat contains a newborn baby and a man's corpse . They are very proud that Isabelle gave birth to a baby girl without any assistance . A man accompanying them sees the toy-like appearance of the baby. First, they take a nice family photo. Lucy's grandparents are overjoyed to see her. Then they go to church to have Lucy baptized . But it seems the church isn't open yet. They wait for it to open . So Tom sees a woman in a nearby cemetery. She's crying profusely in front of a grave. After she leaves, Tom goes to look and discovers that the woman's name is Hannah. It's actually her husband's grave. Tom is shocked to see the man's death date on it, because it was the day after Tom and Isabelle found the boat Lucy was in. This means the body must be Hannah's husband's. Perhaps Lucy is Hannah's daughter. But because Hannah couldn't find the boat or her daughter, she assumed both her husband and daughter had drowned in the sea . That's why a grave has been built here in her husband's name. Hannah's husband escaped in a boat to save himself and his daughter, and then you know what happened. She remembers the time she spent with her husband. The next scene shows Lucy now grown up and very happy on the island. - No Engineer Could Fix the CEO's $2M Yacht — The Single Dad Janitor Did It With a Paperclip.: Kind: captions Language: en Victoria Hayes's $2 million yacht died in the middle of the most important evening of her professional life. 12 engineers stood in a ring around the frozen control panel, each one quiet, each one unwilling to touch the system that had simply stopped responding. The tension aboard was absolute. Then a man in a maintenance uniform stepped forward, Daniel Brooks. He said almost nothing. He crouched down, pulled a small paperclip from his breast pocket, and within 30 seconds, the entire vessel came back to life. But what made Victoria go completely still had nothing to do with the ship. Daniel Brooks was 32 years old, and if you saw him on the dock at first light, you would see only what you were meant to see. A man in a gray uniform pushing a mop across the polished teak, invisible in the way that working people so often become invisible in places built for the wealthy. He moved methodically, without hurry. And he never lingered near the yachts he was not assigned to clean. The marina where he worked was one of the most expensive on the Eastern Seaboard, a place where 12-m vessels rocked gently against their moorings, and the people who owned them rarely learned the names of the people maintain them. The ratio of dollars to decency ran in a particular direction here, and everyone understood it, even the people who preferred not to say so. Daniel had been at the marina for 2 years. In that time, he had earned a reputation for being quiet, reliable, and almost entirely forgettable. His supervisor trusted him because he showed up early, worked late, and never complained when someone assigned him the worst jobs on the shift, the bilge cleaning, the hull scrubbing in January, the overnight watch on the week of the high wind advisory. His coworkers liked him in a mild, distant way. He was polite. He kept his locker clean, and on Fridays he sometimes brought a small tin of oatmeal cookies to the break room. He never talked about himself. When people asked where he had worked before, he said over in the sound with a vagueness that discouraged follow-up, and they took the hint. What none of them knew, what no one at that marina knew, was that Daniel had spent the better part of a decade as a marine systems engineer, certified in electromechanical control systems for mid-range and large-scale vessels. He had commissioned three yachts, overseen the complete refit of a 120-ft charter boat, and once diagnosed a hydraulic fault in a vessel's stabilizer system that two other engineers had spent 11 days failing to locate. He had been good at it, better than good. He had possessed the kind of diagnostic instinct that could not be taught from a manual, the ability to listen to a machine the way a doctor listens to a patient, not just for the obvious signals, but for the absences, the hesitations, the things that were almost but not quite right. Then his daughter was born, and 6 months later his wife left. And the firm he worked for became entangled in an accident that should have ended a senior colleague's career. Somehow, in the way these things sometimes go, Daniel's name ended up in the wrong place on the wrong report, and the career ended his instead. He did not fight it. He had a newborn and no reserve for prolonged battles. He accepted the settlement, moved somewhere smaller and quieter, and eventually found his way to the marina where no one asked too many questions and the work was honest and the hours were predictable enough to plan a child's life around. Chloe was six now. She was small for her age, with her father's eyes and a way of asking questions that consistently caught adults off guard. She spent her after-school hours at the marina on the days Daniel could not arrange a sitter, doing her homework on an overturned crate near the supply shed, watching the big boats with a kind of attentive appreciation that looked, in a child so young, almost like professional interest. She cataloged the vessels without being told to. She could identify 12 different hull designs by sight, and had strong opinions about the relative aesthetic merits of ketch versus sloop rigging. She had once asked her father whether the really large yachts were more complicated than cars, and when he confirmed that they were, she had nodded and said, without any doubt at all, "So you could probably fix them if you had to." It was not a question. It was a declaration of faith from someone who had watched her father fix almost everything that had broken in their apartment over the past 2 years, and had drawn the only logical conclusion. Daniel had not known how to answer, so he had handed her a juice box and changed the subject. Victoria Hayes did not know any of this. Victoria Hayes did not spend much time thinking about the people who cleaned her vessels. She was the CEO of Hayes Maritime Logistics, a company she had built over 15 years into one of the more formidable regional freight and charter operations on the coast. She was 40, precise, and deeply allergic to anything she could not measure or control. She ran her company the way she ran everything in her life, with documented procedures, performance metrics, and a reflexive skepticism toward anything that relied on instinct or luck. She did not believe luck existed. She believed that preparation was either sufficient or insufficient, and that insufficient preparation was always traceable to someone's failure. This belief had served her extremely well. It had also made her difficult to work for, difficult to argue with, and on certain occasions, difficult to help. She owned the Meridian, $2 million of custom German engineering, as much as a business asset as a personal one. She hosted contract signings aboard it, entertained clients, and occasionally conducted negotiation dinners in the main salon when she wanted the other party slightly off balance. The Meridian was part of her apparatus, and she expected it to function. Victoria Hayes and Daniel Brooks had shared the same stretch of dock for 2 years without exchanging more than four words total. The contract that evening was worth $140 million over 3 years. The client was a consortium of regional port operators who had been considering Victoria's proposal for 8 months. The dinner was the final step. She had planned it with the same attention she gave quarterly earnings reports, the menu, the crew uniforms, the salon lighting, the precise order in which documents would be introduced and signed. Every variable she could anticipate, she had anticipated. The one she had not anticipated was the relay, the Meridian's integrated navigation and power management system, the network that coordinated propulsion, lighting, climate control, and onboard communications into a single supervisory architecture went silent 40 minutes before the guests were scheduled to board. Not a gradual failure, not a warning. One moment the system was fully operational, the next, the primary control panel was dark and unresponsive. The backup panel cycling through an error sequence without resolution. The marina's head engineer arrived within 10 minutes, two of his staff close behind. They were joined by a pair of technicians from the systems manufacturer's regional office, who had been reached by emergency line and driven over in something close to a controlled panic. Then two independent contractors arrived, specialists that Victoria's operations manager kept on retainer for exactly this kind of situation. 12 engineers in total gathered around the panel like physicians around a patient who had stopped breathing for no reason anyone could locate. They ran diagnostics. The diagnostics returned clean. They checked the power supply. Stable. They combed through the software logs line by line, looking for an error flag, a corrupted process, anything that could explain what was happening. There was nothing. The system appeared, by every measurable indicator, to be perfectly healthy. It simply was not responding. Victoria stood at the salon entrance and watched. Her phone showed the time, 50 1 minutes until the consortium's lead representative was scheduled to arrive. She had spoken to her operations manager twice in the past 20 minutes. Both conversations had been short. He did not have answers, and she did not have patience for long exchanges without answers. 48 minutes. Daniel was 20 m away, working his way along the dock with a broom, clearing the fine salt residue that gathered at the base of the cleats after a high tide afternoon. He had noticed the cluster of people around the Meridian when it first formed. He had registered the vessel's make and class, German-built, mid-90s design, updated avionics package, the kind of integration retrofit that always introduced small mechanical vulnerabilities when contractors rushed the installation to meet a launch date. He recognized the pattern in how two of the engineers were moving, the slightly too rapid button sequences, the way one of them kept checking the same display screen three times in a row. It was the behavior of people searching for a software problem that wasn't there. He recognized it because he had seen it before, and because the particular combination of symptoms on that panel, total unresponsiveness with clean diagnostics, pointed very specifically to a category of fault that no software tool would ever find. He kept sweeping. The head engineer, a man named Carter, who ran the marina's in-house maintenance team, finally said aloud what several of them had been thinking, that the only remaining option was a full system reboot, requiring a complete shutdown of primary and secondary power to the entire vessel. The process would take approximately 40 minutes to complete safely, and 45 to be conservative. The guests arrived in 47. It was in the silence after Carter's suggestion that Daniel stopped sweeping. He stood still for a moment, listening carefully to the only mechanical process still running aboard the Meridian, the ventilation system, sustained by its own separate emergency circuit. He heard it. A faint, irregular tick, slightly faster than the ventilation cycle, the kind of sound that did not appear on any diagnostic readout because it was not electrical in origin. It was physical, a relay contact slightly off its seating, closing and opening a fraction of a millimeter short of full engagement. Not enough to trip an error code, enough to prevent the supervisory control signal from completing the circuit it needed in order to initialize. He repositioned himself a few feet closer along the dock, appearing to continue his work. He listened again. The tick was consistent. He knew exactly where the relay bank for that class of retrofit was located. Behind a secondary access panel on the starboard interior bulkhead, low and behind the main electrical conduit routing, in a space that no service technician would check first because it did not appear on the primary maintenance schematic. It was the kind of location that revealed itself only to someone who had worked on that specific generation of retrofit architecture, who knew where the corners had been cut in the original installation documentation. He stood there for a moment, weighing things. He could say nothing. Carter would proceed with the full reboot. The guests would arrive to a dark vessel. The contract, $140 million, eight months of work would be in serious jeopardy. That was not his problem. He had a job, and his job was the dock. And the dock was clean. He had made a quiet life in this quiet place, and the central feature of that life was that he did not draw attention to himself. He had learned at considerable cost what attention could do to a person. He heard Carter say, "I'll pull the power sequence sheet." Daniel set his broom against the nearest cleat and walked toward the gangway. "Maybe a mechanical contact fault," he said to the nearest engineer, his voice low enough that only two people heard it. "Not a software issue." Carter turned. He looked at Daniel's uniform, then at Daniel's face, and something shifted in his expression, not quite contempt, but the particular cousin of it that emerges when a credentialed professional is offered an opinion by someone they have categorized as a cleaning person. "This is a restricted work area," he said. "I can hear a relay cycling wrong," Daniel said. One of the manufacturer's technicians produced a short, involuntary laugh. Carter's expression settled into the blankness of someone who has decided not to engage. Victoria had heard the exchange from the salon entrance. She crossed the deck in six steps, heels precise on the teak, and looked at Daniel, really looked at him, possibly for the first time in two full years of shared proximity. What she saw was a man in a gray maintenance uniform, holding nothing, no tools, standing among a group of engineers as though he belonged there. "Stick to your job," she said. Her voice was not loud. It did not need to be. Daniel held her gaze for a moment. Then he gave a single, small nod and turned back toward the gangway. He was three steps down the dock when he became aware that Chloe was watching him. She had been sitting on her crate near the supply shed, homework open in her lap, silent throughout all of it. When her father turned to walk away, she met his eyes. The look on her face was the kind that 6-year-olds produce without intending to, unguarded, wondering, quietly disappointed in the way that only someone who believes in you completely can be disappointed. Daniel stopped. He stood on the dock for 4 seconds. He counted them. Then he turned around. He went back up the gangway, moved through the cluster of engineers without making eye contact, and went directly to the starboard interior bulkhead. He had the secondary access panel open in 15 seconds. Behind the conduit routing, exactly where he knew it would be, was the relay bank, a row of 12 mechanical switches. The third from the left, he could see the misalignment without a measuring tool. The contact plate had shifted a quarter of a millimeter off its seating, barely perceptible, entirely consequential. The fault develops when a vessel pitches in certain sea conditions, and the installation torque on the relay housing is 2 ft lb short of specification. The relay had probably been marginal since the day it was installed. Daniel reached into his breast pocket and removed the small paper clip he had carried there for 12 years, out of a habit he had never quite broken. He used them to tag entries on the dock maintenance clipboard and had simply kept one on his person out of the same quiet consistency that defined everything else about how he moved through the world. He straightened one end, slid it through the relay housing access port, and applied a light lateral pressure, not enough to force anything, just enough to guide the contact plate back to its correct seating position. He felt it settle. A small, definitive click. He waited. For 30 seconds, nothing happened. The panel remained dark. The engineers behind him were silent in the particular way of people who do not understand what they are witnessing and have not yet decided whether to be skeptical or alarmed. Then the Meridian supervisory system initialized. The panel came on in stages, status lights first, then the primary display cycling through its boot sequence, then the secondary systems following in order, navigation, climate, communications, propulsion monitoring. The salon lighting shifted from emergency yellow back to its normal warm gold. The engines, suspended since the failure, returned at idle with a low, steady hum that traveled through the hull and up through the deck into the soles of everyone standing on it. The yacht was alive. Daniel closed the access panel, stood, straightened his jacket, and walked back through the group without looking at anyone. He retrieved his broom from the cleat, moved to the next section of dock, and resumed working. No one spoke for a long moment. Then Carter said something quiet to one of the manufacturer's technicians, and the technician responded using the phrase contact fault in the tone of a man explaining something he had naturally been weighing throughout. Victoria had not moved. She was looking at the access panel Daniel had just closed, then at the dock where he had returned to his broom. Something had shifted in the set of her jaw and the stillness of her hands, and she was working to contain it. She was very good at containing things. She was not entirely good enough. 39 minutes before the consortium arrived, Chloe came running up the dock and wrapped both arms around Daniel's waist. She said, just quietly enough for the two of them, "I knew. Ba, I always knew." He held the back of her head with one hand and said nothing. Victoria watched this from the stern rail. The expression on her face, in that unguarded moment, was not an expression that belonged to a CEO. That evening, after the consortium's representatives had arrived, signed, and departed, the $140 million contract secured without any awareness on their part of how close the evening had come to failure. Victoria asked her operations manager to pull Daniel Brooks's personnel file. What he brought her was thin. Marina employment records, a standard background check covering the previous 2 years of verified work history. Prior to the Marina, the file was effectively blank, not in the way that a quiet life leaves no trace, but in the way that a person's record appears when someone has been deliberate about what remains visible. Victoria made two phone calls the following morning, framing each question in the careful way she had developed for inquiries she wanted answered honestly rather than confirmed. By noon, she had what she needed. Daniel Brooks, licensed marine systems engineer, certification lapsed. Previously employed by Coastal Marine Technologies for 7 years. Three formal commendations in his file during that period. A documented history of complex diagnostic work on integrated control systems, including the exact class of retrofit architecture installed aboard the Meridian. And then, 4 years ago, an incident, a mechanical failure aboard a vessel during a client demonstration. An investigation. A findings report naming Daniel Brooks as the engineer of record responsible for the faulty installation. He had accepted the finding, signed the acknowledgement, surrendered his active certification without contesting the process. The other engineer on that project had been a man named Gerald Holt. Gerald Holt was currently employed as senior technical director at a competing marine engineering firm. He had delivered a keynote address at a regional maritime conference 11 months ago. His certification was current. His record was clean. Victoria sat with this for a long time. She had a particular relationship with facts. She trusted them absolutely, which also meant trusting the discomfort of facts that complicated pictures she had already assembled. This set of facts was deeply uncomfortable. She knew, without requiring further documentation, what the actual shape of that situation had been. She had seen the pattern before, in other industries and other contexts. A junior engineer absorbs a finding that belonged to someone senior. He has a newborn. He has no appetite for a prolonged fight. He signs. He disappears into a gray uniform somewhere quiet, and he does not complain, and he does not explain, and years accumulate. She thought about what she had said to him the evening before, in front of the engineers and the dock and his daughter sitting on her crate. "Stick to your job." She put down the file. She did not have a comfortable category for what she was currently feeling, and that was itself a disorienting experience. Two days later, she found him at the dock before anyone else had arrived. He was hosing down the gangway of a 40-ft sailboat, unhurried, not looking up. "I want to offer you a position," she said. "Not maintenance. I need a consultant, someone who understands integrated marine control systems at the level you clearly do. The Meridian's retrofit has serviceability problems I've spent a day and a half beginning to document. There are likely more. I want someone to go through the vessel properly and give me an honest technical assessment. She paused. I'm also in conversations about acquiring two additional vessels in the next 18 months. I'd want informed input before finalizing those purchase agreements. He listened without interrupting. When she finished, the silence was measured. I appreciate that, he said. But I spent years building something in that field. And when it mattered, the field made very clear what it thought I was worth. I don't have any desire to return to a version of that. He reached for the hose. The engineer who filed that report, Victoria said, was Gerald Holt. Daniel went completely still. I've reviewed the maintenance log sequence from that incident, she continued. I'm not going to pretend I can undo what happened. And I'm not offering this as compensation for something I had no part in. I'm offering it because you are the most technically capable person I've encountered in a very long time. That capability is going to waste. And I find that difficult to simply accept. The silence stretched. From somewhere down the dock came the sound of a pelican landing on a mooring post. Water against the sailboat's hull. A line of morning light traveling slowly across the teak. Daniel did not answer. He set down the hose and walked to the end of the dock, where he stood with his hands in his pockets and looked at the water for a while. That evening, Chloe sat beside him at the kitchen table while he looked over the marina's maintenance schedule, a habit, something to do with his hands. She was drawing on the back of a permission slip from school. She did not look up when she said, "That woman came to talk to you today." "She did," Daniel said. A moment passed. "You didn't say yes." "No." She looked up then with the particular directness that was entirely her own. "Are you scared of going back?" she asked, "or did you already give up on it?" He looked at her for a long time. He did not answer that night. Three weeks later, Victoria was midway through a presentation to a group of port authority representatives when her operations manager appeared in the conference room doorway. She excused herself. The Meridian had been moved two days earlier for routine bottom cleaning. When the cleaning crew restored the vessel to its slip and reestablished power, something in the restoration sequence triggered a cascade fault in the primary electrical distribution board. A small fire had started in the electrical compartment. The suppression system activated correctly, no injuries, no structural damage to the hull, but the distribution board itself was destroyed. She stood in the hallway and did the arithmetic she did not want to do. The port authority meeting was concluding a 6-month process that, if successful, would allow Hayes Maritime to expand into two additional terminals, the largest growth initiative the company had undertaken in a decade. The consortium representatives who had signed 3 weeks ago were due for an onboarding tour aboard the Meridian in 4 days. The manufacturer's regional office had confirmed they could have a technician on site in 72 hours. 72 hours was not 4 days. She put the phone in her jacket pocket and walked out through the building's side entrance. Daniel was on dock seven replacing the rubber gasket on a dock cleat fitting, not technically his assignment, but the kind of small repair he addressed when he noticed it needed doing because that was how he worked and always had been. She did not approach him the way she had before. She walked up and stopped and said, "I need help." Not as a client or an employer, as someone who is out of options and doesn't know who else to call. He looked at her. She was not performing anything. That was different. The Meridian's primary distribution board failed. I have 4 days before a critical client tour and 72 hours before the manufacturer sends a technician, which is too late. The pause that followed was brief but visible. I'm not asking you to go back to anything. I'm asking you to come look at a problem. Silence on the dock. Water, wind, the distant sound of rigging. Daniel set down the cleat fitting. He thought about Chloe at the kitchen table, permission slip in hand. The difference between being afraid and having surrendered. "Show me the vessel," he said. What followed over the next 31 hours was not dramatic in any conventional sense. It was methodical. Daniel worked through the Meridian's electrical architecture with the kind of focus that requires not just technical knowledge, but a particular ship with complexity, the patience to hold an entire system in your mind simultaneously and find the path through it that others miss because they are reading the schematic instead of listening to the machine. He located the cascade fault's origin point in the first 2 hours. He identified two contributing factors that the cleaning crew's restoration procedure had inadvertently introduced procedural gaps that were not in any written guideline because no one had considered the interaction. He sourced replacement components through a supplier network that did not appear in Hayes Maritime's vendor directory because no one at Hayes Maritime had worked with anyone who knew it existed. He had the distribution board rebuilt and independently tested in 31 hours, 3 hours before the manufacturer's technician arrived. Victoria was present for much of it. She had learned, watching him work 2 years ago on a dock she had never thought to consider, that the correct way to be present for someone like Daniel was to stay close enough to understand what was happening and quiet enough not to interrupt it. She watched the way he moved through the problem, quiet, systematic, confident without any need to announce the confidence. She watched him stop midway through a diagnostic to explain a finding to one of Carter's junior staff members, not because the explanation was required, but because the junior staff member was curious and Daniel saw no reason not to answer. He spoke as if the knowledge belonged to anyone willing to pay attention, as if there were no particular value in keeping it close. On the morning of the second day, she brought coffee down to the engine compartment. He accepted it without looking up. "The incident report," she said, "I referred the inconsistency in the maintenance log sequence to the licensing authority. I don't know what they'll determine, but the record is there now." He set down his tool. He looked at her for a moment. Then he looked back at the panel. "Thank you," he said. When the port authority representatives arrived for their tour, the Meridian was fully operational. Victoria walked them through the main deck and the salon, and at a certain point without advance notice to her operations manager, who would later describe the moment as professionally alarming, she stopped her prepared remarks and introduced the man standing at the helm console completing a final navigation calibration. "This is Daniel Brooks," she said in front of seven port authority officials and her own senior staff. He has worked with this vessel's systems more effectively than any engineer I have formally employed. He is the reason this tour is taking place today and the reason these vessels will continue to operate the way they should." "His exact title is something we are still working out together." Daniel did not look up from the console for a moment. Then he did. The port authority representatives received this with the mild, courteous interest of people who had not been aware there was a subtext. Carter, near the gangway, studied the teak with careful attention. After the officials departed, Daniel came to stand beside Victoria at the stern rail. The water behind the marina was green and still. He had the paper clip in his hand. He had been carrying it in the same breast pocket for 3 weeks without quite deciding why. He set it on the rail between them. Victoria looked at it. She picked it up and turned it once in her fingers. "I should have proper instruments ordered for you," she said. "I already have tools," he said. She set the paper clip back on the rail. The title question. "I meant what I said. Whatever structure works. Consulting arrangement, advisory role, something else. I'm not interested in a category that doesn't fit." He considered this. The water moved against the hull. From below the gangway came the sound of and small footsteps crossing the dock and a homework folder unzipped with the characteristic decisiveness of someone who had done this 400 times. "Let's start with the assessment work," he said. "The Meridian first. Then we'll see where it goes." Victoria nodded. There was more that could have been said, she was aware of that, and so was he, but neither of them was in a hurry. There had been 2 years of quiet patience on one side and considerably more years of controlled certainty on the other. And the space between those two things was not going to be resolved in a single afternoon on a stern rail. It would take the form of early mornings and problems approached honestly and small, unceremonious gestures, and it would take whatever time it required. Chloe appeared at the stern rail before either of them said anything further. She positioned herself between her father and the woman standing next to him, looked at the paper clip on the rail, and without preamble picked it up and turned it over in her fingers the same way Victoria had slowly, with assessment. "This is what fixed the whole boat?" she asked. "This helped," Daniel said. Chloe studied the paper clip with the grave seriousness of someone conducting a formal evaluation. Then she set it carefully back on the rail. "It's very small," she said, not as a criticism, as a matter that required acknowledging. Victoria looked at the paper clip, then at Chloe, then, briefly, at Daniel. "Sometimes," she said, "the smallest things are the ones that fix what everything else missed." The sun was going down over the water. The Meridian rocked gently in its slip, fully alive, its systems running cleanly and without interruption. The dock was quiet in the way it became at the end of the day, when the water settled and the rigging stopped moving, and the whole marina seemed, at last, to exhale. The paper clip sat on the stern rail between them, catching the last of the light.

- Ocean Cleanup Systems: Restoring Our Oceans | InnoVision Tech: Kind: captions Language: en ocean cleanup systems restoring our oceans the Great Pacific Garbage Patch is a stark example of the plastic pollution problem with over 5 trillion pieces of plastic floating in our oceans this pollution poses a serious threat to marine life and ecosystems to combat this Innovative ocean cleanup systems are being developed one such project the ocean cleanup uses floating barriers to collect plastic from the ocean surface while allowing marine life to pass safely underneath these systems work autonomously Guided by ocean currents to areas with high plastic concentrations the ocean cleanup operates on a simple principle passive collection u-shaped barriers are placed in areas like the Great Pacific Garbage Patch where currents Corral plastic into the system's Center for easy collection the systems are energy efficient requiring no external power once collected the plastic is transported to land where it is sorted recycled or repurposed into new products helping reduce overall plastic waste A major advantage of these systems is their scalability they can be deployed in large garbage patches and Nearshore areas where plastic is more concentrated smaller versions can be used in rivers to intercept plastic before it reaches the ocean with global deployment ocean cleanup systems could remove 90% of ocean plastic by 2040 restoring Marine environments and protecting biodiversity however cleanup alone isn't enough preventing new plastic from entering the oceans is equally important requiring Global efforts to reduce plastic production improve waste management and boost recycling combined with ocean cleanup Technologies these efforts can lead to cleaner oceans and a healthier planet

are nothing new and they were invented back in the 1800s they have been used in a lot of industrial applications but they are also known to have a lot of vibration issues and obviously they are very noisy reluctance basically means the resistance to the magnetic flux so the current is constantly switching and each rotor pole is pulled towards the approaching stator pole now this motor is definitely reliable but it's very tricky to switch the current at correct time intervals so turn chide has revised this by increasing the number of rotor pulls and creating a sensorless algorithm this allows the current in the coils to be switched at 20 000 times a second thus increasing efficiency now this variant is relatively new so it's going to be interesting to see the power to weight ratio and whether or not it can actually branch outside of industrial use so the question still remains on what will be the dominant motor design for electrical vehicles in the future the majority of motor designs which i have talked about earlier are radial flux but a newer type of design will likely take over the high-end market the yokeless actual flux motor is only developed by a few companies which includes the magnax motor they have achieved 16 kilowatts per kilogram which is over three times the power density of a tesla model 3 radial motor the actual flux design has been known for some time and it has a shorter direct flux path therefore it has a stronger magnetic which is why the motors have a really good power to weight ratio now the actual flux is a pretty challenging motor to build but magnax has claimed to solve the production issues ultimately their motors should be out on the market in just a few years now another neat development which is worth checking out is the neodym magnetic transmission they have shown some really neat demos of their 160 newton meter gearbox obviously it's not 100 percent efficient but it can handle 10 000 rpm with very little noise and friction now this type of design could particularly be useful for wind power or even electric bolts which brings us to the number one position so probably one of the most unique designs out there is the deep speed hydro jet this weird open-ended hydro jet is an all-electric propulsion system which can be pretty much mounted like an outboard motor or as an independent 360 degree pod which can provide reverse or sideways thrust there are no gears shafts or universal joints only a pair of wires running from the batteries to the hydro jet now obviously you have to consider the weight of the batteries but the base model only weighs 75 kilograms so it can be considerably lighter for short range applications when compared to combustion engines now the rear design is kind of like a turbo fan and it funnels water into the front and accelerates it out the back meaning it's highly efficient and it accelerates faster than a propeller the company is working on multiple variants ranging from 100 to   2. @anonymous-dk1wb [2 likes]: The community that my sailboat is hooked at has a wonderful response community last month a boater was away when seacock hose failed and cracked under pressure someone noticed the boat was sitting low so everyone just springed into anction one person located the new hose and two others who had generator power and high suction hose got there started pumping while the others replaced the hose line and even after that myself and one other was at work so when we got off went down with fresh water and soap and started drying and diluting down the salt drying out any wet items i had a land spot to wash there clothes and everything went as calmly and organized as possible and i trust the community out there to do the same if anything happened while i was away.   3. @donskinner9430 [1 likes]: I guess what can be gleaned from the near sinking or sickening panic... is to know where ALL factory holes are and be able to access them quickly and have bungs around in several locations. be aware in choppy conditions .... there can be structural damage as well ... I have heard of guys crashing into God knows what in high seas and not knowing until they can inspect the hull.  it is good to run the motor as well the heat will help dry things out and get moisture out of the motor too..... Glad you are ok.... Thank you for sharing...
  4. @lelandparker3507 [1 likes]: Definitely a near-miss with deep lessons.  Zingaro suffered the same failure of the speed wheel blowing out but I don't know if they were beating through waves or not.

Two tips...
1. you can place a piece of sail cloth, vinyl, or canvas against the exterior of the hull at the hole to dramatically reduce water ingress while the hole is open.  Of course, this means someone has to be under the hull, but a large piece of coarse, water-resistant fabric will cut water flow to a fraction like 1 to 3% compared to the open hole.
2. If possible, leave the wooden bung in.  It will swell and seal way better than any adhesive applied in a submerged environment.  Adhering the bung gives extra peace of mind, considering it will be hard to sleep well for a while even under the best of conditions.

SO GLAD you guys caught it in time and THANK YOU for documenting the incident so thoroughly.  You've probably already saved some lives with the lessons so graphically illustrated here and will continue in the future.

QUESTION: How deep was Lady K wallowing in the water relative to where wave action would start washing into the cockpit?
  5. @mitchpender8113 [1 likes]: Actually dude you are better off running a solar controller for each panel  so 2 30a controllers  having seperate controllers increases the charge current substantially

Larger animals such as hammerheads, oceanic white tips, and tiger sharks are disappearing fast. There's one place in the world where sharks still flourish. The Bahamas. In the crystalline waters of this island nation, sharks are highly protected. It's illegal to kill them. Stuart Cole's great passion is sharks. And I I think that's what they do with us because we always find them sneaking up on the divers. They come [music] from behind. >> I'm going to watch your back. >> Okay. >> You [music] watch mine, too, though, cuz they're always sneaking up behind. I'm not getting any younger, but diving with these [music] sharks, man, it's the best thing I can do. I I never get bored of it. the Tiger Beach experience. We had a spectacular day. The water was flat, calm. As soon as we got to location, we had two large lemon sharks. Stu set the uh box up at the bottom, started gently putting the bait into the water. One big tiger, female I think, came along and then uh a few more lemons came along. I said, "This is pretty easy. I'm doing great." And >> within 10 minutes, we had a big tiger shark and we started to interact with those three sharks. When we get in the water with the tigers and the lemon sharks, obviously, we draw them to us with a bait box and we take bait out of the box and we feed it to the sharks. Uh it's very controversial, but you're not going to get the kind of uh interaction and close encounters [music] without using food and chum and baiting in these sharks. We bait them in so close that we can actually touch them and rub them and they seem to enjoy it. All of a sudden, two tigers, three tigers, four tigers, 15, 20 lemons, and I'm going, gee, this could look really good. And then they start coming two feet off the bottom and they're like they're coming at my feet. So I've got the camera right there face following them and then they're crossing each other right in front of right in front of my feet. I going this is okay right still. Oh yeah. This is great Jim. Just keep going. You're doing great. And then after a while I'm spinning like a top trying to keep an eye on these things. So uh it goes from controlled shooting to totally uncontrolled shooting. You're trying to watch a tiger shark and there's one over here, one over there, and all of a sudden there's one going between your legs from behind. Fantastic. A solitary, mostly nocturnal predator, tiger sharks are second only to great whites for the most recorded attacks on humans. They often roam [music] close to shore, creating the potential for encounters with swimmers, surfers, and divers. Unlike [music] great whites that mostly prey on seals and sea lions, tiger sharks will eat virtually anything, including us. Steuart Cove is certainly well acquainted with sharks and doesn't have any qualms about feeding them, but handing out pieces of fish to [music] 10 to 15 ft tiger sharks without chain mail or the protection of a cage is a bit extreme for some people. These animals are habituated to the presence of divers [music] and used to being fed. But the small scraps of food they receive are a tiny fraction of their daily requirements. It's more like handing out treats to really big dogs. Dogs that can bite you in half. >> That was unbelievable. I've done several dives out here over the years and that there was to me the best. >> But that was a fantastic dive. And I didn't really see any super aggressiveness down there. And you were touching them and and uh you seen them moving them along a little bit like big dogs. You get your share and they weren't pushing and shoving. They kind of lining up for their food. What got them wrapped up there near the end was there was a big piece of wahoo carcass which is very bloody and I took it out and I was trying to give it to the tiger shark but the lemon shark grabbed hold of it and went taken off and shook it and the blood went everywhere and you could see everything changed. The all the lemon sharks went after them. The tiger sharks kind of arched their backs a little bit and at that point I think I felt a little bit uncomfortable but most of the dive with those sharks all around me. I mean, they're around my legs, on my feet. Had no protection on my hands, no chain mail on my body, but I didn't at any time really feel in danger. Wow. I just love my job. For a fish with such a vicious reputation, sharks in the Bahamas make a disarming first impression. They're not exactly cute or cuddly, but sharks here simply go about their business, as most predators do when left undisturbed. We're generally not on their menu, and they view us as just another fish on the reef. Tourism accounts for nearly half the gross national [music] product of the Bahamas. And diving is a multi-million dollar industry with sharks an everinccreasing draw. It's abundantly clear that a live shark here is worth far more than a dead one. The Bahamas is one of only five small nations that have completely banned commercial shark fishing. Multinational fleets are not welcome in these waters. It really was a big deal that a small country like the Bahamas, you know, joining other small countries took on the giants and, you know, determined that this was something that had to be done. We are very optimistic that we're going to be able to influence the larger countries and get them to see why it's important to protect sharks. So, I think the future is bright [music] and as a small country, we really feel proud that we stepped up to the plate and we salute the government. You know, here in the Bonds, we're the leaders in shark conservation. We realize how valuable a shark is, not only to the economy, but to the environment. And hopefully other countries have realized that a live shark is going to keep their waters healthy, keep their fish population strong. [music] A dead shark isn't going to do much good. It's only good for them one time. I am very proud to be a Bahamian and proud of our country and the stance that it has taken in conservation and protecting sharks. It's estimated that a single live shark is worth as much as $200,000 in tourism revenue over its lifetime. [music] Its ecological value is inestimable. Studies in the Bahamas and the Caribbean [music] have shown that where sharks are keystone species, their depletion could ultimately destroy coral reefs. Their role in the food chain is crucial. If the sharks are gone, so too goes a rich ecosystem, [music] one that feeds local people and keeps visitors coming back. I've been blessed [music] to grow up with all this healthy marine environment here in the Bahamas. Lots of sharks with oceanic white tips, hammerheads, [music] reef sharks, tiger I hope that they're here for my grandchildren, my great-grandchildren, for many generations beyond that, that they can enjoy this wonderful resource. And I am very optimistic [music] that many of these species that are now threatened are going to be able to bounce back and that human beings are going to understand how important it is to live in balance with these very important creatures that inhabit our waters. Heat. Keep going.

Also. I would have made a wider repair area. Wider & Longer.  Reason being. The repair is a lot stronger making the first piece have more area to bond. Remember it’s your first piece that bonds the best with really cleaning it good with Acetone more than one time. For my second piece reducing each layer  a couple inches to increase the overlap area to stagger and tie the layers together While building up low spots the same to put back missing material with the first layer of chop strand being a piece as long and wide as the total prepared area. To be stronger as you add more pieces in layers to add several inches in each direction to increase strength also more layers turned in different directions crisscrossing each layer.  I’ll even pull and tear the chop strand for added strength. No cuts on the chop strand. Tearing it is better.
