- Recovery from a BLACKOUT in Engine room: Kind: captions Language: en an unexpected loss of electrical power a blackout is a very unusual event particularly for anyone down in the engine room and particularly if you've never experienced it before it suddenly becomes very dark and relatively quiet although some auxiliary diesel engines might still be running technology should guard us from such events we have power management systems pms preferential tripping of non-essential machinery and we often have sequential start systems to help us get going again automatically however there is a danger that we might have become so used to the system taking care of us automatically that we've become unfamiliar with how to recover from a blackout when a blackout occurs stay calm and reach for the procedures remember that the emergency generator should start and give you essential services back within 45 seconds in the meantime battery should keep your alarm and monitoring systems working as soon as you can inform the officer of the watch on the bridge of the general situation giving as much accurate information as you can if the engineers alarm has not already sounded operate it now and call the chief engineer always remembering that they might well be on their way to the engine room by now in most cases loss of electrically driven lubricating oil fresh water and sea water pumps will have stopped the main engine but bring the control lever back to zero anyway ready for an orderly restart if the auxiliary boiler was running shut the main steam stop valve to maintain the steam pressure once manpower is available it might be a good idea to close off the steam to non-essential services such as tank heating etc this will make it quicker to re-establish steam to the fuel oil heaters necessary to restore electrical generation and propulsion try and make sure everyone logs such actions so that everything can be restored to normal later on obviously we should try and find out the reason for the blackout and there might be some evidence of this in the data logger find out the reason for the blackout and fix it if you can or isolate any defective equipment that caused the blackout by now reading this list you should be thinking that a there's a lot to do and b we need to go about it in an organized fashion so you really need to work from procedures and there should be management of the situation with specific tasks delegated to individuals now we can think about restoring power let's go back to basics in order to do this as a minimum we'll need starting air fuel and to prime the lubricating system of the diesel engine driving the main generator now a lot depends on what ship you're on and what situation you're in in a blackout the fuel oil feed might only be via gravity from a pencil tank the cooling water pumps might be driven by electrical motors in which case you need power and maybe only particular ones can be fed from the emergency switchboard the emergency generator might give you all the power you need to start a main generator but then you might only have a limited amount of time to start sea water and fresh water pumps necessary to provide cooling for this main generator there are so many variables that the only way to ensure that this is all done in the correct sequence is to develop a procedure beforehand have it ready for emergencies and use it so know which main generator can be started in a blackout and how to do this consider if you can still start them on heavy fuel oil because you may or may not have enough residual heat and steam to do this change over to distillate fuel if in doubt once you get it started restore power to the main switchboard and make sure you start any additional cooling pumps needed before it overheats and get the boiler going again to restore steam get the lubricating oil and fresh water pumps running on the main engine as soon as you can and get the main engine fuel oil system back into operation keep an eye on the load on this main generator you've just started and start another main generator if needed keep the bridge updated on progress you should have a better idea of when normal service will be resumed by now we need to keep in mind that on most but not all ships we can't run the main and emergency generators in parallel we can often synchronize them but there are usually two circuit breakers arranged so that only one of them can be closed at any given time this means we can feed the emergency switchboard either from the main switchboard or the emergency generator what we can't normally do is back feed the main switchboard from the emergency so now we will usually be in a situation where we have the emergency switchboard fed by the emergency generator and the main switchboard fed by the main however the two are not connected keep this in mind at some point we need to reconnect the main and emergency switchboards which means to restore the feed to the emergency switchboard from the main of course we should be used to this routine from testing the emergency generator on load every week if we can synchronize them great if we can't then keep in mind that there might be a momentary loss of supplies to anything fed from the emergency so know what this affects since it could potentially cause another blackout for instance if it is supplying power to the main generator fuel pump essential navigation equipment is normally kept supplied using individual uninterruptible power supplies ups during this changeover so make sure that you don't forget to restore the feed from the main switchboard to the emergency switchboard and to stop the emergency generator there have been many occasions after a blackout where the emergency generator has been left running make sure all controls are set back to the auto position ready for the next time now that we have enough power reset breakers and start all the other required machinery and systems for example tank heating this includes breakers in the preferential tripping sequence non-essential machinery with modern power management systems an unexpected loss of power is less common than it once was but that is exactly why it might catch us unprepared if we do nothing so use this learning session to think about your own ship there about to be differences with the general procedures described here have a look at your own procedures check that you understand them and review them to make sure they are up to date

- 5 Essential Tips Before Buying a Liveaboard Boat | Avoid Costly Mistakes!: Kind: captions Language: en So, you want to buy a boat and beat the crazy property market. If you find a boat you're interested in, get a survey done or find someone to have a look over it who knows something about boats. Don't do what I did. Go to a local yacht club and find someone at the bar who doesn't even own a boat to give you advice. My boat is a 1983 half tunner racing boat. It's cold molded, so made of wood, and that makes it now 42 years old. Number two, you're going to have to learn to fix everything. The word marine is a bit like the word wedding. You put it in front of anything and suddenly it becomes expensive. You're going to have to learn to become a mechanic, a shipwright, a plumber, an electrician, a rigger, and if you're lucky, you might actually do a bit of sailing. It's just a reality of boats. Uh the environment is very tough and they're always needing work. Number three, you have to become a minimalist. I'm usually on the boat if the winds are over 30 knots just to make sure it doesn't drag anchor. Number five, ports rot boats and men. If you can afford to spend more on your initial your first purchase of a boat, do that. Look for boats that have had the rigging done, the sails done, the wiring done, where someone else has done all that hard work because then you can just go out and enjoy the boat and and sail. Getting an old boat where you have to do a lot of that work yourself, it's going to be very draining on your wallet and if it's all new to you, possibly on your motivation. uh you fix one thing, you find two more problems. So, if you can try and find a boat that's had all that hard work done and it's ready to go. down to the shop pick up another sheet of plywood and fix it which is kind of nice i really wanted to go fossil fuel free when i bought the boat it had a functioning diesel engine in it and a lot of people were like why would you get rid of a functioning diesel engine the decision to go electric did have something to do with the environment the solar panels have gotten that much more potent the lithium batteries have really dropped in price now is the time it's exciting and i just want to be part of that but i also think it's about convenience you know with a boat like this if i do decide to do long crossings eventually i now can be motoring every day and i don't have to be limited by a tank in order to get the surface area necessary to make enough solar power to move a boat consistently you really need a multi-hull so i was looking at trimarans catamarans so i have 16 330 watt canadian made solar panels the boat has 5280 watts of solar which is a lot for a household or for a regular boat you know i'm able to run ovens and induction cook plates and a washing machine and water makers and just anything i want but once i start moving it's not actually that much power it's only about seven or eight horsepower which doesn't exactly move about very quickly so if you want to go any faster than three and a half four knots you're gonna have to start using the batteries i have just short of 20 kilowatt hours worth of battery and i'm using one hp ev ac 12 kilowatt motor so i do want to stress this is a sailboat there will be sales eventually i'm just not there yet but as it stands right now i'm making enough power in the summer time to lift anchor in the morning and travel 50 kilometers across the georgia strait to vancouver and arrive well before sunset and not have touched my batteries at all so that's just off the power coming in on the panels so i really like having this level of solar power and that's the real game changer is is having the low resistance in the water of a catamaran and having the high power of all that solar there are some downsides to solar it is weather dependent for instance if it's raining you're not making much solar power but usually with rain comes wind and i'm putting up some wind turbines in fact i'm putting up the same amount as i have solar on the roof so hopefully those two will balance each other out well and hopefully i don't have any very calm very cloudy days i really designed this boat for me and i'm a big foodie i love to cook so the galley takes up a huge amount of space in the middle of the boat and everything else is a much smaller area my city is just a little cubby hole in the corner my cockpit at the back is not even built yet and i have four berths and all each one of them are little nooks that you tuck into get your own privacy but they're just there for sleeping and relaxing so i'm on gabriela island in the southern gulf i am only on this island because i bought the boat here and this community just sucked me in this is one of the most tropical locations in all of canada which is a bit of an oxymoron i suppose but it is a beautiful place and it's a great learning ground as a new sailor to start getting out and getting around like there's lots of little tiny islands to explore and there's current to learn about and there's seasonal storms to learn about my boat is on a mooring ball not anchor so there's less to worry about there because it is very secure it has a huge concrete block and really heavy chain that holds my boat in place so i'm not really worried about my boat breaking loose but that doesn't mean somebody else's boat might not break loose and i'm downwind on a lot of these storms from most of the other boats in the bay so there's still a lot to watch out for so on anchor you don't pay any rent on a mooring ball while you have to own a mooring ball but you know all things considered it's relatively cheap it's only when you take your boat to the dog that it really starts racking up a bill to heat my boat i've built my own stainless wood stove but i'm hoping to transition more to electric powered heat and building my own heat pump because it's more convenient i can just flick it on and set it to a temperature and i don't have to stock a wood stove all the time and keep wood on board as much in the winter time around here it gets quite dark this is pretty far north and so i don't actually make that much solar power in the wintertime to heat the boat off that will be challenging so really it will rely on the wind turbines to fill in that gap because there's a lot more wind in the winter and i make my own fresh water i have a water maker and with all this power it's easy to make fresh water at first when i got out here i told people i'd be sailing in six months i didn't know anything didn't know what i was doing the greatest thing that this boat has taught me is just all of the skills i've learned along the way you know i'm mentoring under a sail maker i'm learning to tig weld i'm learning to machine i've done so much woodworking and boat building and i'm working at the shipyard doing fiberglass work and engine work all the time and there's so many skills and when you're living on a boat you don't always have access to a shop that you can just walk down and hand your problem to so you got to fix it yourself and i really appreciate all of those skills that i've learned sometimes not always the easy way [Laughter] people always ask me where i want to take the boat first and i don't really know because i don't know what the boats would be capable of everything i'm doing hasn't really been tested out in the wild yet and so if it ends up that the boat can only do these southern gulf islands while there's worse places i could be stuck not a big deal i still have a four bedroom home on the water that's all fossil fuel free which is amazing but i think that these technologies and the things i'm doing are going to let this boat go some great distances i mean on paper this boat is lighter better powered with a higher bridge deck and lower cabin top than a laguna and those things cost half a million bucks so i think i'm doing great so a lot of people get into boating with a great big wad of cash i didn't have that option um so i bought a cheap boat and i've been buying cheap broken secondhand things fixing them myself and that's been the main thing that's kept the price down for me by the time this boat is done it should have spent roughly 45 000 canadian dollars and about just short of five years of my life building this boat but i've learned a lot i've had a place to live the whole time i'm living in paradise i mean all in all not a bad way to go i'm trying to focus on getting my boat done but i also work at the shipyard here and i work at the food bank regularly i also have a youtube channel finding simon i've been documenting this entire build i think i'm 200 episodes in or something like that and i also do some diving work now and some welding work and boat repair work i'm installing solar panels on boats regularly there are a lot of things people expect to be difficult about living on a boat like having to carry or make your own water having to make or carry your own electricity somehow carrying fuel out food out getting on board the boat like if you're on a mooring ball traveling back and forth from the beach is the worst and from a dock is better but like that's just a lot of extra time every time you forget your wallet on the counter and have to double back you know it's a lot of work and if you don't have an outboard and you're rowing like that's even more work there's a lot about living on a boat that's waiting for tide and waiting for current waiting for the right weather window teaches you a lot of patience teaches you a lot of zen calm down and relax and there's a lot of reason to calm down and relax i'm in a very beautiful place but you know Wrap copper pipe AROUND flue. (Only if and where flue is hottest.)
Feed copper pipe into elevated 5 gallon aluminum foodsafe- metal, covered tub. The copper coils will draw the water FROM the rub, around through the coils, and present you with hot water as needed...Yankee Ingenuity! 😅😉
You'll need a pressure release valve, and a petcock...Yes, I said it. PET COCK! 🤣 To release the pent up pressure...you cheeky monkey!! 🤣🤣

- How to repair and service a watermaker on a sailboat. Sailing Q&A 4: Kind: captions Language: en this is from David Duncan wondering what kind of water maker you have sounds great that you only draw 8 amps and get that amount of water which is 35 l so which water maker is it so we've had a water maker on board esper for the last 8 years now and we opted for the shanka 35 modular it's run pretty well although it did run into a few problems which shanka have since addressed uh more on that in a moment first of all what is a water maker and how does it work so in a nutshell a water maker takes salt water and it converts it to freshwat and it does this by putting the salt water under extremely high pressure about 800 PSI or 55 bar and by pushing this through a very very small hold membrane it allows the water molecules to get through the holes but it manages to hold back grit dirt bacteria and viruses so only fresh water is allowed through the membrane as it's pushed through at extremely high pressure and the rest of the salt water the byproduct is just dispensed over the side of the boat so how is a Shanker water maker different to a normal water maker well I mentioned that you need that high pressure uh the 55 bar or 800 PSI and normally to attain that you need a high pressure pump to get that kind of pressure but the shanko water maker has this energy recovery free system so it takes in the water pressure to round about 5 bar puts it through the 5 micron filter and it then goes through this energy recovery system it actually increases the pressure hydraulically by about 10 times so it attains that pressure through its special uh system so what this means is is that we don't need a high press pump we only need the low pressure pump and the uh result of that of course is that you don't need as much power so our model runs at 8 amps and is able to produce 35 L of water um most other water makers to get that same uh amount of output per hour you normally have a high pressure pump which means more amps so water makers could be described as a necessary evil they're wonderful when they work I mean we produce so much water that we can make as much tea and coffee as we want and we can have showers and we can even do clothes washing as well the end dependence that it offers you is amazing second only to solar panels but the problem is of course like most things on boats they go wrong and when water makers go wrong they go properly wrong the problem we had with shanker's design was that it has this uh polypropylene uh block which allows the Piston to drive the water through and within that block in the original design there were around about 12 O-rings within the actual cavity itself and and of course over time these O-rings will disintegrate the problem is when they disintegrate you stop making fresh water and eventually the actual piston itself will stop moving through it now I'm happy to say that shanka have addressed this problem and they have their model 2.0 and they've done away with these 12 O-rings and they've used these four super strong uh thicker Rings inside that cavity which uh apparently called to Shanker were made with nanot technology don't ask anyway as you may know if - How A Watermaker Works (Explained in 5min) *SeaWater Pro Watermaker Installed On A Sailboat*: Kind: captions Language: en clean ocean water from below my water line is here at the through hole ready to be sucked in to begin the process that clean ocean water from outside now comes up through the through hole under here to a diverter valve this diverter valve now brings the sea water around and through a sea strainer this will strain the raw sea water and get out any debris as it's now clean enough to flow through a boost pump this boost pump must be below the water line and this will give pressure to the rest of the system we'll follow this line up through the wall up all the way under and into here goes through a one-way check valve going into my two pre-filters a 20 micron and then a 5 micron filter once it goes through that we'll see this little pressure gauge here which will let us know that it is correctly feeding the right amount of water into the system now that the raw ocean water has been cleaned enough it'll come down through here around and feed into the main pump this pump is a rather large one horsepower ac motor although they also come in dc motors as well this will pressurize the system up to 700 psi the required amount going through this high pressure line it then enters the membrane on this side pressurizing the outside of the membrane with sea water then water that does go through will be purified fresh water not allowing the salt to go through the membrane as it passes through on this side we get the collection of the different waters the brine water will go out this way and be ejected out the boat the product water will come up this way and go through this system this setup over here is very important this is what you use to adjust the amount of pressure and you want your psi again to be up at 700 this gauge will then tell us how much product water or fresh water is coming out through the system this monitor that is attached to that will tell us how many parts per million are on there the beginning of the product water comes through here to this diverter valve before the water reaches the correct amount of parts per million and still has some salinity to it it will go out here and follow the discharge of the brine water once the water is pure enough this switch will now be flipped and this water will go directly back into the tanks that's it that's how freshwater is made with a seawater pro water maker there are a couple of the functions that the water maker can do in order to keep the membrane clean you cannot just run it and then leave it indefinitely you have a couple of options first off you could run it every other day up to every third day that will push through any potential growth keeping the membrane and your pre-filters clean second you can run your own purified water through the whole system killing anything in here that would be living in the salt water and third you can make a pickling solution that will go through all of your system and just like the name suggests it will pickle it ensuring that no life can grow inside there a pickled water maker can be stored effectively indefinitely a water maker that has its fresh water run through it can be stored approximately a week and a non-cleaned water maker needs to be run every other to every third day to ensure there's no growth and doesn't harm the system another option to make fresh water is if you're at a marina but you don't want to actually suck up and use the sea water that's around you or move your boat you attach one of these into the system a carbon filter from the hose provided at the marina this will remove things like chlorine that would be detrimental to the system you would plug it into here and this then would go with a push to connect quick connect pex pipe that quick to connect would then go right here into another one way it would not be able to go down because of this other one way and they would then be running the purified water through your pre-filters like before getting into the correct psi feeding it to the boost pump going through the membrane and giving you your product water just like you would normally following this diagram instructions from seawater pro you can see just how easy this system is raw water comes in from the ocean goes to a sea strainer so it doesn't clog up your boost pump boost pump which is below your water line because it does not solve priming will then go through this this one-way valve here going into your two different pre-filters you'll then see you're at the right psi that can feed into your main pump this main pump will then pressurize the system up to 700 psi going into the system here this membrane that is doing the actual work of separating the salt water with the fresh water will literally filter out the sea water that's all it's doing salt water passes over the outside and any pressurized water that can which becomes your fresh water makes it through into the center the two different waters your product water and the brine water are then separated here at the end brine water going overboard being discharged fresh water once it's ready from the t-valve will then be diverted into your tanks i hope you found this helpful as a quick run through of how simple and easy these seawater pro water makers are to use if you have any questions feel free to reach out and i'll see if i can help

- How to make Water on a Sailboat (and how to Build your own Watermaker) - 4 years later review: Kind: captions Language: en over three years ago back in 2019 we realized that if we wanted to cruise the Mediterranean without having to rely on very expensive marinas for water we would need a water maker the problem is is that with Spectra water makers starting at seven thousand dollars an even cheaper brands starting at five thousand we simply did not have the budget to invest in popular off-the-shelf brands water makers are fairly simple systems and here's how they work essentially a water maker is a fancy name for a big filtration system it filters all the stuff that you do not want to drink out of the water such as salts and impurities a water maker does that in two steps the first one via a regular filtration system like the one you would find under your sink or in a Brita jug the second one with a reverse osmosis membrane reverse osmosis is the process by which salt water is pushed at very high pressure through a semi-permeable membrane to remove salt and all other kind of nasty stuff that you wouldn't want to drink here's how we make it work on a boat saltwater gets in the boat via a regular through haul which is first filtered through a sea water strainer that will remove the potential bigger crud that may come in with the sea water such as seaweed and shark poop the water then goes through a boost pump to push it to the next part of the system next we have a one-way valve which will come in handy later in the process but we'll talk about that more in a moment for now our seawater is going to go through a series of two water filters the first one a 20 micron filter which will filter out any particulate bigger than 20 microns and the second filter a 5 micron filter which will filter you guessed it anything bigger than 5 microns at this point the water is a bit cleaner but still salty and gross so now we're going to pump it at very high pressure through our reverse osmosis membrane first it will go through a high pressure pump which will shoot the water into a high pressure hose at 800 PSI for comparison our boost pump pumps out water between 2.5 and 4 PSI so yeah it's a lot of pressure okay so now we've reached the point at which the magic happens our high pressure hose is connected to a high pressure vessel into which our reverse osmosis membrane lives on the other end of the high pressure vessel we have the crossroads this is where fresh water gets separated from all the nasties on one end the fresh water comes out and on the other end the salty discharge called the brine by slowly cranking up the control valve we control the pressure through the membrane until it goes up to between 800 and 1000 PSI our fresh water will first go through a flow meter to give us an indication of how much fresh water is being produced then through a TDS monitor TDS standing for total dissolved solids which gives a measure of the amount of minerals salts metals and other particulate present in the water its TDS shows anything above a thousand parts per million it's considered unacceptable as drinking water according to the World Health Organization and anything under 300 parts per million is considered excellent so that's what we're aiming for so that's it now we've made fresh water from seawater using parts that you can buy at a hardware store but our water maker isn't complete yet in order to preserve the Integrity of the membrane the system needs to be flushed regularly kind of like a toilet it's a bad joke so here's how we do that first we connect a timer that's connected to our water tanks on Polar seal we have our timer set to flush our system for 10 minutes every Sunday at 11AM so now you know well then we have our house water go through a charcoal filter to eliminate potential traces of chemicals such as chlorine which the membrane of the water maker is particularly sensitive to then the water goes through a one-way valve which we mentioned earlier and runs through the system that we just described starting with a 20 and 5 Micron filters the high pressure pump and the membrane purpose of the one-way valve is to protect the house water from potential salt water contamination because well it comes from the same place and that's it that's exactly how a Water Maker Works so after a lot of research I realized a few companies sell water maker kits that are made of parts that you would find in whatever hardware store around for a fraction of the price of an off-the-shelf system like a Spectra or shineker obviously you have to assemble the kit yourself but at the price point given the incredible Vantage that making our own water represents it's worth it for us buying a DIY kit made the math work as we could recoup the cost of having to go to marinas to fill up our water tanks within a season so in December 2019 we ordered a water maker kit from seawater Pro our water maker is a 12 volt 20 gallon or 60 liter per hour kit that contains all the parts one would need to install a fixed water maker on board the kit comes with a low pressure and high pressure pump a reverse osmosis membrane three pre-filters all the piping you need and even an auto flush system initially we installed the version without a control panel and I would go into the garage where our water maker is installed and operate it each time we wanted to make water did not find this to be any inconvenience and again at the price point in considering the tremendous value of making water on board we didn't care the installation itself went pretty smooth but I shall warn you if you intend on installing a water maker on your sailboat no matter if it's an off the shelf or a DIY kit set aside a few days for the install preferably at a dock and prepare for a few showers it's completely normal I remember one point when I was tightening one of the fittings and the system was pressurized which you should never do okay that was a big mistake I made the thing burst in my face and unfortunately the camera wasn't on because that would have made for some great comedy the hardest part of the installation honestly is to figure out where you will install all the different parts but once you've figured it out it's surprisingly straightforward I made a video about the installation you can check it out in the link above myself on the screen a year after our initial installation seawater Pro added a remote control panel to its product offering and we chose to install it in the galley which turned out to be a massive life changer in terms of how easy it would be to operate our water maker so how does it perform well our water maker produces about 20 gallons or 60 to 70 liters per hour depending on the temperature and the salinity of the seawater we typically run it for about one to one and a half hours every other day it draws 60 to 70 amps with our 12 volt system however we try to time Water Production for when the sun is at its peak with our solar array pumping in 30 to 50 amps when the sun is at its peak we normally draw 20 to 30 amps from the battery when the water maker is on of course this depends on the size of the solar setup we have used it in clean ocean water in extremely dirty Lagoon water of Saint Martin with great success however Sophie has asked me to tell you that you really shouldn't run it excessively in Gross water good tip having the capacity to produce our own water on a budget has completely changed our lives as we have gained self-sufficiency enormously it's also increased the Comfort on board by allowing us to take daily showers even during long ocean Crossings with crew and we were even able to install a small washing machine on board the washing machine will be the subject of another three year later review but for now I just wanted to say how life-changing making water on board has been but what we love most about our water maker is how convenient it is to maintain or repair when something goes awry because we do not have to rely on proprietary Parts as would be the case with a off-the-shelf system I can find parts in every single hardware store that we visit even in the most remote places of the world which obviously in the context of Our Lives is a great Advantage I can buy replacement parts and filters anywhere and honestly I haven't had to do a lot of repairs on a rare occasion I had some pipe Spurs but that was 100 my fault so take this as a friendly reminder that you should always open the seacocks and inlets before turning your water maker on the repairs have always been very easy as the pipe is really not hard to find and Mike at seawater pro has always been super helpful when I've had any questions and over the years I've seen him a Justice product to make it better and more dependable which is pretty cool but this wouldn't be a real honest review if I didn't tell you about some of the cons of the system first it takes more power than a Clark pump which you find on a lot of off-the-shelf systems for our part our electrical system can support our water maker ease easily so this really isn't a problem next some people may find the system a bit noisy but no water maker is silent and it does not run permanently just a few hours every few days but in our eyes the pros largely outweigh the cons being the system is generally inexpensive compared to a lot of others and makes making water on board affordable it's easy to get parts everywhere in the world even in remote parts of the world which is great for us easy to install and troubleshoot but above all else it makes great tasty water I have thought a couple of times about changing our system to a 220 volt AC system so that I can have two membranes in double our production but I think I'll keep that for Polar seal number two and yes polar seal number two will definitely be equipped with another seawater Pro water maker as we simply find it to be the best value around so my final grade for the seawater Pro water maker kit is a solid a plus totally recommended and if you're considering getting one do it in the meantime let us know if you'd like us to review more of our equipment on board and if you'd like to support the production of these videos directly consider joining our patreon page until next time [Music]

- A Quick Guide to Basic Generator Maintenance | PartSelect.com: Kind: captions Language: en we all know how unpredictable weather can be and we've all experienced power just during severe storms and how many times have we relied on our generator to get us out of trouble and it wouldn't start hi it's steve from partselect in this video we'd like to share with you some tips on how to get your generator in top working condition so it won't let you down when you need it the most now if you have a larger generator you probably have a battery assist to get it started you'll want to make sure that your battery is fully charged at all times that may involve putting it on a battery charger on a regular basis to verify that your battery is working properly simply try to start your generator if it doesn't turn over easily or won't turn over at all you'll want to look at your battery first thing we'll inspect is the cables to make sure that they're tight not corroded or damaged if they seem to be fine you can use a volt meter and check your battery to ensure that it has the proper output voltage if it doesn't try recharging it if it won't hold a charge you'll need to replace it so it doesn't let you down when you need it the most now that we've checked out the battery start function and made sure that that's okay we're next going to look at our engine oil most modern generators now have an oil level sensor if your oil level is too low your generator won't start so it's important that we check that oil level to begin with if you use your generator on a regular basis you should follow the manufacturer's recommendations on how often you should change your oil and typically that would be at least once a year now that we've taken care of the oil our next step will be to look at the fuel system now with any gasoline it's really critical that you not store fuel in the tank you should always keep fresh premium grade fuel on hand but never store it in the tank we should also check the fuel tank to make sure that it's in good shape as no sediment in the bottom if your model doesn't have an inline fuel filter it probably has a filter that fits into the filler opening so you'll want to remove that and inspect it you can then view the inside of the tank and if there's any sign of corrosion you'll need to have that replaced now that we've checked out our tank and tank filter we'll next want to look at the fuel lines going to the carburetor to ensure that they're in good shape not cracked or corroded or leaking and if you see any indication of leaking or cracks you'll want to replace that fuel line so that it doesn't let you down now that we've verified our fuel lines are in good shape the next thing we'll want to look at is our air filter so we'll just pop this cover off and we'll inspect the filter make sure it's not plugged up now if your filter needs to be clean you simply wash it with soapy water rinse it well and dry it well and typically these foam type filters require that you saturate them with clean engine oil and then wring out any excess and then reinstall them if the filter is damaged you'll want to replace it now that we've inspected the air filter our next step will be to check with the recoil starter so what we'll want to do is inspect that cord make sure it's not frayed or damaged and also check the knot on the end make sure it's secure if you find any damage with that cord you'll need to remove that starter and replace it now that we've checked out the recoil starter the last thing we want to look at on the engine is the spark plug so next we'll locate the spark plug wire pull that off the end of the plug and we'll want to inspect that cap to make sure that there's no signs of any corrosion in there if so you'll need to have that serviced next we'll remove the plug now if there's any signs of excessive wear or corrosion on the end of that plug you'll need to replace it now that we've checked out all the essential components on the engine to make sure that they're in top working order just have a quick look at any of the electrical connections check ones going to a fuel solenoid to your starter any of those things looking for any signs of corrosion if you find something you'll need to either have that repaired or replaced once we've done all that add a little bit of fuel to it and we'll start it up and make sure it runs smoothly we'll plug something into one of the outlets and verify that the generator portion is working fine then we're ready to shut it down drain the field out and put it safely into storage and you'll be ready thank you so much for watching this video we certainly hope that these tips will help you with your generator maintenance for more videos on appliance repair maintenance and cleaning tips be sure to visit our website if you have any suggestions be sure to share them in the comments section below hey don't forget to subscribe you

- A Day in the Life of a Generator Service Technician: Kind: captions Language: en a generator service technician's day is anything but predictable between a morning login to their work schedule to an evening debriefing with a service manager a lot can happen when the job is defined a technician will check out the required Parts the technician's truck is equipped with all the tools required for a planned maintenance or service call service technicians usually have prior authorization to enter the generator facility technicians are trained to work on all generator system controls and equipment and the technician will start by verifying the systems operational status before working on the generator the tech will ensure that it's in the off position and turn off the generator breaker in a planned maintenance visit the first things to check are the oil and coolant levels verify that the block heater is keeping the engine warm using the infrared meter and make sure all hoses are tight and not leaking check the radiator hoses for leaks and tighten when required check the proper operation of battery charger check the battery to ensure the battery voltage is being maintained check the battery connections and tighten and secure any loose connections verify any auto battery charger is fully operational check the engine oil filter is tight and secure and verify their date of change is followed after verifying the breaker is in the off position the technician will then remove the generator panel to gain access to the generator output wiring once inside the technician verifies the correct torque on the electrical connections the voltage regulator above the generator is also checked when the inspection is complete the panel is replaced now the technician moves to the engine to check the radiator fan belt and hoses to ensure everything is tight and free of debris check the air filters to ensure they're clean and reset the system with a reset button also check for any exhaust leaks once the unit inspection is complete the technician will start the unit and verify voltage frequency oil pressure and coolant temperature the technician then Returns the control panel into Auto position and Returns the breaker to the on position a technician's truck today can also be his office before leaving for the next job the work undertaken is logged and recorded via a notebook computer in any given day the technician can move on to several more service calls either scheduled or emergency when returning to the office the technician meets with a service manager to discuss the work undertaken any issues and to plan the next field service trips the rewarding aspect of a service technician's job is that every job is unique your out and about and your high level of training and experience is highly respected by the customers you serve in both industrial or residential applications

  Top Comments:
  1. @terrynicklin417 [3 likes]: 1nHz = a wavelength of 32 years!! Can this be true, and what possible use is that? Sorry if I missed it.
  2. @robertwebb5586 [3 likes]: I'd like to see the Junctek on your live stream, I'm looking at this one since I work on Transistor AM/FM radios now that I'm a retired A/V tech.
  3. @D614G_aka_Doug [2 likes]: I'd bet big money you'd never get a full cycle at 1 nHz out of such an instrument. You're likely to have power outages and the instrument itself isn't likely survive for a full cycle.

Instruments like those probably don't have very good timebases. Even much higher end instruments generally use pretty ordinary crystals. Some have options for "ovenized" crystal oscillators that can be very good but rather expensive. If the instrument has an option for an external timebase input, a "GPS-disciplined" crystal oscillator can be very higher performance at pretty low cost. A really accurate timebase often isn't required but can be useful if you need to trim the frequency of the oscillator of a digital clock (like the real-time clock in a PC) or a frequency counter or other instrument.

If you want a stable scope display of AM you need to set the trigger level for the scope to, ideally, the highest or lowest peak of the signal. If the peak amplitude isn't stable you might need to compromise with something a bit lower than the peak. Triggering at zero crossing will never get you anything stable except by good luck or tweaking "holdoff." In general, a good digital scope should be much better able to cope with weird waveforms than an analog scope but setting up the trigger may be a bit involved.

The so-called "transformer" in a flyback converter will always have an air gap in the magnetic path. That results in some amount magnetic flux slopping around the landscape. Often a "shorting band" of copper, soldered so it is a good "shorted turn" is used, usually about 1/3 of the bobbing width and placed outside the core and winding. The idea is that the mag field that would cause interference is opposed by the field generated by the current it induces in the shorting band. (If the band were just around the winding inside the core window it would look like a very serious shorted turn in the winding.) Wrapping the whole thing in copper foil will cause some degree of interference with normal operation but it can be a tolerable compromise. Flyback converters tend to generate more noise than other popular topologies. Cheap ones often use a core type that has terrible "self shielding." Pot cores are good but introduce some limitations.
  4. @Manticore1960 [2 likes]: Measuring AM waveforms on digital scopes are best done if you have a sync output on the generator. You can connect the sync out to ch2 & set the scope to trigger on that. But only one of your sig gen's had that, so the next best thing is to adjust the scope's trigger hold off until you get a stable trace.
  5. @perkulant4629 [1 likes]: I love chinglish brand names 😂 junc tek haha. It does actually look great though.
  6. @Patrick-rd9qo [1 likes]: Aren't these complete overkill for audio work? You can't hear over 20kHz?! These look like they're designed for RF/ham work to me.
  7. @Nūrd_AF: Was the mains AC leakage to either source or ground shield measured on BNC terminals?

- He turned this abandoned boat into a luxury yacht | full start to finish @FrengenEngineering: Kind: captions Language: en The restoration of a neglected vessel always begins with ruthless elimination. Before any new material can be integrated, years of decay, obsolete wiring, and weathering must be stripped away. Watch how the builder immediately targets the corrupted elements. Tangles of degraded nylon rope and outdated navigational hardware are completely ripped from the fiberglass hull. The captain's chair, secured by rusted fasteners, is unbolted from its pedestal to expose the stained, weathered teak decking beneath it. This initial phase is entirely about establishing a clean baseline. An old Garmin dome antenna is unscrewed and its coaxial cables are pulled through the bulkheads. With the bulky hardware removed, the focus shifts to surface decontamination. A heavy application of alkaline foaming detergent is sprayed across the stern. This thick foam clings to the porous teak and oxidized gelcoat, chemically breaking down embedded salt, algae, and organic buildup. Using a stiff-bristled deck brush, the builder aggressively scrubs the wood grain, lifting decades of embedded grime before power washing the slurry over the transom. The high-pressure water physically ejects the loosened debris, revealing the true condition of the timber and fiberglass below. Once the exterior is neutralized, the boat is loaded onto a heavy-duty roller trailer and transported inside a climate-controlled garage. The transition indoors is critical. Fiberglass repair, epoxy application, and marine painting require strict temperature and humidity controls to cure properly. With the hull completely stabilized in a dry environment, the real structural recovery can commence. The first target is the hull's damaged underbelly, demanding intense mechanical precision and heavy abrasion. Beneath the waterline, the hull is coated in layers of failing antifouling paint. This ablative coating is designed to slowly wear away in salt water to prevent barnacle growth. But over years of neglect, it flakes and blisters, creating severe hydrodynamic drag. The builder initiates the removal process using a high-RPM Makita impact driver fitted with a heavy-duty abrasive wire wheel. The rotational friction obliterates the compromised top layer, generating a cloud of toxic marine dust. Transitioning underneath the trailer, the removal becomes intensely physical. The flexible blade contours directly to the curved geometry of the hull, ensuring the resin completely fills the void without leaving any hidden internal air pockets behind. Once the marine epoxy achieves full chemical cure, it forms a dense, waterproof matrix that is significantly harder than the original fiberglass. The builder utilizes an orbital sander equipped with extremely sharp aluminum oxide paper to level the cured patches. The orbital motion aggressively flattens the convex filler until it sits perfectly flush with the surrounding hull. With the surface structurally restored and flattened, absolute precision is required for the new waterline. A continuous length of high-adhesion marine masking tape is stretched precisely along the chine. Next, the builder prepares a high-build epoxy barrier coat. This specialized gray primer is formulated with interlocking microscopic solids that create an impenetrable moisture shield, preventing water molecules from penetrating the porous fiberglass. The high-viscosity fluid is mechanically agitated with a drill mixer to ensure uniform suspension. Using a high-density foam roller, the primer is methodically applied along the lower strakes. The foam roller ensures a consistent, even film thickness, which is crucial for maintaining optimal hydrodynamic efficiency. Before the primer fully cures, the primary antifouling layer is prepared. A heavy, dark blue self-polishing copolymer paint is poured and mixed. This specialized coating is designed to constantly renew its surface through water friction, preventing marine organisms from anchoring to the hull. The builder applies this dense blue layer directly over the tacky gray primer, ensuring a maximum chemical bond between the two coats. The neglected teak decking requires extreme mechanical resurfacing to eliminate decades of deep cellular oxidation. This extremely dark blue antifouling matrix will constantly abrade in the water, chemically repelling marine growth to maintain peak hydrodynamics. With the external hull fully armored, the focus shifts inward to the deteriorated interior. The boat's teak decking requires extreme mechanical resurfacing to eliminate decades of deep cellular oxidation. Teak is a remarkably dense tropical hardwood, naturally loaded with silica and oils that resist rot, but continuous ultraviolet radiation destroys its surface cellulose, turning it the extremely dull, dead, chalky silver. The builder attacks the flat deck panels with a high-oscillation palm sander. The abrasive pads effortlessly grind away the dead wood fibers, instantly exposing the vibrant, unweathered hardwood locked beneath. However, for a true marine restoration. This high-velocity extraction requires surgical physical control. A single microscopic slip of the rapidly spinning diamond-edged blade would irreparably gouge the expensive timber, permanently destroying the linear aesthetic appearance of the deck. The precise removal of this old caulk is absolutely critical for long-term structural waterproofing. Following the aggressive mechanical routing of the deck seams, a heavy-duty industrial wet vacuum is deployed to extract the dense accumulation of toxic rubber, particulates, and sawdust from the newly milled grooves. Every microscopic particle must be eliminated to guarantee absolute adhesion for the incoming marine sealant. As the synthetic bristle brush glides across the dry planks, the powerful capillary action of the wood immediately drinks the viscous fluid down. The dull sanded surface instantly ignites into a rich, deeply saturated mahogany tone. This highly elastomeric compound cures into a flexible synthetic rubber designed to expand and contract with the wood during extreme temperature fluctuations while maintaining an absolute watertight seal. - Trim properly and a following sea is no problem: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] Catamaran myth number one. Catamarans don't perform well in following seas and suffer from bow steer that makes them swerve. Well, I got to tell you that any boat does that in a following sea if you don't know what you're doing. If you trim a monohal's bow down in a following sea, it's going to swerve because you're trying to stuff that bow in and you get resistance. It's the same thing with a catamaran. If you have your boat trimmed down at the bow and you are in a following sea, yeah, you're going to push those bows in and the wave is going to force the bow one way or the other. However, just like in a monohole, if you trim the bow up in a following sea, you come up and over the wave instead of trying to push down and into it. And that's what causes that swerving steering in a following sea in any boat. And you just have to know what you're doing.

- Stop the Rock! Do power catamarans benefit from a Seakeeper?: Kind: captions Language: en Hi, I'm Shane Grover from Mai Yachts and I'm here today to answer some of the questions we frequently get asked and to debunk some of the myths about power catamarans. One question we surprisingly get asked quite frequently is whether a seaeper can be fitted to a power catamaran. A seaeper is a type of stabilizer using a gyroscope. It's mounted into the hull and powered by electricity provides stability. There's other types of stabilizers which include foils or tabs that work while the boat is underway, but the gyroscope is powered by electricity and can work when the boat's stable or underway. Yes, a seaeper or stabilizer can be fitted to a power catamaran. However, it is entirely redundant. It is not necessary in any form. The power catamaran gets a stability from its inherent hole form. The wide beam and two holes make a large wide platform keeping the center of gravity central and low. Catamaran is far more stable than a monohole because it has two holes and a wide beam. Think of it like a table. A table with two legs is going to be far more stable than one with a central leg. When a monohole goes over a wave, the hole tips from side to side with the wave. When the wave passes, this doesn't just stop. It takes time for the energy to dissipate. With a catamaran, the wave will still pass under the boat, tilting the hole somewhat, but it will not have the exaggerated impact after the wave passes. This keeps the catamaran so much more stable and comfortable and is one of the primary reasons credited when people transition from monoholes to catamarans. You can only really appreciate this when you try catamaran in real life. If you haven't been on out in the water on a power catamaran, give us a call, send us an email. Let's get in touch and bring you out on the water at the next opportunity. I hope you found this video interesting and I'll be seeing you out in the water very soon.

- How a Seakeeper Gyro Stabilizer works to reduce side-to-side Roll on a Boat or Yacht......: Kind: captions Language: en hey guys it's Chris with one Water Yacht group today we are talking about sea keeper gyroscopic stabilizers now this big white box that I'm sitting on here in the engine room is a sea keeper gyroscopic stabilizer and the physics of gyroscopic stabilization are pretty complex but the implementation of those physics and how they work is actually pretty straightforward so here's how a gyroscopic stabilizer Works number one we spin up a very heavy frisbee shaped disc inside of this box and we spin that disc up to about 10 000 rotations per minute and when you start your boat it often takes 20 to 40 minutes depending on the size of your gyro to get that disc spinning to the full 10 000 RPM speed now once the disc is spinning we can either Engage The Gyro or disengage the gyro the disk stays spinning at all times regardless and when we Engage The Gyro all we are doing is we are allowing this gyroscope to freely move forward and aft as the boat rolls side to side so a lot of people myself included assumed that well if we're using a gyroscope to counteract the motion the side to side motion of a boat we are going the opposite way so if the boat's going this way or moving our gyroscope that way it doesn't work that way and we don't actually move the gyroscope at all the gyroscope moves itself so when the boat goes that way the gyroscope freely moves forward because that gyroscopic precession has a perpendicular torque effect not to get too detailed that counterbalances the motion this way and when the boat moves this way the gyroscope freely on its own wrote slot swivels back towards the stern of the boat so the gyroscope moves on its own perpendicular to the motion it's counteracting now the physics are complicated but the implementation is pretty straightforward we spin a gyroscope 10 000 RPM we either lock it in place so it's not engaged so it cannot move back and forth or by engaging the system we allow the gyroscope to freely move back and forth and that free movement back and forth the ability of the gyroscope to move on its own naturally according to the laws of physics very very dramatically counteracts that side to side motion of the boat so physics are complicated the implementation is straightforward a large spinning disc ten thousand rotations per minute if we want to counteract rotation we allow it freedom to swivel back and forth in response to the side to side motion of our bone and it works incredibly well

  Top Comments:
  1. @SailingSunday [8 likes]: UPDATE: 
I deleted the puppy pump and it's basically now just:
Sea strainer after the seatcock - food grade hose - 20 & 5 Micron filter - food grade hose - Highpressure pump - highpressure hose - PV1 #1 - highpressure hose - PV #2. 

- Brine output = high pressure hose - dial - needle valve - hose - cockpit seacock.
- Freshwater output = food grade tubing - TDS meter - food grade tubing. and I manually put in the tanks or fill bottles. 

Notes* The HP catpump has a pressure relief valve to protect it and I don't flush the watermaker after every use because we live on the boat 24/7, I use it for about 1 hr or every 30 minutes every 2 days and seems to be ok. The last membrane never went higher than 200ppm after 3 years. I only replaced the membrane because I upgraded it.
