- The Truth About Boat Insurance: 12 Mistakes That Kill Your Claims: Kind: captions Language: en If you just bought a boat, this is the video you watch before you ever call an insurance company. Because here's the nightmare sequence most people never see coming. You finally get a policy. You spend a year fixing leaks, replacing rigging, upgrading electronics. Then something happens. A storm, a collision, a sinking at the dock, and the insurer calmly says, "Claim denied. Breach of warranty. Policy void." In this video, I'm going to walk you through the biggest mistakes new boat owners make with insurance that get claims denied or policies canled, even when they think they're fully covered. And if you stick to the end, I'll give you a simple fiveline email template you can send to any broker or insurer that forces them to spell out the traps and exclusions before you sign. It's basically a pre-screen that flushes out nightmare policies before you pay a scent. If you want more honest, no BS deep dives on buying, insuring, and actually using boats, hit subscribe right now so you don't miss the next one. All right, let's start with the single biggest mistake people make with their new boat and insurance. Mistake number one, lying on the application, even a little. A lot of people think of insurance forms as suggestions. How much experience do you have? Well, I sailed a bit as a kid. Any prior claims? Not really. I mean, nothing big. That is a great way to turn your policy into decorative paper. Insurance is a contract built on good faith. If you misrepresent material facts, things that would have changed their decision to ensure you or how much they charged, they can walk away when you need the most. Common places people fudge the truth. You round your experience up. You say you've done offshore passages when your longest trip was 20 m. You list yourself as primary operator, but in reality, your inexperienced partner is at the helm most of the time. You skipped mentioning that little grounding last year because it wasn't a big deal. Individually, these feel harmless, but in a claim review, an adjuster is literally paid to go line by line through the original application and see if the story matches reality. If there's a serious loss, someone injured, major damage, salvage involved, they will dig. They will pull Coast Guard reports, marina records, prior insure history, survey notes. If something doesn't match, they now have an excuse to deny. So, rule one is brutal and simple. Tell the truth, even if it makes you look less impressive. I've never owned a boat this big before. I have zero offshore experience. I grounded last year in the ICW and needed to tow. You're better off paying a bit more or even being declined by one company than having a cheap policy that evaporates when something goes wrong. Mistake number two, trying to ensure a boat before you really know what you bought. Most people do this backwards. They find a deal on a boat. They rush to close to beat another buyer. Then after the money is gone, they start calling around for insurance and are shocked by what underwriters come back with. For older boats, especially anything over about 20 25 years, insurers want three things. A recent outofwater survey from a surveyor they consider competent. proof that major risk items have been dealt with. Old fuel lines, ancient wiring, rotten decks, standing rigging, a clear picture of how and where you'll use the boat. If the survey turns up a long list of major issues, underwriters will do one of three things. They'll decline outright. They'll agree to insure you only after you fix specific items, warranties, or recommendations. or they'll insure you but exclude certain things from coverage like rigging failures or osmosis. If you already own the boat, you're trapped. You can't go back and undo the purchase. What you want instead is this sequence offer on the boat. Survey the boat. Send the actual survey and photos to a broker and ask, "Can this be insured? At what value? And what will you require me to fix?" Only then decide if the deal still makes sense. Do not assume that because the seller had insurance, you will automatically be able to get it. Carriers change appetite. Regions change. Old policies are sometimes grandfathered in under rules that no longer exist. On older fiberglass sailboats, red flags for insurers include old standing rigging, soft decks or wet core, corroded through hulls and seoxs, gas engines with sketchy fuel systems, ferro cement or homebuilt hulls. If you don't know how those play with insurers, you're walking blind into a deal that might be uninsurable. Mistake number three, confusing agreed value, stated value, and actual cash value. On paper, it looks simple. You tell them what the boat is worth. They insure it. Done. In reality, the valuation language determines whether you get a proper payout or a low ball check if the boat is damaged or totaled. There are three main flavors. One, actual cash value. This is the car insurance model. They pay what the boat is worth at the time of the loss minus depreciation. If prices in your area have crashed or if they decide your boat has more wear than you think, you get hammered. Two, agreed value. You and the insurer agree on a number upfront. If the boat is a total loss, that's what they pay, usually without haggling about market depreciation. This is what most serious cruisers aim for. Premiums can be higher, but it removes nasty surprises when something big happens. Three, stated value. This is the sneaky one. You state a value, they calculate the premium off it, but in a claim, they reserve the right to pay either the stated amount or actual cash value, whichever is lower. You feel like you're insured for 100K, but they might decide it's really worth 65K when push comes to shove. If you want to avoid this particular nightmare, you do two things. First, ask explicitly, "Is this policy actual cash value, agreed value, or stated value?" Second, on older boats, document why your agreed value makes sense. Recent survey, big refits, engine replacement, new rigging, electronics. If you overstate value wildly just to feel better, you're wasting premium, and you make it easier for them to argue later that you were unrealistic from day one. Mistake number four, not reading or respecting your navigation limits and layup clauses. Every policy on a cruising boat has answers to three questions. Where can you legally take the boat? When can you use it? And what do you have to do when a named storm is coming? People skim past these and then they're stunned at claim time. Navigation limits might say inland waters and coastal waters within 50 nautical miles of the US east coast between Maine and Florida. If you quietly wander into the Bahamas or cross to Bermuda without getting that added in riding, you may be cruising totally uninsured. Layup or out ofuse clauses might say boat must be hauled or not used between November 1 and April 1 in your region. If you decide to do a cheeky Christmas sale and wrap the boat on a rock, the insurer can point to that clause and say, "Nope, you were outside allowed operating season." Named storm plans matter even more now. Some policies literally spell out what you must do when a hurricane is forecast. Boat hauled by x hours before landfall or moved north of a certain latitude or tied into an approved hurricane mooring field. If you ignore that, leave the boat in a cheap exposed slip because it's too much hassle and it ends up in a mangrove pile, they're going to ask for your storm plan and evidence that you followed it. No plan, no evidence, no payout. So, with any new policy, read these three things like your wallet depends on it because it does. Your navigation limits, your layup or usage dates, your named storm or severe weather obligations. If something in there doesn't match how you actually plan to use the boat, fix it up front or walk away. Mistake number five, treating a live aboard boat like a weekend boat on paper. This is a big one, especially now that more people are living aboard to escape rent. Most standard pleasure use policies assume the boat is used for day sailing, weekends, a couple of trips a year, and otherwise sitting mostly empty. As soon as you're living aboard full-time, especially in a marina, your risk profile changes. There is more wear on systems, more cooking, more electrical load, more chance of fire, more people coming and going. If you tell the insurer, "We just day sale and maybe do some weekends, but in reality, you're full-time aboard with a space heater, air fryer, dehumidifier, and three laptops on 247." You're giving them an easy out on any claim. Likewise, some policies explicitly exclude unapproved live abboard use, long-term rentals or Airbnb style use, carrying passengers for hire, or doing paid charters. I see people constantly buy a cheap excharter boat, slap a charter logo on social media, and never bother to upgrade to a commercial or charter policy. They are one accident away from discovering that their normal policy doesn't apply to business use at all. The bottom line, if you live aboard, say so. If you ever take money for taking people out, say so. If you intend to use the boat for charter, say so. You will pay more. You might need a different type of policy, but you will not be hiding a business inside a pleasure policy and hoping no one notices. Mistake number six, ignoring survey recommendations once you have the policy. This one is sneaky because it feels like you're covered. You get a survey. It has a long list of findings and recommendations. The insurer says, "We'll bind coverage, but we require items A, B, and C to be fixed within 30 or 60 days." You sign, you sail. Life gets busy. Months later, an accident happens that has nothing to do with those items. Maybe you have a galley fire or a collision at the dock. The adjuster comes out, looks at the file, and sees that the required work was never documented as completed. They can now argue that you were in breach of warranty. From their perspective, you agreed to certain conditions to get coverage. You did not satisfy them. They are no longer bound by their part of the deal. It does not matter if the fire started on the stove and the recommendation was about old rigging. To avoid this, you treat insurer required survey items like the most urgent jobs on the boat. Fix them by the deadline. Take clear before and after photos. Keep invoices and receipts. Send a polite email to your broker or underwriter. Per survey dated X and your requirement dated Y. The following items have been completed. Attached are photos and invoices. Please confirm these are now considered compliant. Then you save that reply. Now, if there's ever a dispute, the file shows you met the conditions they set. That simple paper trail has saved a lot of people from very ugly claim fights. Mistake number seven, not documenting the boat's condition and contents the day you insure it. When there's a partial loss, theft, fire in one cabin, collision damage to one side, disputes are common over what was on board and what condition it was in. Did you really have a brand new $6,000 dingan outboard on the Davitz? Was that electronic suite really new or 10 years old? Were those sails really replaced last year or are they the originals from the8s? You can make these arguments much easier by doing one simple boring thing when you buy and insure the boat. You walk around with your phone and film everything. Hull, exterior, deck, rig, cockpit, cabins, engine compartment, electrical panel, sales if easily accessible, tender and outport. Talk to the camera. This is our 1984 Islander 36 insured today for X as agreed value. Here's the Rayarine plotter installed in 2022. Here's the new main sale from 2021. Photos of the tag. Here's the high field dinghy and 15 horsepower Yamaha purchased in this month and year. back that up to the cloud. If anything happens later, you are not arguing for memory. You can say, "Here is video from the date the policy began," showing the boat's condition and gear. Adjusters are human. If you show them organized, dated evidence, the conversation changes from prove it to, "Okay, how do we value this?" Now, mistake number eight, letting just anyone drive the boat. Many policies have language about who is allowed to operate the vessel. Sometimes it's broad, any competent operator with the owner's permission. Other times it's tighter, named operators only, or people with specific licenses. If you let an inexperienced friend, teenager, or drunk neighbor take the helm and they hit something, you have handed the insurer a great excuse. Even if there's no explicit named operator clause, they will ask, "Who is at the helm? What experience do they have? Were they under the influence? Were they doing something obviously reckless?" If the answer is my buddy who has never driven a boat before, it is not going to help you. This is especially important with high-powered boats and catamarans. A 40ft cat or a 40 knot center console can do huge damage in a tight marina. So, your rule, even with friends, is simple. If I would not be comfortable putting this person alone at the helm and walking below for 10 minutes, they don't get to drive. You can let people feel the wheel or tiller while you're right there handon controls, but the operator of record is you. Also, do not mix alcohol and helm. Insurance aside, it is just a bad equation. Expensive fiberglass plus crowds plus docks and pilings. Mistake number nine, confusing wear and tear with insurable loss. Insurance is not a maintenance plan. They do not pay because your 30-year-old engine finally dies of old age. They do not pay because your rigging rusted quietly for 25 years and a shroud lets go in 10 knots of wind. They do not pay because osmosis blisters slowly appeared over decades. Those are all examples of wear, corrosion, and age and deferred maintenance. These fall on you. Most policies even have explicit exclusions for gradual deterioration, rust, rot, corrosion, electrolysis, design or manufacturing defects, lack of reasonable care. Where they step in is when there's a sudden accidental fortuitous event. You hit a submerged log and bend the shaft. A fire starts from a specific incident. A storm tears the boat off a properly secured moing. You want to avoid the nightmare where they point to an exclusion and say, "This was inevitable, not accidental." How? By maintaining and documenting. Keep basic records. Oil changes, impeller changes, major service, standing rigging replacement dates, throughhole replacements, bottom jobs with notes on blister repairs. If there's ever a borderline case, you want to show that you were a conscientious owner, not someone trying to shift a decade of neglect onto the insurer. Mistake number 11, buying the wrong kind of policy for what the boat actually is. There are important differences between a small runabout you trailer and store at home, a coastal cruiser in a marina, a bluewater boat leaving the country, a charter or commercial vessel. People constantly use a cheap liabilityonly policy and think they have hole coverage. Use a basic local policy and then head offshore far beyond the stated limits. Use a pleasure policy and then quietly chart of the boat. For a new to you cruising boat, especially if you're planning big trips or live aboard life, you want to be very clear. Do I have hole coverage or just liability? Is salvage covered and how is wreck removal and pollution fines covered? Are crew or paying passengers covered? You don't want to discover after a marina fire that yes, they'll pay for the other boats you damaged, but nothing for your own wreck or the cost to remove it. Similarly, if you ever plan to cross borders or go offshore, you need a policy that explicitly allows it. Some require specific approvals for ocean crossings or time in certain regions. It is worth paying for a marine specialist broker who does nothing but boats. They know which carriers are still insuring older holes. They know what underwriters hate. They can tell you this cheap policy looks good on paper but has a hurricane deductible that is insane. Mistake number 11, letting your policy quietly lapse. This one sounds obvious, but it happens all the time. Mail from the insurer goes to an old address. You change emails and don't update the contact. Your credit card on automatic payment expires. You assume you're covered. You are not. There are two problems. One, if something happens during the gap, there is simply no coverage. Two, for future underwriters, a lapse looks bad. They ask, "Why did your last policy end? Non-renewal, cancellation, non-payment." You never want canceled for non-payment anywhere near your file. It hints at financial stress and poor organization. So, when you buy a boat, you set a calendar reminder one month before renewal to review coverage. Confirm your billing details and contact info are current. If you plan to switch insurers, make sure the new policy starts before the old one ends so there is no gap. Mistake number 12, not reporting incidents promptly because you don't want to get them involved. A lot of boat owners are reluctant to call their insurer or broker when something happens. They bump a rock at low speed, feel a vibration, hope it's fine. They get caught in a storm, think they got away with it, and ignore a few new noises. They have a small galley fire, put it out themselves, don't mention it. Months later, the cutless bearing is destroyed, the shaft is bent, the engine mounts are shifted, or the wiring damage from that small fire causes a bigger failure. Now, when you finally file a claim, it looks like long-term neglect rather than a specific recent event. You do not need to file a full claim for every little thing, but you should at least notify your broker or insurer when something substantial happens. We hit bottom at around this speed here on this date. We've scheduled a haul out to check for damage. We had a small fire in this compartment. We've replaced X and Y. Here are photos. That way, if deeper damage shows up later, there's a clear dated event to anchor it to. All right, let's talk about what you actually do with all of this. Here is the promised part. A simple fiveline email you can send to any marine broker or insurer that will drastically reduce your odds of an insurance nightmare with your new boat. You send this before you bind coverage while you still have options. It goes like this. Line one, describe the boat and your plans honestly. I'm buying a 1984 36- ft fiberglass sailboat with a diesel engine. I plan to live aboard year round in a marina and do coastal cruising with the possibility of going to the Bahamas in the next 2 years. Line two, ask clearly about valuation. Can you confirm whether your quote will be actual cash value, agreed value, or stated value, and how total loss and partial loss would be settled on a boat of this age? Line three, ask about survey conditions and warranties. What survey requirements and post survey recommendations would be considered conditions of coverage and what deadlines would I have to complete them? Line four, ask about usage and limits. Will live aboard use, occasional paid skipper trips, or offshore passages to the Bahamas or Caribbean be allowed under this policy? And what navigation limits and storm plans would apply? Line five, ask about exclusions that bite. Are there any exclusions or deductibles I should be especially aware of for named storms, older rigging, osmosis, or mechanical breakdown on a vessel of this age? You send that once. You see how they respond. A good marine broker will answer in plain language, maybe over the phone, followed by an email summary. They'll tell you, "We can only offer actual cash value." Or, "We'll want rigging changed within a year." Or, "We won't cover you below this latitude in hurricane season." A bad one will dodge, send boilerplate, or refuse to be specific. That is your signal. Your goal with insurance is not to find the absolute lowest premium. It's to find a policy where you understand the value basis, understand your obligations, understand your limits, and have a broker or underwriter who will talk to you like a human before money changes hands. If you do that, you dramatically reduce your odds of the classic nightmare, standing on a sinking dock, phone in hand, listening to someone say, "Sorry, that's not covered." If this helped you think differently about insurance on your new boat, hit subscribe so you don't miss the next deep dive. I'll keep trading hype for honest checklists every

- Sailing to the Bahamas: Kind: captions Language: en (upbeat music) - We're Sheryl and Paul Shard and have been sailing and living aboard for 30 years now. Last time on Distant Shores we left Rudee Inlet in Virginia and sailed around Cape Hatteras and Cape Lookout, dealt with a fuel system problem that shut down our engine for the whole passage, and arrived at Beaufort in a gale with gusts near 40 knots. This time we're continuing further south sailing offshore from North Carolina towards Florida and the Bahamas. - [Sheryl] Hey, what's happening? - Very exciting, we heading out from Beaufort heading south, it's a pretty nice day, just a few days before the middle of November, which is our hurricane deadline. We can't be south of 30.5 North before the 15th of November, so we're heading down south near to that. We'll do probably one or two days overnight. Got a nice day for this, a bit of motoring today, I think, nice change after that windy day yesterday. (light music) Forecast winds for this leg are light and variable. Purple is calm, blue is five, and 10 knots is green on this weather map. We'll likely be motoring from 10 to 30 miles off the coast of the Carolinas and Georgia before making landfall in Florida. Total 350 miles. - We left Beaufort, North Carolina this morning at 9:30 and we're heading to Cape Fear, that's our waypoint for this evening. And then carrying on right down to Jacksonville, Florida. So we'll be at sea for a couple of nights, we've got a beautiful night with a nice, big moon, so we'll have light through the evening, and it's very calm, and we're very happy that the motors working again, and looking forward to good night watches. - This is a nice, pretty night. Full moon tonight, so the biggest tides. We're also looking at the currents around here. We're using Predictwind to download satellite data. This is a current model showing where the Gulf Stream is. We're heading down the offshore on the East Coast of the U.S., and further down in Florida, the area where you can go gets much narrower as there's not a lot of room between the coast and where the current runs. But a little further up north and all the way to the Carolinas, the current tends to run further offshore. It's still moves around a lot so we like to see the up-to-date data, and you might even be able to catch a lift from back eddies in the current. - [Sheryl] It takes us two days to get to Florida, and then we're in the mouth of the Saint John's River passing the Mayport Naval Station, the third largest naval base in the U.S.A. accommodating large ships right up to aircraft carriers. - There's a lot of current in the river, and now it's ebbing, more than three knots of current slowing us down to just four knots over the ground, although we're motoring at seven and a half. The river is being dredged as part of a project to allow deeper ships to get to the container port. It used to allow 40 foot draft. The new goal is to allow ships that are 47 feet deep to get up the river. Wow. (wind blows) The Jacksonville Bridge has to open up for us, this is Main Street. I always feel bad stopping traffic. - [Sheryl] The bridge has a 40-foot vertical clearance when closed, but can be increased to 135 feet when the span is raised to its highest position. The bridge tender lifts it just enough for our 71 foot mast to clear and so road traffic is not held up longer than necessary. - So what's next is a fixed bridge, and it's right beside a railway bridge, so we can do both at the same time. And the fixed bridge span is 75 feet and we're 71 foot six at high tide, and it's currently high tide. So that means we have a clearance of about that much on top of our mast as we go through here. Always looks like you're gonna hit it. To prepare for the Bahamas we've snagged a slip at the lovely Ortega Landing Marina in Jacksonville and get down to work on boat projects. I ask the Marina Operations Manager if I can borrow a tool to make the mount for my new fuel filter. You here, Bill? Look at this place. This is a cute little shop. The Maintenance Manager here, I came and asked him for a bit of advice and we were talking about some parts and stuff. I think he's gone home, but look at this. So there's some Florida hospitality for you. We've only been here a day and a half, I've been really impressed how super friendly everybody is and we've had so much help. So this project will get finished. Just gonna, this bit of wood, mounting a new Racor filter on top of it. These filters are really great. Using Bill's tools I've shaped this wooden mount and drilled and installed mounting bolts behind, then bonded it in using 3M 5200. That's not going anywhere. Our new filter is the Racor 500. We've used this on all previous boats as our primary fuel filter, removing any water sediment and particles in the fuel down to 10 microns. The bowl collects debris and water, and the vacuum gauge detects when the filter is getting clogged. That should stop any dirt from getting into the engine, but I decide to open up the tank and use the inspection port to check inside. In the U.S.A., where we bought this fuel, the Environmental Protection Agency mandates the use of red dye to identify diesel fuel meant for off-road use such as yacht fuel. Inside it looks quite clear with no sign of dirt in the fuel, except perhaps a few distinct bits lying on the bottom, possibly construction debris. At our next opportunity I will rig something up and try to vacuum the bits from under the fuel. - [Sheryl] Thank you very much. - No problem, you have a good day. - [Sheryl] Yes, thank you. - [Paul] We're off to sea, huh. - Yes, we're about to sail to the Bahamas. We had three grocery carts full of goods, not just for this one week, but for most of the fall too. - Mostly wine from the look of it. (laughs) Underway from Florida. - Ho-ho, its so warm here. - Yeah, sunny Florida. - Off to the Bahamas. - Strong north winds have set up a current in our favor as we head down the coast before the Gulf Stream reestablishes itself as we pass cape Canaveral. Then we will head directly across the stream to minimize the time in the current sweeping us north. It'll be a two-night passage. Well here we go, welcome to the Bahamas, we're just getting up to West End on Freeport. We haven't been in here for 30 years, yikes. What's the news, Sher? - So I just called Old Bahama Bay Marina, they are open for clearance, Customs and Immigration, and they have a slip for us for the night. Standby. - All of our islands are so beautiful, and even with the devastation of primarily mostly the Marsh Harbor area and a few of the neighboring areas, there are still some choices in the Abacos that you can still visit. However, we're the alternative for that because there are still so many beautiful islands, including West End here on Grand Bahama. - [Paul] But weren't they hurt by the storm too? - No, we were able to salvage basically everything that we did experience in a very short time and be ready to open for November first. As a matter of fact, this very week, the 22nd, we're happy to be hosting a Wahoo tournament that will bring us 47 boats and about 170 people. - [Paul] Oh great. - So we're really looking forward to getting back on the, what do they call it, the swing again, and just reliving everything that we've done in the past. - [Paul] We love the Bahamas and we're glad to hear you guys are doing well here. - Well, you mentioned earlier that you were here during the days of Jack Tar, which takes us back quite a bit, and to have you still returning to us, it sends a definite message of something being done right. So just know, we continue to be the ambassadors for our beautiful islands here and it's a pleasure hosting you today. - [Sheryl] Join us next time as we explore the remote Berry Islands then cross the Yellow Banks on the way to the Exumas. - Why you don't NEED a Watermaker to live on the hook! | ⛵ Sailing Britaly ⛵: Kind: captions Language: en Hi guys, in this sailing tips video we're going to talk about how you can live on a small boat without a water maker we don't have a water maker on this boat and we only have 120 liters water storage onboard this is our water maker a 20 liter jerrycan. It's till got the price on it it was four euros 40 cents! The reason we're making this video is because we read recently about somebody who's preparing a boat for a long voyage where they wants to live aboard at anchor for long periods and they were saving up money to buy a water maker which they were struggling to afford but they thought that it was necessary to buy one in order to avoid staying in marinas well that's just not true there are two different ways of doing it the first way is what we did when we went on our honeymoon to Corsica we stayed on the boat for 23 days we were anchored every single night and at the time we weren't using this jerrycan method but what we did was we conserved our water so we used very little water - brushing our teeth with a tiny amount for example - and half way through the holiday we went into a port we didn't stay overnight but we just paid to get the water you can also find free water and lots of petrol stations so if you go to get fuel for your boat often you can get free water anyway the second method in our opinion is the best and that is just to take a jerrycan with you every time you go ashore then you can find a free water fountain pretty much everywhere you refill your tank and put it in your tender when you go back to your boat you transfer it to your water tank and doing this we've just spent a couple of weeks at anchor and when we got back the tank was still full because every couple of days we were filling the tank and put it into our main water tank so it's very simple you don't have the expense or the maintenance of all the hassle of owning a watermaker so it's a valid way of doing it and this could make the difference for someone who's buying a boat or preparing themselves to go on a big journey this extra expense could be the thing that's stopping them from being able to go and do it so you don't need a watermaker necessarily in our opinion on a boat of this size a water maker isn't practical because not only do you have to find the space to fit the watermaker which would take up some room we could find the room for that on board but the problem is the power requirements for a watermaker are huge so on top of the water maker you then have to have an external generator for a very very large amount of extra power generation just to run your water maker so from that sense in our minds it's not really practical for a smaller boat also they're really expensive not just to buy but also to maintain the membranes are very expensive and they take a lot of maintenance a lot of the time you're going to your boat and you'll be spending time fixing the water maker rather than enjoying your boat so they do have advantages I'm not saying that nobody should have a water maker it depends on your circumstances but what we're saying is you don't need a water maker you can make it work without one if you have a bigger boat then you've got room for the water maker and it's spares and you've also got room for the extra power generation required to run the wate maker so the calculation may be different on a larger boat but in our minds on a small boat around this size 30 foot a water makers not really practical and you don't need one because this is a water maker do you agree yes or no comment below and let us know what you think before you leave check out the rest of our videos we've got lots of different kind of videos coming boat recipes boat maintenance sailing blogs sailing tips all kinds of different stuff if you like what you see then please subscribe to our channel so you don't miss our future videos thanks for watching we'll see you soon ciao

  Top Comments:
  1. @jaimeparra27 [2 likes]: Mister, it is easy to talk, I want to see you sailing in the caribbean without a watermaker
  2. @The_Gryph [1 likes]: I wonder if this opinion has changed since the video was first published. 

In europe this is fairly reasonable. There's good access to clean water in most places you go. At anchor in other places around the world, this isn't as accurate and I would recommend a watermaker solely on the basis of it's filtration ability. 

Rain capture sounds like a fix but you'd still want to treat it. Clouds travel and the precipitation that falls may have formed from elsewhere, bringing with it microbes and chemicals. In the main it's not something to be overly concerned about but some form of water treatment for any captured water is a good idea. 

Regarding the numerous comments on sail harvesting, again I would filter and treat it, no matter how clean anyone thinks thier sail is. 

Parts-wise a lot has changed in 5 years, so the replacement parts are comperable in price to any other servicable components onboard. Membranes you tend to change around 6 months or so, depening on usage (law of common sense applies), filters have thier own expiry and a lot of the prefab watermakers have PPM sensing as a standard these days.

Is a watermaker required? No. Is it recommended? Yes. 

It's like any other system on the boat. It's an extra layer of safety for an environment we're not fully designed for. Whether or not people plan to buy or make on is really down to how much value they place on safety and what thier approach is to acceptable levels of risk. 

There are plenty of ways around not having one, especially in europe. Further afield, I wouldn't want to be caught out and I'd say it's a necessity for global travel.

So guys water storage in gallons is a good thing but don't forget when you are drifting in open water, gallons are empty and haven't been raining for at least a month the buddy that will keep you alive is a manual water maker or portable solar water maker. kicking I will I paid $2,400 for her and got to work getting her back into sailing condition I sailed her every chance I got from weekly Day sales to weekends away at Catalina Island to two we trips at the northern Channel Island I slowly learned what the boat needed and didn't need and would spend a month or two knocking out big projects then get back to sailing her I did the refit over 4 years working paycheck to paycheck investing close to $30,000 to transform the boat into a proper blue water cruiser that was ready to sail the world on August 12th 20 21 that time had come my slip tras lines from a guest slip at marina Del re California and pointed the bow West Bound for the Hawaiian Islands halfway to Hawaii I lost the ability to steer after striking a submerged object that eventually led to Total Rudder failure I found myself a drift in the middle of the Pacific Ocean 1,000 nautical miles from the nearest land after 3 days of attempting to dive on the rudder I eventually rigged up a drug that allowed me to steer the boat and get back to sailing completing the 2300 nautical mile passage in 32 days I spent the next 8 months exploring the Hawaiian Islands from aahu to mikai laai Maui and the Big Island all the while awaiting the correct sailing season to sail South when the time finally arrived I hauled up the anchor and sailed towards the Southern Cross ocean passage presented me with new challenges in regards to both ship handling and repairs things that se break they rattle loose and they wash open with the NeverEnding abuse of the ocean I made the outer reefs in the loud group I found my engine would not start with light winds I adjusted my cells throughout the night to make sure I got safely past the dangers and into the prote Waters of the Coro sea I didn't know it at the time but the large following Seas had forced sea water slowly up my exhaust pipe and back into my engine on passage a possibility I was well aware of and tried to islands one morning my engine troubles finally got the best of my starter I found myself possibly stranded away from all services in this remote island chain after taking the starter apart and putting it back together I finally managed to get the engine to run and made my way to fiji's largest island vti levu where I spent 3 weeks trying to sort out the engine issues I managed to get it running leaving the reliable trade WIS behind I'd become spoiled with the incredible sailing conditions that are known as the coconut milk run now heading due south sailing into the variables I found more calms than ever and when there was wind it was strong and mean it seemed like the winds would either blow five knots or 40 knots and rarely much in between without a working engine I couldn't motor through the comms as most Cruisers do so I would sit and wait as each new storm system approached after a 15-day passage of 1100 nautical miles I arrived to New Zealand in a building Gale I managed to sail within 1 mile of the quarantine dock but found it impossible to short tag the last distance against increasing winds and an outgoing current I dropped anchor and began getting the dingy ready to attach to the hip when a Good Samaritan arrived and offered me a toe I tied up to the dock as the storm gained strength and felt a great sense of relief after getting my engine rebuilt I spent the be the easiest ocean passage I had ever done I managed to sell on the edge of some very nasty storm fronts and use the top of high pressure systems to make my way west arriving to Sydney Harbor in the dark after 13 days at Sea a passage of 1200 nautical miles as I crossed into Sydney heads my heart raised so we just crossed into Sydney Harbor into the entrance and I have officially crossed the Pacific ocean solo and it feels fantastic covering more than 11,000 nautical miles over 2 and 1/2 years time on a 59-year-old 30t sailboat alone I hope my story helps you realize that seemingly Uh, these are fairly large alternators. They weigh in at about 95 lbs a piece. They are huge. Um, and they're capable of putting out a continuous 300 amps. Um, and you know, over time, all of that load pulling on the generator, uh, the the the diesel motor could possibly have some problems with bearings. So, what I decided to do was run two alternators because I wanted not only to split the load on the bearing, I wanted to split the load on the alternator. Uh, I didn't want to have one alternator running peak output because that induces heat, uh, just more wear on the alternator altogether. But if I took two of them and I only demanded half amperage on them, then that would work for me. Not only that, the the amperage that these could put out was less in a single alternator than what I wanted ultimately. Uh so two alternators just made sense. So it worked out really well that with two alternators, I could configure them. So they each had their own set of belts, but they were tensioned with each other. So I had one tensioner that went across the top of the two alternators that tension them together. And what that meant was that my side loading on my generator bearings were next to nothing because they were each pulling in an opposite direction at the same load. Now, at this point, I was pretty happy with this configuration. Uh things were working well, making lots of amperage, uh charging the batteries fast, minimal generator run time, and then disaster happened. Um, one of the pivot bolts on the inboard alternator sheared, and I'm talking a half inch thick bolt that's pivoting in two separate points. So, it's not double shear, per se, but it did have two shear points on it, just on two different mounts. Um, so yeah, a lot of force there. And I think just with 95 lbs of alternator vibrating around on there, eventually it just snapped. Uh, so what I did, uh, rather than reinventing everything is first of all, I had to get a new fan because what happened when that bolt sheared, the fan dropped down and uh, hit the actual ear that the pivot bolt went through. And let me tell you, that was a pretty horrendous noise. It was like a screech, vibration, terrible, like I could hear it from the back of the boat and I came running to shut the generator down. Um, so the fan was destroyed. Uh, it had spun around and had taken off every single one of the fan blades on there. Um, useless, completely useless alternator at that point. So, what I did was, um, I decided that first of all, I needed to get a new fan. So, called up my buddies at Brian Roberts Auto Electric. great guys uh in Victoria. They rebuilt alternators. Uh really good work. They're dealers for Dixie Electric who made the alternator. It's a Dellstar 300 alternator. Um and they gave me a call back and they said, "Hey, um we tried to call Dixie Electric and they told us that that part was for internal use only and not available for sale to the public." And I thought to myself, "What the shit?" like how can you have an alternator that you cannot sell parts for to people in the aftermarket? And I wasn't even, you know, it wasn't me calling in and asking for this. It was a a distributor and uh account holder for Dixie Electric. So, I found that to be a little bit crappy. Uh, fortunately, when I had first started looking into these alternators, I did contact a gentleman in Vancouver uh, who was one of the local distributors for Dixie Electric. And, um, so I did have his contact number. So, I messaged him and I said, "Hey, I'm working on a bit of a prototype here. I had a failure on it. I lost a fan. They're telling me that this fan's not available. What can we do?" And he says, "No problem. I'll figure something out." So he talked to his supervisor, whatnot, got back in touch with Brian Roberts and they said, "Hey, you know what? Look, we will just send you out a fan. No charge. We'll call it a sample to sort you out." And of course, I'm, you know, I was not expecting that. I expected to pay for a fan. And I was through the moon that they were just sending a fan out for free. And uh quite honestly at that point my opinion of the service and you know assistance of uh Dixie Electric and Dellstar super happy with them. Um I mean because not many people would do that. So got this fan in. They sent it out perfect. Um got it all assembled. And what I figured I'd do is I believe that initial bolt that I had in there was a grade five bolt. So, next thing I had to try because I didn't want to have to redo everything was shifting up to a grade eight bolt. And um got it all back together again. Things were going gang busters and even in less time than the first time had another failure of a pivot bolt. Same exact thing again with a great eightbolt this time. Um, I was less than happy and uh it was obvious that gonna have to do a little bit of changes on this to make it work the way I want it to. Another fan. destruction again. >> Oh yeah, >> even I'd say I'd almost consider it worse than mine. Why? >> Uh because the whole brake mount came So >> I'm stuck. >> You can see my pivot plate here. And then that is a half inch grade eight bolt times two sheared. So that's what held the pivot plate on to the alternator and that snapped right in half. So obviously the engineering is not sound. We're going to have to reinvent this and uh do it differently cuz this is obviously not working. >> [clears throat] >> I did realize in hindsight that if I had had the fan guards on these that might not have happened. So what I decided after that second failure was obviously this was not a viable option. I was going to have to do things differently. And what I had also noticed when I was replacing the pivot bolts was that the the two cradle bolts that were both half-inch bolts into the side of the alternator, um, when I went to take them out to replace the cradle bolt, um, or sorry, the pivot bolt, I realized that they were starting to break, too. One actually broke as I was trying to unscrew it with very little tension on the wrench. So, it was obviously cracked through as well, ready to go. So, at that point, I knew we were going to have to do things differently. this was not going to work. So, what I decided on was I made a complete cradle. That was my my first starters. I mounted that alternator in a cradle with all four of the mounting bolts being contributing to holding the alternator in place. So, now I've quad quadrupled the ability of this mount to hold the weight. Then what I did is fortunately when I built my main mount um I built it for some little delrin sleeves like vibration damper slider sleeves in there uh to clamp down on the alternator and that left me room to put a bigger bolt in. So what I did was I went with a thinner steel sleeve and I took out the 1/2-in bolts and I put in 3/4 in grade eight bolts. These are monstrous pivot bolts. All right, so time has come for me to revisit the mounting on the alternators for the generator. And uh this is what I'm working on here today. So I'm actually converting these back to um the actual uh saddle style or cradle style. So I've got my two mounting plates. I'll weld that guy in the middle. Uh and then I'll build up uh plates here and here and then do mounting strips on either side. So, basically, this cradle will bolt down to the plate on the generator and uh rather than having any sort of tensioner off of the alternator, all the tensioning will be done by stretch belts. So, that should work out better because I think what's happening right now is with the vibrations, there's just too much load on two bolts to hold the the full weight of the alternator. Uh cuz these aren't the ones I have on the generator, but they're close and they weigh about 90 lb. So, having a three-cylinder generator vibrating around with 90 pounds of weight just on two half inch bolts, I think is too much. So, with a with the cradle assembly, uh, I'll be using all four bolts and the weight of the alternator will actually be sitting in the cradle. So, the bolts won't necessarily be holding the weight of the alternator. The cradle will do that. So, hopefully that'll fix the problem and I won't have any more broken bolts and fragged fans. So, we'll see how that goes. All right. And I am working on generator stuff today, trying to get this mess sorted out. So, I'll show you guys what I had. So, basically where this where this bolt's going in, um I had this plate here and that plate bolted with those two bolt holes to the side of the alternator. So, that was my pivot. And then I had another plate bolted up on the top row of bolt holes that are rotated around now, so you can't see them. And then I had a pivot arm bolted up there. So basically all of the the weight loading was on the bottom two bolts on this plate. So what I've done is I've now got a full cradle on that pivot. Now I've got to this one I've got to finish up. Actually I've got to finish the other one up too. This one I've just got tacked in place on that on that cradle. So that will sit uh actually another thing I'll point out too. This was the original bolt and that is the new bolt. So this is a half inch one. The new one is a 3/4 inch pivot bolt. Uh so much much heavier duty. Uh all grade eight, so about as strong as I can really get on that. Uh without specialty bolts. Um so yeah, now I've got a cradle. All four bolts on the alternator are uh attached to that cradle. So, there's no more full load on just two of the four bolts. So, I'm hoping that will make a good difference there and eliminate my bolt braking problem. But, we shall find out. So, basically, I'm going to use roughly the same idea here as I had before. Uh, I've got an adjuster. Mind you, I am going to upgrade the size of this guy. Uh, he's going to go on the top of that one, and that's going to span across to the other alternator. So, they'll tension against each other. That means my crankshaft loading is going to be the same because they're roughly about directly opposite of each other on the generator itself. Um, so basically, they all tension against each other. So it they're the load on the actual bearings uh will be neutral because there's not going to be any direct belt loading on it cuz they're tensioning against the other alternator. Um might be an ever so slight amount, but you'll get a better idea once I get them in place and show you what I've got going on there. Okay, shifting to the other alternator bracket. Slightly different design, but same basic idea. Uh, so yeah, just going to last weld that I've got to do is just on that arm. Uh, and then I can mount up the other alternator and I can start figuring out how I'm going to do the tensioning. Might just temporarily put something on there just to get some tension happening for now cuz it's going to probably be rainy over the next day or so. It'd be nice to have the generator operational. So, I'm just going to play around a little bit and see how it goes. >> Heat. Heat. So, finally I've got these alternators mounted in such a way that I don't have to worry about those portions breaking anymore. Uh the 3/4in bolts have proven to be sufficient. Um shifting to a cradle mount and altering the angle of the plates so the cradles are more just holding the alternators from falling rather than having a heavy shear load on the bolts has really helped as well. Uh when they start getting up to really high RPMs, the efficiency drops a lot. And being I'm not having a lot of heating issues with these, I can spin them slower and they still maintain temperature. And mill a head on the opposite end, maybe  just a 9/16" thread, 5/8" long. with a 1/4 shoulder run a 9/16 thread nut on it  , what 7/8"  15/16" nut size? Weld the nut to the  e thread.  And grind it smooth, making a long bolt roughly 12" long. Make a spacer to fit between  mounts , so a single bolt will clamp evenly, then heat treat the bolt . Because the heat affected the area of the weld. It's plenty strong as is, with the vibration and weight the heat treat , normalize, and temper to reduce any hard spots around the weld area. Allowing more plastic deformation before things get serious  and bad starts being possible..  I would  also get a chunk 1" her to make a couple of oversized nuts for the bolts . 🔩  maybe  make the ones that get welded on? To allow a 1" wrench to fit both ends. And possibly  have a under cut flange , so there is nothing to dig into the surface  when tightening the nut down.  It's just a smooth round area on both sides about 1/16" - 1/32" to keep a smooth area for a washer or lock washer yo perform its best 👌.   With the nut  being 1/4" over size, make it about 5/8" - 3/4" tall, also maybe .850" or .875" or .900" -.925" tall with the undercut flanges.  Possibly  heat treat the nuts along with the bolts , the  threads could possibly  be toughened up a bit  to mm watch  the cold rolled  tube spacer   and make extra thick washers while making nuts.   About 3/16" thick and made round from the 1" her,  grind flat on both  sides with a surface grinder.  Leaving 3/16"-4/16"  (aka1/4")😂   the bolt being the most fancy part. , may as well  cold blue them, or black oxide. Best yet, NICKEL PLATE THEM! PREVENT RUST,  you can do it at home , just toss in. A couple "NICKELS" 😂 COPPER PLATE THEM FIRST, GET A FAIRLY THICK COPPER PLATE. .00006" and build up about the same nickel , keep the nickel under .00015" and over .000075" so cut the threads .000081" and .00016"   extra  clearance, tap the nuts first  then single point the bolts.  And make the bolts fit the nuts lose then plate to fit. Mays well engrave the date and any other info, a laser engraved would be cool, add names dates , maybe pictures on the head of the bolt. Even plate the washers. If you are going to do cool, be good a doing cool 😎 , a .0001" - .0005"  thick nickel plate on washers is good. Especially  if boiled in bees wax.  Heated to about 220°f after a acid etch.  About 20 minutes at 220°   or use teak or tunge oil heat the polished  , acid etched  plated bolts and such cleaned with 91-99% alcohol ,  then heat to 250°f drop into tung oil teakoil or bees wax, the wax /oil already hot, 220°-250°f 10-30minutes , to drive off any moisture  then remove and cool.  After cool allow to dry and hand polish  with  cotton  cloth.  It should shed water  preventing rust. If nothing else is available.  Heat engine oil to 300° soak alcohol cleane parts for 10-30 min.  Remove to cool , polish clean with a rag. water should bead up. 🎉Enough rambling,  have an Awesome 👌  day🎉

  14. @BWhitten: If I may offer a suggestion:
Those numbers on the data plate are not set in stone. You could use the phrase "wishful thinking". The numbers used to determine that are  subject to maximum conditions. The fan rating is typically insufficient at anything other than maximum RPM. The people who make them will sell more parts when it isn't cool enough. You could find a way to increase the pitch on the fan blades and /or vent the input to outside air - carefully. Engine room temp is not your friend.

- SAILBOAT TECH: How to transform your ENGINE into a GENERATOR! // Ryan's Tech Corner #10: Kind: captions Language: en hi guys so during our trip across the mighty atlantic and throughout the first few months here in the caribbean i came to the conclusion that the charging systems on polar co were drastically undersized we already had a ton of solar no room for a generator wind is too noisy and a hydro generator just costs too much this leaves us with our last solution the alternator in this video i'm going to talk about what an alternator is how they work challenges with alternators on boats and how we will transform our basic engine and alternator to a monster power generator just want to mention that this video is not sponsored and we purchased all of the products we discussed in the video ourselves so what's an alternator an alternator is a device that attaches to your engine you will find these on boat engines car engines and even small airplane engines their first job is to recharge the starter battery so that you can start the motor again the next time their second job is to supply power to devices running like lights and they then charge the battery to support house loads like hair dryers and chart plotters most alternators found on boats coming off the production floor are the same ones found on car engines the alternator that we have on polar seal is a common 80 amp hitachi alternator how does an alternator work typically an alternator is operated by the engine and is connected through a series of belts on polar seal's yanmar engine the alternator is activated when the engine is turned on and the belt starts spinning the belt is attached to a pulley which is on the outside of the casing this pulley then attaches to a rod which is inside of the alternator which is called a rotor inside the alternator the rotor turns with some magnets on it and it spins as the engine runs the rotor spins inside the housing called a stator the stator is essentially a big ball of wires wrapped around an iron ring having the rotor spin inside the stator is what produces electricity through the principles of electromagnetism however the electricity that it's producing is ac or alternating current like is found inside the wiring of your house the problem with this is most electrical systems on boats cars planes operate in dc if you're not sure what ac and dc means you can check out this video on the basics of electrics which we produced so to change ac to dc the alternator has a piece of equipment called a rectifier this is a series of diodes that converts ac to dc and once the current has been converted to dc it passes through a very basic charge regulator and then to the batteries the regulator controls the basic output voltage of the alternator simple right it's true car and truck alternators are actually very simple they were designed to be used with lead acid batteries provide a charge to the battery the one that was being used in starting the engine and then provide small power output to meet the needs of the vehicle well the problem on boats is that we were asking the alternators to do a lot more than what they were designed to do today's boats are asking for more and more power from our electrical systems a boat might have 300 amp hours of power it needs to have replaced in its batteries plus the loads which are currently on the dc system with an alternator operating at full speed at efficiency which you will never ever come close to it would take nearly five hours with polar seals alternator to charge the a good example is when we crossed the atlantic ourselves our two ways of charging were through the solar and through alternator if it had been a really cloudy day or cloudy for multiple days we need to run the alternator and the engine for multiple hours some days up to eight hours just to get our batteries up to eighty percent that's a lot of engine running time just for your batteries so why the lack of efficiency anytime we create energy we produce heat and alternator is no different the alternator produces about 50 percent of its heat in the stator the coils that are wrapped around the rotor and about fifty percent of its heat in the rectifier most alternators are attached directly to the engine block which because it's producing energy is also producing heat typically in a very confined space as the alternator and the engine compartment heat up the efficiency of the alternator is going to decrease so how hot does it have to get well some cheap alternators can produce so much heat that you can literally fry an egg on top as the temperature increases the energy output starts to decrease so the longer your engine runs the hotter it gets the less alternator output you're going to have on a boat we're asking the alternator to provide a lot of power for a long period of time which also increases the heat another issue impacting efficiency is the rotation that happens inside the alternator so the alternator spins faster it's going to produce more power most alternators are designed for a maximum output a specific rpm which is well above the rpm that our diesel engines can run at to get around this we use a larger and smaller pulleys you see here the flywheel on polar seal is very large compared to the alternator wheel so every one rotation that the engine makes the alternator might make two or three rotation while this helps getting the alternator closer to its maximum rpm it is still nowhere near the maximum output on top of all of this we then have inefficiencies with the belt which is turning the alternator if the belt is too loose it will slip and not provide good energy transfer from the engine if it's too tight it would create too much friction and thus more heat this is why it's so important to check your belt tension during your engine checks your engine manual will describe the maximum tolerance for your belt but to check you simply push down on the belt and see how much it moves so how does all this inefficiency translate to power output well on polar seal we have an 80 amp hitachi and there are times when i only see 20 or 30 amps output you can plan your alternator output generally to be between 50 and 60 efficiency range but it will decrease as the alternator ages to expand our alternator problems we now add lithium with normal lead acid batteries because the resistance is so much higher the batteries can only accept a certain charge load as the batteries become topped up the resistance increase and the alternator dials back the output it will continually do this throughout the charge cycle lithium does not work this way the resistance is low throughout the entire charge cycle lithium can handle a massive amount of current with charging these basic alternators don't understand this so if you connect an alternator to a lithium battery it will just keep pumping out power and power and heating itself until boom smoke done bye bye there's a really good video from victron energy which shows what can happen when you connect a lithium battery to a standard alternator i wanted to show you myself but sophie said no so remember to send your complaints to instagram ryan and sophie's so great ryan you're being grumpy again and just telling us all the problems we have with alternators what is the solution well my youtube friends there are some very cool solutions on the market and i'm about to tell you about them so what have we decided to do on polar seal i looked at most options available i wanted as high output as i could safely manage as lithium has its safety limitations i wanted good efficiency and i also wanted a charge controller that could handle lithium batteries a few that i looked on the market were bomber alternators and regulators american power alternators and regulators mark gracer alternators and wake speed alternators and regulators in the end we decided to go with a 180 amp alternator from mark racer what's unique with mark's design is that he has removed the rectifier away from the alternator so if you remember from the start of the video i said that the rectifier is what turns ac power to dc power it's also what generates nearly 50 percent of the heat so moving this part away from the alternator into its own cooling box will allow for better efficiency of the system super cool we also decided to include a wake speed 500 regulator with our system the wake speed regulator is one of the most unique and advanced on the market as of 2020. it can sense both current and voltage of the entire charge system and allows me to set the limits for it so for example the maximum charge current i want going into our entire lithium system is 150 amps if i am running the alternator on a day where our solar system is providing a lot of current my alternator might be pushing out let's say 140 amps while my solar is producing 50 amps the total of this is well above the 150 amp limit the wake speed will recognize this and dial back the power of the alternator to meet this limit it also has a lithium charge setting which is programmable and is important with any lithium setup there are a lot of other alternator chargers out there with lithium settings balmer being one of the most popular however many of these will only sense voltage and not current so that's one of the drawbacks another cool feature of the wakespeed 500 is that if you're on a catamaran you can connect the two charge controllers without the need for a center fielder a topic for another time maybe when we get a cat lastly we decided to upgrade our pulley and belt system to a serpentine system this will allow for less slippage and more efficient energy transfer and hopefully last less black dust all over the engine compartment changing the belt was not really a choice but a necessity as the current belt system was just not powerful enough to meet the needs of our new alternator we ordered the entire system through ocean planet energy who we are not affiliated with nor do we do any product exchange with but they did offer us great support check out mark's kit and ocean energy's website using the link below first sizing when looking for a new alternative the general rule of thumb is to purchase one with an output which is between 20 and 30 percent of your battery bank size a special note here for lithium battery owners you also need to ensure the batteries can accept the entire load of your alternator on polar seal our maximum charge current is 200 amps for our lithium batteries which i don't like to do so i keep things below 150 amps our 180 amp alternator factoring in all of its inefficiencies would be perfect for this job next is the physical size of the alternator most alternators are packed in small engine compartments and include very small alternators we call these small case alternators you can find small case alternators which will produce energy upwards of 200 amps and that should be good enough for most of us the problem with small case is that they're harder for them to dissipate heat and have a bit lower efficiency if you would like to have more output and a bit higher overall efficiency you will need to start looking at large case attachment points are another thing you need to consider there are a few different types of attachment points based on the make and model of your engine on the mr we use a saddle mount which looks like this so before you order an alternator check with your dealer or distributor to ensure that you get the correct type of mounting another important thing to remember is that when you put additional loads on your engine you will take away some horsepower that would normally be there to power the propeller a rule of thumb not requiring a lot of math is to use one horsepower for every 25 amps of alternator output on polar steel we have calculated this to be about six horsepower for our new alternator that translates to a lot of horsepower when we already feel a bit underpowered while motoring so this is something to consider the last consideration is the belt and pulley system on your engine most marine belts and pulleys are barely big enough for standard alternators that come with the engine so the odds are you will need to make modifications to this by either adding a second belt or a serpentine belt if you have a common engine like yanmar volvo or beta there are lots of kits available on the market to help with this pro number one lots of extra power for the induction stove number two higher efficiency from operation so you need to run your engine a lot less to produce more power number three if changing your charge controller you can handle more advanced batteries and charging setups like lithium now for the cons the first one complexity the systems are a bit more complex to stall than your basic alternator con number two cost we spent nearly 3 500 on our setup which what i will admit is a little eye watering what but during our atlantic crossing we put 90 hours on the motor over half of this was for charging only there was one day we were on the crossing where we ran the engine for nine hours nine hours just to charge all i could think about other than noise was how much fuel we were wasting and how much wear and tear we were putting on the motor this all adds up so when trying to decide if the cost of a high output alternator it was worth it i looked inside nigel calder's book the boat owner's mechanical and electrical guide the bible for boat owners so that's it guys that's how you turn your engine into a super duper generator using a high output alternator we know that the pace of these videos has been a little slow lately and that's because we're back and we're working on a bunch of different projects but we do have another video where we talk about how we installed this alternator and we also have some other tech videos coming so please bear with us they are coming we're gonna have them they're gonna be great but that's it for now thanks for watching bye-bye i can't believe we spent 3 500 bucks on this

  5. @bobrose7900: Just to add after watching this again, heat management of the alternator is paramount, so low engine speed low output which sounds obvious but alternators can go to full output at quite low rpm and their inbuilt fans will not cope and thermal runaway will occur, most external controllers can be programmed to output at different rates for different rpms.  A second alternator setup is much preferred, engine battery(lead acid) for standard alternator and second for lithium house bank.  I have a sense switch that will take the house alternator out of circuit for starting and manoeuvring.  The other item to consider is to get your house bank on a 24v system, reduced current and cable sizes but may require a dc-dc inverter for some equipment
  6. @Nicholas2011ist: Throw a shaft generator on... Using a permanent magnet dc motor as a generator through an mppt regulator on a boat your size could easily offset 99% of your electrical loads under sail

  18. @mboyer68: How about a propane generator? A new 1000 watt propane generator will run 60 hours on a 20lb tank. They're light and portable and very quiet, and some are made to be able to connect together. You can run a typical sailboat AC unit with two 1000 watt units together. 1000 watt propane generators sell for ~$530 on Amazon. I'm thinking of going this route and trying to figure out if it's a mistake? I decided to look into this because my friend has had a small 4 stroke gas Yamaha generator for probably 20 years. It always starts, runs perfect, it's very quiet, small and light. It's the size of a small cooler. Plus, no part of the exterior gets hot, the exhaust is hidden, and I've seen it get knocked over, it immediately shuts off. It also has low oil and other emergency shutoff systems, and it's old! 
So...if you were running two 1000 watt units on your crossing, you would've gone through probably four 20lb tanks (assuming the manufacturers rating of 20lbs/60 hours is with no load) in 93 hours i think you said. Include tank changes, where to put them, where to put the generators, ya, long passages would be tough with this setup. Plus, given the global inconsistency with propane tank fittings you might have to carry either different tanks or adapters, which I'm not sure are legal or safe. Ya, I'm talking myself right out of this...unless I'm not going far!
  19. @apollo8352: Please do not run your main diesel engine just to charge your batteries.

Hi I have years of experience as an electrician and working with diesel engines and big industrial diesel generators, as well as onboard my own boat.  I enjoy your videos very much.  But was horrified you ran your main engine crossing the Atlantic for nothing more than battery charging.

Unfortunately diesel engines have a problem, if you run them to long only lightly loaded they glaze up their bores and piston rings and injectors.... this shows up with reduced performance, excess smoking, and eventually higher and higher oil consumption.  It should be all diesel engine owners goal to make their diesel work fairly hard when running to prevent the glazing...

If the glazing in the engine is only slight you may find a good hard run makes the engine run better than ever, but this will only happen in a boat if the propelor is big enough to make the engine work hard.  

If left unchecked cylinder glazing, and piston ring glazing will eventually cause enough lube oil consumption the engine goes into a run away situation which has two possible outcomes, either the engine speeds up uncontrollably and blows up mechanically or you physically get close enough to the speeding engine to block off its air intake with some solid object that will not get sucked into the engine, (certainly not your hand). 

Of course engine run away and excess oil consumption can be fixed by having the engine dismantled and deglazed, but that is a major engine rebuild.  Some fuel additives claim to deglaze your engine, but I'm not aware of any produce used by professional diesel engine owners, which says lots. For the sake of induction cooking I think the true cost for you, is massively more than just the fuel used! 

While your alternator upgrade is great, the two things I would say is reduce your amps well away from the alternator maximum rating, and if possible add a wind turbine and more solar to give you more power.... also install an alternator cut out switch on the field input,  to cease battery charging if your engine is slightly under powered...so can turn off battery charging when you need the extra horse power in those situations..

As a side note I'm currently running an under powered RV, (land yatch), and it has 1100watts of solar, 110 amps of engine alternator, current to charge the 300AH lithium house batteries is limited by the resistance in the cable length which gives maximum house battery charging between 35 and 50 amps, well below the alternator rating, I can adjust the house battery charging to occur as a dynamic load on down hill runs or if I'm desperate I can charge batteries on flat runs, but I never charge house batteries with the engine while going up hill...  .  The starter battery is a standard connection to the engine alternator and takes care of the over volts from alternator output switching...that is caused by the delayed effect of the alternator voltage regulator needing to reduce the alternator internal magnetic field which is slow reaction, before the alternator output reduces, hence volts go up....  

The voltage spike of an alternator needs to be addressed some how with lithium batteries otherwise when the battery management switches of the charge current the delay in alternator response will cause a high voltage spike that can damage everything else including the battery management circuitry.  I have seem a couple of examples now of good lithium batteries having their battery management circuits destroyed in what could have been a fire on board, if things had turned out worse....     

Happy sailing
  20. @basilroland: Ryan's eyes are upside down.

- How to drive a boat in rough water | Big sea throttle techniques explained | Motor Boat & Yachting: Kind: captions Language: en john mendes here with the latest in our how to's this one's all about the back of the next wave that's the most civilized way to travel upwind that's probably the hardest that's where we're going up the face of every single wave over the crest and down the other side
